In the case of large deviations from stoichiometry, simple associates or more extended defect clusters can be formed. One example is the Koch-Cohen defect cluster in nonstoichiometric wüstite ( FeO ). This defect cluster bears a strong resemblance to the structure of $\mathrm{Fe}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$. One can think of nonstoichiometric FeO as fragments of $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ intergrown in the rock salt structure of FeO. ${ }^{22}$ Another well-known cluster in the oxide-interstitial defect cluster is
anion-excess fluorite-structured $\mathrm{UO}_{2+\mathrm{x}}$. This cluster, comprising an $\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime \prime}$ and two relaxed oxide ions, thus leading to $2 \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{O}, \mathrm{r}}^{\cdot \cdot}$ and $2 \mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}, \mathrm{r}}^{\prime \prime}$, in addition to the regular interstitial, has been used to explain extended defect clustering in fluorite-type anion-excess solid solutions in the systems $\mathrm{MeF}_{2}-\mathrm{ReF}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{MeF}_{2}-\mathrm{UF}_{4}$. Here, Me stands for an alkaline earth metal and Re for a rare earth metal.

The defect chemistry of nonstoichiometric transition metal oxides has been studied extensively, and many of these studies ${ }^{62,63}$ have been reviewed. $15,19,54,64-68$

Schematic Kröger-Vink diagrams of metal oxides have been reported, ${ }^{15,68}$ and these resemble the defect diagram in Figure 5.2 as expected.

## B. GENERALIZED APPROACH TO THE DEFECT CHEMISTRY OF TERNARY COMPOUNDS

For the simple binary compounds discussed in Sections II.A. 1 and II.A.2, at given P and T, usually the partial pressure of the electronegative element is used. In ternary compounds a second component activity has to be determined, leading to a three-dimensional representation for the relationships between defect concentrations and component activities.

In ternary compounds of the type $(\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}) \mathrm{X}$, the point defect concentrations and hence the deviation from stoichiometry depend on the concentrations of the two cations. Since in general the equilibrium constants of the different point defect equilibria in ternary compounds are composition dependent, their thermodynamic treatment is more difficult, and usually much more experimental data are required than in the case of the binary compounds. The complexity of the ternary compounds is the reason why point defect chemistry has been worked out more or less quantitatively only for some oxide and fluoride systems, using the concepts of Kröger and Vink, ${ }^{8,10}$ Kröger, ${ }^{10}$ Schmalzried and Wagner, ${ }^{25}$ and Schmalzried. ${ }^{26}$

Schmalzried ${ }^{26}$ has pointed out that the theories of the defect chemistry of spinels can be generalized and be applied to all ternary compounds. Groenink ${ }^{34}$ and Groenink and Binsma ${ }^{69}$ made this generalization for ternary compounds comprising two cations and one anion, i.e., $A_{a} B_{b} X_{c}$ with the cations $A^{\alpha+}$ and $B^{\beta+}$, and the anion $X^{\gamma}$. The compound is formed from the binary compounds $A_{p} X_{q}$ and $B_{r} X_{s}$. They choose $\beta \geq \alpha$. In this section the generalization reported by Groenink and Binsma ${ }^{69}$ will be summarized.

For the ternary compound the following independent variables are chosen, i.e., total pressure P , temperature T , partial pressure $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{X}_{2}}$, and the activity $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{Ap}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{x}}$ of the binary compound $A_{p} X_{q}$, hereafter referred to as $a_{0}$. The following defects can be present in $A_{a} B_{b} X_{c}$ :

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
A_{i}^{(\alpha-j)^{*}}, & \mathrm{j}=0,1, \ldots \ldots \alpha \\
B_{i}^{(\beta-k)^{*}}, & \mathrm{k}=0,1, \ldots \ldots \beta \\
X_{i}^{(\gamma-1)^{\prime}}, & \mathrm{l}=0,1, \ldots \ldots \gamma \\
V_{A}^{\left(\alpha-j^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}}, & \mathrm{j}^{\prime}=0,1, \ldots \ldots \alpha \\
V_{B}^{\left(\beta-k^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}}, & \mathrm{k}^{\prime}=0,1, \ldots \ldots \beta \\
V_{x}^{\left(\gamma-l^{\prime}\right)}, & \mathrm{l}^{\prime}=0,1, \ldots \ldots \gamma
\end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
A_{B}^{(\beta-\alpha-m)^{\prime}}, & \mathrm{m}=0,1, \ldots \ldots \beta-\alpha \\
B_{A}^{(\beta-\alpha-n)^{\prime}}, & \mathrm{n}=0,1, \ldots \ldots \beta-\alpha \\
e^{\prime} & \\
h^{\cdot} &
\end{array}
$$

Here, ionization of the defects is assumed to occur. The total electroneutrality condition is, taking into account the possible defects,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& {\left[e^{\prime}\right]+\sum_{j^{\prime}=0}^{\alpha-1}\left(\alpha-j^{\prime}\right) V_{A}^{\left(\alpha-j^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}}+\sum_{k^{\prime}=0}^{\beta-1}\left(\beta-k^{\prime}\right)\left[V_{B}^{\left.\beta-k^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}}\right]+\sum_{i=0}^{\gamma-1}(\gamma-1)\left[X_{i}^{(\gamma-1)^{\prime}}\right]} \\
& \quad+\sum_{m=0}^{\beta-\alpha-1}(\beta-\alpha-m) A_{B}^{(\beta-\alpha-m)^{\prime}}=\left[h^{\cdot}\right]+\sum_{j=0}^{\alpha-1}(\alpha-j)\left[A_{i}^{(\alpha-j)^{\cdot}}\right] \\
& \quad+\sum_{k=0}^{\beta-1}(\beta-k)\left[B_{i}^{(\beta-k)^{\cdot}}\right]+\sum_{l^{\prime}=0}^{\gamma-1}\left(\gamma-1^{\prime}\right)\left[V_{X}^{\left(\gamma-l^{\prime}\right)^{\cdot}}\right] \\
& \quad+\sum_{n=0}^{\beta-\alpha-1}(\beta-\alpha-n)\left[B_{A}^{(\beta-\alpha-n)} \cdot\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Usually, for a given combination of thermodynamic variables, the concentrations of two defects of opposite sign are much higher than the concentrations of all other defects. Using this approximation, Equation (5.55) reduces to a form for this majority defect pair, i.e., $\left[\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\alpha}\right]= \left[A_{i}^{\alpha}\right]$ for the majority defect pair $V_{A}^{\alpha}, A_{i}^{\alpha}$.

Table 5.2 presents a number of defect equilibria and equilibria with the gas phase.

## 1. Variation of Defect Concentrations with a ${ }_{0}$ and $P_{x_{2}}$

If the variation of the concentration of a defect j as a result of a change in $\mathrm{a}_{0}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{X}_{2}}$ occurs between equilibrium states in a reversible way, one can write at fixed P and T ,

$$
d[j]=\frac{\partial[j]}{\partial_{P_{X_{2}}}}{ }_{P, T, a_{0}} d P_{X_{2}}+\frac{\partial[j]}{\partial a_{0}}{ }_{P, T, p_{X_{2}}} d a_{0}
$$

It is more convenient, however, to consider the change of the logarithm of the defect concentration, because this is a simple function of $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, a, b$, and $p$, whereas $d[j]$ still contains unknown defect concentrations. For the calculation of

$$
\frac{\partial \log [\mathrm{j}]}{\partial \log \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{X}_{2}}} \quad \text { and } \quad \frac{\partial \log [\mathrm{j}]}{\partial \log \mathrm{a}_{0}}
$$

four equilibria are required, viz., Equations (5.64) to (5.67).

TABLE 5.2
Some Internal Equilibria and Equilibria with the Gas Phase

$$
\begin{array}{ll}
A_{A}^{X}+V_{i}^{X} \rightleftarrows A_{i}^{\alpha}+V_{A}^{\alpha^{\prime}} & K_{A F}=\left[A_{i}^{\alpha^{\cdot}}\right]\left(V_{A}^{\alpha^{\prime}}\right) \\
X_{X}^{X}+V_{i}^{X} \rightleftarrows X_{i}^{\gamma^{\gamma}}+V_{X}^{\gamma^{\cdot}} & K_{a F}=\left[X_{i}^{\gamma^{\gamma}}\right]\left[V_{x}^{\gamma^{\cdot}}\right] \\
B_{A}^{(\beta-\alpha)^{\cdot}}+V_{i}^{X} \rightleftarrows B_{i}^{\beta^{\cdot}}+V_{A}^{\alpha^{\prime}} & K_{4}=\frac{\left[B_{i}^{\beta^{\cdot}}\right]\left[V_{A}^{\alpha^{\prime}}\right]}{\left[B_{A}^{(\beta-\alpha)^{\cdot}}\right]} \\
O \rightleftarrows a V_{A}^{\alpha^{\prime}}+b V_{B}^{\beta^{\prime}}+c V_{X}^{\gamma^{\cdot}} & K_{S}=\left[V_{A}^{\alpha^{\prime}}\right] a\left[V_{B}^{\beta^{\prime}}\right]^{b}\left[V_{X}^{\gamma^{\cdot}}\right]^{c} \\
O \rightleftarrows e^{\prime}+h^{\cdot} & K_{i}=\left[e^{\prime}\right]\left[h^{\cdot}\right] \\
X_{i}^{\gamma}+h^{\cdot} \rightleftarrows X_{i}^{(\gamma-l)^{\prime}} & K_{5}=\left[X_{i}^{\gamma^{\prime}}\right]\left[h^{\cdot}\right]^{1} \\
A_{p} X_{q}(g)+p V_{A}^{\alpha^{\prime}}+q V_{i}^{X} \rightleftarrows p A_{A}^{X}+q X_{i}^{\gamma} & K_{6}=\frac{\left[A_{A}^{X}\right]^{p}\left[X_{i}^{\gamma^{\prime}}\right]^{q}}{a_{A_{p} X_{q}} \cdot\left[V_{A}^{\alpha^{\prime}}\right]^{p}} \\
2 A_{p} X_{q}(g)+2 p V_{A}^{\alpha^{\prime}} \rightleftarrows 2 p A_{A}^{X}+2 p \alpha e^{\prime}+q X_{2}(g) & K_{7}=\frac{\left[e^{\prime}\right]^{2 p \alpha} \cdot P_{X_{2}}^{q}}{a_{A_{p} X_{q}}^{2} \cdot\left[V_{A}^{\alpha^{\prime}}\right]^{2 p}} \\
A_{p} X_{q}(g)+p V_{A}^{\alpha^{\prime}}+q V_{X}^{\gamma^{\cdot}} \rightleftarrows p A_{A}^{X}+q X_{X}^{X} & K_{8}=a_{A_{p} X_{q}} \cdot\left[V_{A}^{\alpha^{\prime}}\right]^{p}\left[V_{X}^{\gamma^{\cdot}}\right]^{q} \\
B_{r} X_{s}(g)+r V_{B}^{\beta^{\prime}}+s V_{X}^{\gamma^{\cdot}} \rightleftarrows r B_{B}^{X}+s X_{X}^{X} & K_{9}=a_{B_{r} X_{s}}\left[V_{B}^{\beta^{\prime}}\right]^{r}\left[V_{X}^{\gamma^{\cdot}}\right]^{s} \\
r a A_{p} X_{q}(g)+p b B_{r} X_{x}(g) \rightleftarrows p r A_{a} B_{b} X_{c} & K_{10}=a_{A_{p} X_{q}}^{r a} \cdot a_{B_{r} X_{s}}^{p b} \\
2 X_{X}^{X} \rightleftarrows 2 V_{X}^{\gamma^{\cdot}}+2 \gamma^{\prime}+X_{2}(g) & K_{11}=\left[V_{X}^{\gamma^{\cdot}}\right]^{2}\left[e^{\prime}\right]^{2 \gamma} P_{X_{2}}
\end{array}
$$

Defining

$$
\begin{aligned}
& {\frac{\partial \log \left[\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\alpha \prime}\right]}{\partial \log \mathrm{a}_{0}}}=\xi_{1}, \\
& \frac{\partial \log \left[\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\beta}\right]}{\partial \log \mathrm{a}_{0}}{ }_{\mathrm{P}, \mathrm{~T}, \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{X}_{2}}}=\xi_{2},
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
{\frac{\partial \log \left[\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{X}}^{\mathrm{V}}\right]}{\partial \log \mathrm{a}_{0}}}=\xi_{3},
$$

and

$$
{\frac{\partial \log \left[\mathrm{e}^{\prime}\right]}{\partial \log \mathrm{a}_{0}}}=\xi_{4},
$$

and differentiating the logarithm of Equations (5.64) to (5.67) with respect to $\log \mathrm{a}_{0}$, one obtains

$$
\begin{aligned}
p \xi_{1}+q \xi_{3} & =-1 \\
r \xi_{2}+s \xi_{3} & =\frac{a r}{b p} \\
\xi_{3}+\sqrt{\xi_{4}} & =0
\end{aligned}
$$

A fourth relation between $\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \xi_{3}$, and $\xi_{4}$ can be found by differentiating the logarithm of the electroneutrality condition for the majority defect pair considered with respect to $\log _{0}$. This electroneutrality condition can then be rewritten in the form:

$$
x_{1} \xi_{1}+x_{2} \xi_{2}+x_{3} \xi_{3}+x_{4} \xi_{4}=0
$$

with the aid of the following relation,

$$
\frac{\partial \log [\mathrm{j}]}{\partial \log \mathrm{x}}=a_{1} \frac{\partial \log \left[\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\alpha^{\prime}}\right]}{\partial \log \mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{a}_{2} \frac{\partial \log \left[\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{B}}^{\beta^{\prime}}\right]}{\partial \log \mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{a}_{3} \frac{\partial \log \left[\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{X}}^{\mathrm{Y}}\right]}{\partial \log \mathrm{x}}+a_{4} \frac{\partial \log \left[\mathrm{e}^{\prime}\right]}{\partial \log \mathrm{x}}
$$

in which x denotes either $\mathrm{a}_{0}$ or $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{X}_{2}}$.
The numbers $x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{3}$, and $x_{4}$ in Equation (5.72) are characteristic for the given majority defect pair. Solving the four linear equations in $\xi_{\mathrm{i}}$ yields the values presented in Table 5.3.

The values for $\mathrm{a}_{1}, \mathrm{a}_{2}, \mathrm{a}_{3}$, and $\mathrm{a}_{4}$ are given in Table 5.4.

## 2. Composition and Defect Chemistry

The compound $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{c}}$ is made up of $\mathrm{L}^{\cdot} \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{q}}+\mathrm{M}^{\cdot} \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{s}}+\mathrm{NX}_{2}$. $\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{M}$, and N are integers, L and M strictly positive, and N positive, zero, or negative. The total concentrations of A , $B$, and $X$ are

$$
\begin{gathered}
{[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{tot}}=\mathrm{L} \cdot \mathrm{p}} \\
{[\mathrm{~B}]_{\mathrm{tot}}=\mathrm{M} \cdot \mathrm{r}} \\
{[\mathrm{X}]_{\mathrm{tot}}=\mathrm{L} \cdot \mathrm{q}+\mathrm{M} \cdot \mathrm{~s}+2 \mathrm{~N}}
\end{gathered}
$$

TABLE 5.3
The Dependence of the Logarithm of Some Defects on the Logarithm of $\mathbf{a}_{\mathbf{0}}$ and $\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{x}_{\mathbf{2}}}$

$$
\begin{array}{lcc}
\text { [j] } & \frac{\partial \log [j]}{\partial \log a_{0}} P, T, p_{X_{2}} & \frac{\partial \log [j]}{\partial \log P_{X_{2}}} P, T, a_{0} \\
V_{A}^{\alpha^{\prime}} & \frac{-(a \alpha+\beta b) x_{2}-b\left(-x_{3}+x_{4}\right)}{C} & \frac{\alpha x_{4}}{B} \\
V_{B}^{\beta^{\prime}} & \frac{(\alpha a+\beta b) x_{2}+a\left(-x_{3}+x_{4}\right)}{C} & \frac{\beta x_{4}}{B} \\
V_{X}^{\gamma^{\cdot}} & \frac{-b \gamma x_{1}+a \gamma x_{2}}{C} & \frac{-\gamma x_{4}}{B} \\
\mathrm{e}^{\prime} & \frac{b x_{1}-a x_{2}}{C} & \frac{-a x_{1}-\beta x_{2}+\gamma x_{3}}{B} \\
& C=p b\left(\alpha x_{1}+\beta x_{2}-\gamma x_{3}+x_{4}\right) & B=2 \gamma\left(\alpha x_{1}+\beta x_{2}-\gamma x_{3}+x_{4}\right)
\end{array}
$$

The composition of the compound is known if the ratios

$$
\frac{[A]_{t o t}}{[B]_{t o t}} \text { and } \frac{[X]_{t o t}}{[A]_{t o t}}
$$

are known. For the ideal compound composition these ratios read

$$
\frac{[A]_{t o t}}{[B]_{t o t}}=\frac{L \cdot p}{M \cdot r}=\frac{a}{b}
$$

and

$$
\frac{[X]_{t o t}}{[A]_{t o t}}=\frac{L \cdot p+M \cdot s+2 N}{L \cdot p}=\frac{c}{a}
$$

The deviation from the ideal composition can be described by two parameters, i.e., $\Delta \mathrm{x}$, the deviation from molecularity, and $\Delta \mathrm{y}$, the deviation from stoichiometry. An excess $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{q}}$ gives $\Delta x>0$, and an excess $\mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{s}} \Delta x<0 . \Delta y$ is the parameter which determines, at a fixed ratio $\frac{L}{M}$, whether there is an excess $X_{2}(\Delta y>0)$ or a deficiency $X_{2}(\Delta y<0)$. The compound has the ideal composition for $\Delta x=\Delta y=0$. Defined in terms of Equations (5.77) and (5.78), these parameters are

TABLE 5.4
Coefficients a ${ }_{1},{ }_{2},{ }_{3}$, and ${ }_{4}$ in
Equation 5.73
for All Defects Considered
| [j] | $\mathrm{a}_{1}$ |  | $\mathbf{a}_{2}$ | $\mathrm{a}_{3}$ | $\mathrm{a}_{4}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A_{i}^{(\alpha-j)^{\cdot}}$ | -1 |  | 0 | 0 | +j |
| $B_{i}^{(\beta-k)^{\cdot}}$ | 0 |  | -1 | 0 | +k |
| $X_{i}^{(\gamma-1)^{\prime}}$ | 0 |  | 0 | -1 | +1 |
| $V_{A}^{\left(\alpha-j^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}}$ | +1 |  | 0 | 0 | $-\mathrm{j}^{\prime}$ |
| $V_{B}^{\left(\beta-k^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}}$ | 0 |  | +1 | 0 | -k' |
| $V_{x}^{\left(\gamma-1^{\prime}\right)^{\cdot}}$ | 0 |  | 0 | +1 | -l' |
| $A_{B}^{(\beta-\alpha-m)^{\prime}}$ | -1 |  | +1 | 0 | -m |
| $B_{A}^{(\beta-\alpha-n)}{ }^{\cdot}$ | +1 |  | -1 | 0 | +n |
| $\mathrm{e}^{\prime}$ | 0 |  | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| h* | 0 |  | 0 | 0 | -1 |
|  | $\Delta x=\frac{r}{p} \frac{[A]_{t o t}}{[B]_{t o t}}-\frac{a}{b}$ |  |  |  |  |


and

$$
\Delta y=\frac{\gamma[X]_{t o t}}{\alpha[A]_{t o t}+\beta[B]_{t o t}}-1
$$

Groenink ${ }^{34}$ has demonstrated that a given majority defect pair can only exist in a compound if certain conditions with regard to $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$ are fulfilled. The conditions are specific for the majority defect pair. For $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$, general relations including all the possible point defects were derived, ${ }^{34}$ and the condition for a given majority defect pair are then found by neglecting all the other point defect concentrations in the general relations for $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$. A complete survey of the conditions for all the possible majority defect pairs has been reported by Groenink ${ }^{34}$ and Groenink and Binsma. ${ }^{69}$ For given values of $\Delta \mathrm{x}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{y}$, only a limited number of majority defect pairs can exist in a compound, and which of these pairs occurs in practice depends upon temperature, pressure, and formation energy of the various defects.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-175.jpg?height=1310&width=877&top_left_y=126&top_left_x=342)
FIGURE 5.3. Kröger-Vink diagram of the model material $\mathrm{A}^{2+} \mathrm{B}^{6+} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}$, exhibiting deviations from molecularity.

## 3. Defect Diagrams

The Kröger-Vink diagrams can be constructed using Tables 5.3 and 5.4. It is evident that $\mathrm{a}_{0}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{X}_{2}}$ may vary only within the range of homogeneity of $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{b}} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{x}}$. Figure 5.3 is the Kröger-Vink diagram of a model material $\mathrm{ABO}_{4}$, in which $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$, and O are ions with a charge $+2,+6$, and -2 , respectively. In this diagram $\Delta x$ varies and $\Delta y=0$. For variations in $\Delta y$ at fixed $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{AO}}$, the diagram closely resembles that in Figure 5.2.

The Kröger-Vink diagrams of compounds $\mathrm{ABX}_{4}$, in which $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B}$, and X are ions with charge $+1,+3$, and -1 are presented in Figures 5.4 and 5.5. It is assumed that the model material exhibits anti-Frenkel disorder.

Figure 5.4 presents the relations between the point defect concentrations and a deviation from stoichiometry, while in Figure 5.5 these relations are plotted in the case of deviations from the stoichiometry. ${ }^{27}$

The quantitative relations between the point defect concentrations and the compound activities are very useful in interpreting electrical properties of solid electrolytes and MIECs. The point defect-composition relations also define the electrolytic domain of a solid electrolyte, and hence determine experimental conditions to be fulfilled in order for the materials to be applicable in solid state electrochemical devices.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-176.jpg?height=1051&width=872&top_left_y=126&top_left_x=345)
FIGURE 5.4. Kröger-Vink diagram of the model material $\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{3+} \mathrm{X}_{4}^{-}$, exhibiting deviations from molecularity.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-176.jpg?height=840&width=1300&top_left_y=1352&top_left_x=131)
FIGURE 5.5. Kröger-Vink diagram of the model material $\mathrm{A}^{+} \mathrm{B}^{3+} \mathrm{X}_{4}^{-}$, exhibiting deviations from stoichiometry for $\Delta x>0$.

It should be borne in mind, though, that the traditional mass action laws which govern the point defect concentrations are based on the assumptions of negligible interactions and random distributions. They are not applicable in high-defect concentration regimes. A general defect chemistry formulation based on statistical thermodynamic consideration has recently been developed by Ling. ${ }^{70}$ His statistical thermodynamic formulation explicitly incorporates long-range Coulombic interactions and the generalized exclusion effects. For solid solutions in the system $\mathrm{CeO}_{2}-\mathrm{CaO}$, Ling found good agreement between calculated and experimental conductivity data. The reaction enthalpies are found to be strongly influenced by defect interactions. Only at very high defect concentrations do the effects of defect exclusions become significant.

## 4. Application to Several Ternary Compounds

While intrinsic disorder of the Schottky, Frenkel, or anti-Frenkel type frequently occurs in binary metal oxides and metal halides, i.e., Equations (5.1), (5.3), and (5.5), Schottky disorder is seldomly encountered in ternary compounds. However, in several studies Schottky disorder has been proposed to occur in perovskite oxides. Cation and anion vacancies or interstitials can occur in ternary compounds, but such defect structures are usually to be related with deviations from molecularity (viz. Sections II.B. 2 and II.B.3), which in fact represent extrinsic disorder and not intrinsic Schottky disorder. From Figures 5.3 and 5.4 it is apparent that deviations from molecularity always influence ionic point defect concentrations, while deviations from stoichiometry always lead to combinations of ionic and electronic point defects, as can be seen from Figures 5.2 and 5.5.

A material which has attracted widespread attention is the perovskite oxide $\mathrm{LaCrO}_{3}$, and especially the $\mathrm{LaCrO}_{3}$-based solid solutions. It is the state-of-the-art interconnection material in the solid oxide fuel cell (SOFC). $\mathrm{LaCrO}_{3}$ is known to be a p-type conductor, owing to the presence of electron holes in the 3d-band of the Cr ions, i.e., $\mathrm{Cr}_{\mathrm{Cr}}$. Conduction occurs by the thermally activated hopping of localized charge carriers ( $\mathrm{Cr}_{\text {Cr }}$ ). ${ }^{71}$ Doping with relatively small amounts of $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}, \mathrm{Sr}^{2+}$, or $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ enhances the electronic conductivity. Under oxidizing conditions, charge compensation takes place by a $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ to $\mathrm{Cr}^{4+}$ transition, thereby increasing the electronic charge carrier density. Under reducing conditions, charge compensation takes place under the formation of oxygen ion vacancies, and no increased electronic conductivity is expected. The interconnection material in an SOFC operates under these extreme conditions. For $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$-doped $\mathrm{LaCrO}_{3}$ the defect $\mathrm{Mg}^{\prime}{ }_{\mathrm{Cr}}$ is formed. The acceptor is electrically compensated by the $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ to $\mathrm{Cr}^{4+}$ transition ${ }^{71,72}$ or by $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{\bullet \bullet}$. The electroneutrality condition reads

$$
\left[M g_{C r}^{\prime}\right]=\left[C r_{C r}^{\cdot}\right]+2\left[V_{O}^{\cdot \cdot}\right]
$$

Here, anti-Frenkel disorder is assumed to occur. The corresponding defect equilibrium can be written as

$$
O_{O}^{x}+2 C r_{C r}^{*} \rightleftarrows 2 C r_{C r}^{x}+V_{O}^{* *}+\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g)
$$

For this material other defect models have been proposed. ${ }^{73-76}$
Flandemeyer et al. ${ }^{73-74}$ and Anderson et al. ${ }^{75}$ used a defect model based on Schottky disorder, i.e.,

$$
O \rightleftarrows \quad V_{L a}^{\prime \prime \prime}+V_{C r}^{\prime \prime \prime}+3 V_{O}^{\bullet \bullet}
$$

To determine the influence of the oxygen partial pressure on the oxygen ion vacancy concentration, the following defect equilibrium was assumed:

$$
V_{L a}^{\prime \prime \prime}+V_{C r}^{\prime \prime \prime}+6 C r_{C r}^{\cdot}+3 O_{O}^{x} \rightleftarrows 6 C r_{C r}^{x}+\frac{3}{2} O_{2}(g)
$$

However, it is unlikely that this defect equilibrium determines the oxygen ion vacancy concentration in Mg-doped $\mathrm{LaCrO}_{3}$, as the presence of trivalent cation vacancies in ternary metal oxides is not favored thermodynamically. ${ }^{10}$

In the proposed defect models it is assumed that all defects are distributed randomly over the lattice. Singhal et al., ${ }^{76}$ Van Roosmalen and Cordfunke, ${ }^{77}$ and Van Roosmalen ${ }^{78}$ have proposed defect structures including defect clusters comprising $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{* *}$ next to regular $\mathrm{Cr}_{\mathrm{Cr}}^{\mathrm{x}}$ ions, and $\left(\mathrm{Mg}_{\mathrm{Cr}^{-}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}{ }^{\ddot{O}}-\mathrm{Mg}_{\mathrm{Cr}^{-}}{ }^{\mathrm{x}}\right.$ clusters. While no rationale was presented for the trapping of oxygen ion vacancies at regular $\mathrm{Cr}_{\mathrm{Cr}}^{\mathrm{x}}$ sites, the ( $\left.2 \mathrm{Mg}_{\mathrm{Cr}} \cdot \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}\right)^{\mathrm{x}}$ cluster model seems to describe the experimentally determined oxygen ion vacancy concentrations as a function of $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ by Flandemeyer et al. ${ }^{73,74}$ and Anderson et al. ${ }^{75}$ The corresponding defect equilibrium including these clusters can be represented by

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left(L a_{L a}^{x}\right)\left(C r_{C r}^{\cdot}\right)_{x}\left(C r_{C r}^{x}\right)_{1-2 x}\left(M g_{C r}^{\prime}\right)_{x}\left(O_{O}^{x}\right)_{3} \\
& \rightleftarrows\left(L a_{L a}^{x}\right)\left(C r_{C r}^{\cdot}\right)_{x-2 \delta}\left(C r_{C r}^{x}\right)_{1-2 x+2 \delta} \\
& \left(M g_{C r}^{\prime}\right)_{x-2 \delta}\left(\left(M g_{C r}^{\prime}-V_{O}^{\cdot \cdot}-M g_{C r}^{\prime}\right)^{x}\right)_{\delta}\left(O_{O}^{x}\right)_{3-\delta} \\
& \quad+\frac{\delta}{2} O_{2}(g)
\end{aligned}
$$

Usually, the interconnection material Mg -doped $\mathrm{LaCrO}_{3}$ is produced using electrochemical vapor deposition (EVD). EVD is the key technology developed by Westinghouse for the production of gas tight layers of SOFC solid electrolyte and interconnection materials on porous electrode structures. Recently, Van Dieten et al. ${ }^{79-81}$ used anti-Frenkel disorder in $\mathrm{LaCrO}_{3}$ along with Equations (5.81) and (5.82) to quantitatively model the EVD growth of Mg -doped $\mathrm{LaCrO}_{3} .{ }^{81}$

The clustering concept was not employed in the modeling of the EVD growth, because the EVD process is carried out at temperatures in the range of 1400 to 1600 K . At these temperatures these defect clusters are assumed to be not stable.

The presented concepts for $\Delta \mathrm{x}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{y}$ have been used in other studies on SOFC electrode and interconnection materials exhibiting mixed ionic (via $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{o}}^{\bullet \bullet}$ ) and electronic conductivity. ${ }^{82-84}$

The perovskites $\mathrm{La}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{B} \mathrm{O}_{3-\delta}(\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{Sr}, \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Cr}, \mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{Mn})$ have been reviewed by Alcock et al. ${ }^{85}$ in terms of their stability in temperature and oxygen partial pressure of operation as active sensor material, nonstoichiometry, conductivity, and catalytic properties, and the effect of the strontium content, x.

The deviations from stoichiometry in these perovskites are accounted for using Equation (5.67). The defect models used for the MIECs discussed so far are all concordant with the defect model used by Van Dieten et al. ${ }^{79-81}$ for the modeling of the EVD growth of the SOFC interconnection material.

Another important class of ternary oxides with mixed ionic-electronic conductivity is based on the pyrochlore system, $\mathrm{A}_{2} \mathrm{~B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$. Especially, Tuller and co-workers ${ }^{86-89}$ have studied the defect chemistry of nominally pure, and donor- and acceptor-doped $\mathrm{Gd}_{2} \mathrm{Ti}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ (GT). They have demonstrated that mixed ionic and electronic conductivity can be controlled over wide limits in the pyrochlore oxides described by compositions $\mathrm{Gd}_{2}\left(\mathrm{Ti}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Zr}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ (GZT) and $\mathrm{Y}_{2}\left(\mathrm{Ti}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Zr}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}(\mathrm{YZT})$ by control of both the parameter x and by doping. If GT is doped with $\mathrm{Ca}^{86}$ or by selecting $\mathrm{x}>0.4$ in GZT, ${ }^{87,88}$ an excellent solid electrolyte is obtained. Reduced or donor-doped GT exhibits predominantly a $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$-dependent semiconducting behavior. ${ }^{90}$

The ionic conductivity increases in GZT as Zr substitutes for Ti due to increasing anion disorder, and this appears to be related to the decrease in the average B to A cation radius ratio, which, in the limit, leads to full disordering to the defect fluorite structure $(\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{B})_{4} \mathrm{O}_{7}$. Also, the doping with multivalent donors and acceptors has been studied. An in-depth review has been reported by Spears and Tuller, ${ }^{91}$ in which the application of defect modeling of the pyrochlores to materials design is discussed.

The electrical properties of the titanate-based pyrochlores can be described by point defect models in which the acceptor (A) and donor (D) impurities are compensated by oxide ion vacancies, or oxide ion interstitials, respectively. ${ }^{92}$ The principal defect reactions include the redox reaction, Equation (5.67), the Frenkel disorder, Equation (5.57), dopant ionization, intrinsic electronic disorder, Equation (5.60), and the electroneutrality relation. For these compounds the total electroneutrality condition given by Equation (5.55) is, on the one hand, reduced, taking Equation (5.57) into account, and is, on the other hand, extended to include acceptor and donor impurities. In addition, defect association is included explicitly.

The electroneutrality relation thus reads,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2\left[O_{i}^{\prime \prime}\right]+\left[O_{i}^{\prime}\right]+\left[e^{\prime}\right]+\left[A^{\prime}\right]+\left(D \cdot O_{i}\right)^{\prime}= \\
& \quad 2\left[V_{O}^{*}\right]+\left[V_{O}^{*}\right]+\left[h^{*}\right]+\left[D^{*}\right]+\left[\left(A \cdot V_{O}\right)^{*}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Now the more complex defect equilibria are treated with the aid of numerical methods. Spears and Tuller ${ }^{91}$ show how this approach can be used, not only to extract principal defect thermodynamic and kinetic data, but also to assist in the design and optimization of materials. Using all possible defect reactions and defect equilibria, combined with Equation (5.86), one arrives at an eight-order polynomial in, for instance, $\left[\mathrm{e}^{\prime}\right]$, which can only be solved numerically. In a simplified approach for the nominally pure material, expressions for $\left[\mathrm{e}^{\prime}\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{h}^{\bullet}\right]$ are obtained, which are concordant to Equations (5.51) and (5.52). The simple Kröger-Vink diagram for such a defect situation has already been presented by Kofstad. ${ }^{93}$

For the more complex extrinsic defect structure, the Newton-Raphson method was used to numerically solve the high-order polynomials, which come about during attempts to solve the simultaneous defect equations, using the complete electroneutrality condition. For accep-tor-doped $\mathrm{Gd}_{2} \mathrm{Ti}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ with strong acceptor-oxide ion vacancy association, Spears and Tuller ${ }^{91}$ report the Kröger-Vink diagram for the $\log \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}(\mathrm{~atm})$ regime -50 to +50 . Using this approach, mixed conduction in $\mathrm{Gd}_{2}\left(\mathrm{Zr}_{0.3} \mathrm{Ti}_{0.7}\right)_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ was modeled in terms of the $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ dependence of the point defect concentrations $\left[\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{* *}\right],\left[\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}\right]$, and $\left[\mathrm{e}^{\prime}\right]$ for $\log \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}(\mathrm{~atm})$ in the region -25 to +5 . For $\log \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}<-15$ the reduced electroneutrality condition

$$
2\left[V_{O}^{\bullet \bullet}\right]=\left[e^{\prime}\right]
$$

holds, while for larger $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ values the condition reads,

$$
2\left[V_{O}^{\bullet \bullet}\right]=\left[e^{\prime}\right]+2\left[O_{i}^{\prime \prime}\right]
$$

In GZT, with $\mathrm{x}=0,3$, and doped with an acceptor of $1 \%$ substitution for Gd, i.e., $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}{ }_{\mathrm{Gd}}$, the oxide ion vacancy concentration is increased at the expense of $\left[\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime \prime}\right]$, viz. Equation (5.57). Likewise, the electron hole conductivity is increased at the expense of the electron conduction.

The calculated $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ dependencies at 1373 K of the predominant defect concentrations in $\mathrm{Gd}_{2}\left(\mathrm{Zr}_{0.3} \mathrm{Ti}_{0.7}\right)_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ reveal that a transition from ionic disorder, $\left[\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{\bullet \bullet}\right]=\left[\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime \prime}\right]$, at high $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ to
reduction control $\left[\mathrm{e}^{\prime}\right]=2\left[\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{* *}\right]$ occurs at $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \cdot 10^{-15} \mathrm{~atm}$. For $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}} \cdot 10 \mathrm{~atm}$ a $\mathrm{p}-\mathrm{n}$ junction occurs. ${ }^{91}$ GZT doped with 1 at. percent acceptor, $\mathrm{A}_{\text {Gd }}^{\prime}$, reveals a narrowing of the defect regime defined by $\left[\mathrm{e}^{\prime}\right]=2\left[\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{\bullet \bullet}\right]$.

Also, in acceptor-doped perovskites $\left(\mathrm{Ba}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Sr}_{1-\mathrm{x}}\right) \mathrm{TiO}_{3}$ the principal defects and their dependence on $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$, dopant concentrations, and temperature could be obtained. ${ }^{91,94}$ The approach has recently also been applied successfully to the pyrochlore system $\left(\mathrm{Gd}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Ca}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Sn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7 \pm \delta}{ }^{95}$

For cost and stability reasons, many materials studies for SOFCs are directed toward lowering the operating temperature from $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to below $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Since the pioneering work of Iwahara and co-workers, ${ }^{96,97}$ who discovered high-temperature proton conductivity in the perovskite oxides $\mathrm{SrCeO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{BaCeO}_{3}$, cerate-based perovskite-type oxide ceramics have attracted widespread attention. Doping with trivalent cations, which substitute for cerium, is essential for the occurrence of proton conduction in hydrogen-containing ambients at high temperatures.
$\mathrm{SrCe}_{0.95} \mathrm{Y}_{0.05} \mathrm{O}_{3-\alpha}, \mathrm{SrCe}_{0.95} \mathrm{Yb}_{0.05} \mathrm{O}_{3-\alpha}$, and $\mathrm{BaCe}_{0.9} \mathrm{Nd}_{0.1} \mathrm{O}_{3-\alpha}$ are examples of this class of proton-conducting oxide ceramics. The state of the art has been reviewed by Iwahara. 98,99 Besides application in SOFCs, these solid electrolytes have been explored for use in hightemperature steam electrolyzers, hydrogen sensors and pumps, and electroceramic reactors for (de-)hydrogenation processes. ${ }^{98-105}$ A major disadvantage of this class of materials is their instability in $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$-containing atmospheres. ${ }^{106,107}$

The defect chemistry of these oxides has been studied in relation to the formation of protons in equilibrium with different ambients. The general formula for these oxides can be represented by

$$
\left(M_{M}^{x}\right)\left(C e_{C e}^{x}\right)_{1-x}\left(M e_{C e}^{\prime}\right)_{x}\left(O_{O}^{x}\right)_{3-\frac{1}{2} x}\left(V_{O}^{\bullet \bullet}\right)_{\frac{x}{2}}
$$

with electroneutrality condition.

$$
\left[M e_{C e}^{\prime}\right]_{x}=2\left[V_{O}^{\bullet \bullet}\right]
$$

Here, M represents Sr , or Ba , and Me a rare earth element. Usually x is smaller than 0.1.
In dry oxygen atmosphere the formation of electron holes is described by

$$
\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g)+V_{O}^{\bullet \bullet} \stackrel{K_{h}}{\rightleftarrows} O_{O}^{x}+2 h^{.}
$$

In a wet oxygen atmosphere both protons and electron holes are generated according to

$$
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g})+V_{O}^{\bullet \bullet} \stackrel{K_{H, 1}}{\rightleftarrows} O_{O}^{x}+2 H_{i}^{\bullet}
$$

and

$$
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{~h}^{*} \stackrel{K_{H, 2}}{\rightleftarrows} 2 \mathrm{H}_{i}^{*}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})
$$

The equilibrium state of the oxides can be described by any two of these equilibrium reactions, as

$$
K_{H, 1}=K_{h} K_{H, 2}
$$

In a dry hydrogen-containing atmosphere the equilibrium

$$
H_{2}(g)+2 h^{*} \rightleftarrows 2 H_{i}^{*}
$$

is established.
Hence the electrical properties of the $\mathrm{MCeO}_{3}$-based perovskite-type oxides are quite dependent on the ambients, and can be summarized as follows: ${ }^{98,99}$

- $\mathrm{MCe}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Me}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{O}_{3-\mathrm{Hx}}$ exhibits electron hole conductivity, when sintered in air, and measured in a dry, hydrogen-free atmosphere.
- In atmospheres containing water vapor or hydrogen, protonic conduction increases at the expense of electronic conductivity.
- In pure hydrogen the transference number of the protons becomes unity at high temperatures.
- Mixed oxide and proton conduction occurs in the $\mathrm{BaCeO}_{3}$-based solid electrolytes, when utilized in a SOFC above $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

The proton defect that appears in the gas-solid Equilibria (5.91), (5.92), and (5.94) is represented by $\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{i}}$. It is generally accepted that the protons are bonded to the oxide ions at regular lattice sites, thus forming a hydroxide ion, $\mathrm{OH}_{\mathrm{O}}^{*}$, which is often referred to as $\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{i}}^{*}$.

Equilibrium (5.94) can also be represented by

$$
\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{O}_{O}^{x}+2 h^{\cdot} \rightleftarrows 2 \mathrm{OH}_{O}^{\cdot}
$$

Likewise, equilibria (5.91) and (5.92) can be rewritten to express the formation of $\mathrm{OH}_{\mathrm{O}}$ defects.

The introduction of protonic defects in oxides in hydrogen-containing ambients has been reported frequently, and has recently been reviewed by Colomban and Novak ${ }^{108}$ and Strelkov et al. ${ }^{108}$

For a doped oxide $\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ exhibiting anti-Frenkel disorder, Colomban and Novak ${ }^{108}$ present a schematic Kröger-Vink diagram of the extrinsic and intrinsic point defects as a function of the partial water pressure. With regard to electrical properties, the proton conductivity in the binary metal oxides is usually much lower than in the perovskite-type oxides. ${ }^{108}$

## C. MULTINARY COMPOUNDS

While in many of the fundamental studies of extrinsic and intrinsic disorder simple binary and ternary compounds have been investigated, the search for new materials for application in solid state batteries, high-temperature fuel cells and steam electrolyzers, electrocatalytic reactors, gas-separation membranes, smart windows, and chemical gas sensors has provided the solid state community with a wide variety of multinary materials. Prime examples are the $\beta$ - and $\beta$ "-aluminas, sodium ion conductors with the NASICON framework structure, lithium ion conductors with the LISICON structures, oxide Brownmillerite systems, MIECs in the doped $\mathrm{ABO}_{3}-\mathrm{ABO}_{2.5}$ series, layered perovskite structures, proton-conducting hydrated oxides, and the ceramic high $T_{c}$ superconductors. In addition, solid solutions based on transition metal oxides represent interesting multinary compounds for solid state lithium batteries.

Many studies on multinary compounds have been reported and reviewed in the past decade. ${ }^{109-118}$ Excellent surveys of crystalline multinary solid electrolytes have been reported by West ${ }^{119}$ and Goodenough. ${ }^{120}$

These authors have reviewed positional disorder in stoichiometric compounds in relation to crystal structure, materials exhibiting first-order phase transformations, and diffuse, socalled Faraday, transitions, extrinsic disorder, doping strategies, and solid solutions.

In addition to these surveys, the crystal chemistry and electrochemical properties of a wide variety of multinary oxide solid electrolytes and MIECs are described in the studies reported in Reference 90. While defect chemistry has been successfully employed to model point defect generation and conduction mechanisms in nominally pure and doped binary and ternary compounds, complex defect equilibria occur in concentrated solid solutions based on ternary compounds and in multinary compounds. Usually, these complex defect equilibria are treated with the aid of numerical methods. Examples are the pyrochlores GT, GZT, and YZT as discussed in Section II.B.4.

Since the discovery of the high $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{c}}$ ceramic superconductors, ${ }^{121}$ various properties of these oxide ceramics have been studied, and this is especially true for $\mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-\mathrm{x}}$.

The critical temperature of the high $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{c}}$ ceramic superconductors is intimately connected to the electronic charge carrier concentration, but at temperatures beyond the critical temperature in the nonsuperconducting state, mixed ionic-electronic conduction prevails, especially at the operation temperatures of the solid state electrochemical devices mentioned in the previous sections. In this regime a number of properties are determined by deviations from stoichiometry, which can be related to the high diffusivity of oxide ions in the structure of the high $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{c}}$ ceramic superconductors. In particular, mixed ionic-electronic conductivity in ( $\mathrm{La}, \mathrm{Sr})_{2} \mathrm{CuO}_{4-\mathrm{x}}, \mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-\mathrm{x}}, \mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{Sr}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{6+\mathrm{x}}$, and $\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{Sr}_{2} \mathrm{CaCu}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8+\mathrm{x}}$ has attracted much attention, and has initiated defect chemical studies. ${ }^{114,115,118,122-126}$

For $\mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-\mathrm{x}}$ it has been established that a transition from electron hole to electron conduction occurs for $7-\mathrm{x}<6.3$. For $\mathrm{x}<0.7$ oxygen is incorporated according to

$$
\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g)+V_{i}^{x} \rightleftarrows O_{i}^{\prime \prime}+2 h^{\cdot}
$$

The oxide interstitials can trap an electron hole to form $\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{*}$ defects.

$$
O_{i}^{\prime \prime}+h^{\cdot} \rightleftarrows O_{i}^{\prime}
$$

Here, $\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$ means $\left(\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime \prime} \cdot \mathrm{h}^{*}\right)^{\prime}$ or $\left(\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime \prime} \mathrm{Cu}_{\mathrm{Cu}}^{*}\right)^{\prime}$. For $\mathrm{x}<0.7$ the isothermal conductivity reveals a $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}{ }^{\mathrm{H}}$ dependence, indeed indicating the presence of $\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$ defects. ${ }^{122,123}$ The actual incorporation equilibrium reaction is therefore

$$
\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g)+V_{i}^{x} \rightleftarrows O_{i}^{\prime}+h^{\cdot}
$$

The preference of $\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$ may be related to the fact that in the Cu plane of $\mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-\mathrm{x}}$, a Cu ion belongs to one structurally vacant oxygen site ( $\mathrm{x}=1$ ). The incorporation of further oxygen should mainly lead to the formation of $\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\mathrm{x}}$,

$$
O_{i}^{\prime}+h^{\cdot} \rightleftarrows O_{i}^{x}
$$

In a different approach, the deviation from stoichiometry of $\mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-\mathrm{x}}(0 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 0.7)$ can be described using oxide ion vacancies. The gas-solid equilibrium is then described by

$$
\frac{1}{2} O_{2}(g)+V_{O}^{\bullet \bullet}\left(\text { or } V_{O}^{*}\right) \rightleftarrows O_{O}^{x}+2 h^{*}\left(\text { or } h^{*}\right)
$$

For nominally pure $\mathrm{La}_{2} \mathrm{CuO}_{4}$ the electron hole conductivity has been found to follow a $1 / 6$ power law dependence on oxygen partial pressure, indicating the gas-solid equilibrium 5.96 to occur. For Sr -doped $\mathrm{La}_{2} \mathrm{CuO}_{4}$ incorporation reactions have been presented by Opila et al. ${ }^{126}$ to account for an increase in $\left[h^{\cdot}\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{\cdot}\right]$. The total electroneutrality condition reads

$$
2\left[O_{i}^{\prime \prime}\right]+\left[S r_{L a}^{\prime}\right]+\left[e^{\prime}\right]=2\left[V_{O}^{\bullet \bullet}\right]+\left[h^{\cdot}\right]
$$

The Kröger-Vink diagrams have been calculated for $(\mathrm{La}, \mathrm{Sr})_{2} \mathrm{CuO}_{4},^{124-126}$ assuming fixed doping concentration and temperature. With increasing oxygen partial pressure, the following reduced electroneutrality conditions hold:

$$
\left[S r_{L a}^{\prime}\right]=2\left[V_{O}^{\bullet \bullet}\right],\left[S r_{L a}^{\prime}\right]=\left[h^{*}\right], \text { and }\left[h^{\cdot}\right]=\left[O_{i}^{\prime}\right]
$$

At fixed oxygen partial pressure and temperature, an increase in dopant concentration reveals the following majority defect conditions:

$$
\left[h^{\cdot}\right]=2\left[O_{i}^{\prime \prime}\right],\left[S r_{L a}^{\prime}\right]=\left[h^{\cdot}\right],\left[S r_{L a}^{\prime}\right], \quad=2\left[V_{O}^{\cdot \cdot}\right]
$$

For $\mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-x}$ tentative Kröger-Vink diagrams have been constructed under the assumption of excess Y , and in the case of anti-Frenkel disorder. ${ }^{124-126}$ In the first case the electroneutrality conditions are with increasing $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ :

$$
\left[Y_{B a}^{\prime}\right]=\left[O_{i}^{\prime}\right],\left[O_{i}^{\prime}\right]=\left[h^{\cdot}\right]
$$

In the case of Frenkel disorder the majority defect pairs are

$$
\left[O_{i}^{\prime}\right]=\left[V_{O}^{\bullet}\right],\left[O_{i}^{\prime}\right]=\left[h^{\cdot}\right]
$$

In the high $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ regimes, i.e., $\left[\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\bullet}\right]=[\mathrm{h}]$ the concentration of $\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\mathrm{x}}$ exceeds that of the charged point defects.

In order to obtain quantitative information on the oxide ion conductivity of high $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{c}}$ ceramic superconductors, solid state electrochemical cells of the type

$$
\text { Pt(air)/YSZ/YBa } \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-x} / \mathrm{YSZ} / \mathrm{Pt} \text { (air) }
$$

were studied by impedance spectroscopy. ${ }^{127,128}$ Here, YSZ represents yttria-stabilized zirconia. Similar cell configurations were used to study other high $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{c}}$ ceramic superconductors. ${ }^{124,129}$

A detailed discussion of oxide ion conductivity as obtained using the solid state electrochemical techniques is beyond the scope of this chapter.

Recent high-temperature applications of these ceramic superconductors are

- a novel chromatography detector ${ }^{130}$
- heterogeneous catalysis, ${ }^{131-133}$ and
- Taguchi-type gas sensors. ${ }^{134-137}$

McNamara et al. ${ }^{130}$ used $\mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-x}$ as a catalyst for redox reactions between organic molecules and $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ in a chromatography detector, based on chemoluminescence. $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ is
converted to NO, which is subsequently detected via a chemoluminescence reaction with ozone, i.e.,

$$
\mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{O}_{3} \rightarrow \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2}+h \nu
$$

Hansen et al. ${ }^{131}$ have studied the oxydation of hydrocarbons to $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ over $\mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-\mathrm{x}}$ as a catalyst. Besides full oxydation to $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$, process conditions can be manipulated such as to partially oxidize hydrocarbons to a variety of industrial chemicals.

In addition to deviations from stoichiometry in the anion sublattice, $\mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-\mathrm{x}}$ has been shown to absorb nitrogen, argon, hydrogen, and nitrogen oxide. The solubility of these gases is dependent on the oxide stoichiometry, but does not affect the perovskite structure. At $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 3 \mathrm{~mol} \%$ of NO can be dissolved into $\mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-x}$. Desorption occurs at $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The amount of absorbed NO increases with the parameter x. The NO molecules occupy the empty oxide ion sites. For high concentrations of NO, decomposition occurs according to

$$
\begin{gathered}
2 \mathrm{NO}+2 \mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-x} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{CuO}+\mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{BaCuO}_{5}+ \\
\mathrm{Ba}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)_{2}+2 \mathrm{BaCuO} \mathrm{O}_{2}
\end{gathered}
$$

For simplicity, x has been assumed to be zero here.
Besides absorption of NO, $\mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-\mathrm{x}}$ has been shown to be very effective in the catalytic decomposition of NO to $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}$. This decomposition is related to the amount of absorbed NO, and hence to the deviation from stoichiometry. This is, in fact, one of the first examples of the direct decomposition of NO in $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}$. ${ }^{133}$

The high $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{c}}$ ceramic superconductors have been tested in Taguchi-type gas sensors. ${ }^{134-137}$ The Taguchi gas sensor ${ }^{138}$ is based on a semiconducting metal oxide, which exhibits a resistance change when gas molecules adsorb at the surface and influence the charge carrier concentration in the metal oxide. $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}, \mathrm{In}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, and solid solutions based on these oxides have frequently been studied as active material of the Taguchi sensor.

The sensitivity to $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ and $\mathrm{CO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ has been investigated for $\mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-\mathrm{x}}, \mathrm{Ba}_{1.5} \mathrm{La}_{1.5} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{x}}$, $\mathrm{La}_{1.85} \mathrm{Sr}_{0.15} \mathrm{CuO}_{\mathrm{x}}, \mathrm{Nd}_{1.85} \mathrm{Ce}_{0.15} \mathrm{CuO}_{4-\mathrm{x}}, \mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{Sr}_{2} \mathrm{CuO}_{6+\mathrm{x}}, \mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{Sr}_{2} \mathrm{CaCu}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8+\mathrm{x}}$, and $\mathrm{Bi}_{1.8} \mathrm{~Pb}_{0.2} \mathrm{Sr}_{2} \mathrm{Ca}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{10+\mathrm{x}}$. As expected, $\mathrm{YBa}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{7-\mathrm{x}}$ reacts with NO and decomposes. $\mathrm{Ba}_{1.5} \mathrm{La}_{1.5} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{x}}$ and $\mathrm{La}_{1.85} \mathrm{Sr}_{0.15} \mathrm{CuO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ did not show any sensitivity or selectivity of practical interest, while $\mathrm{Nd}_{1.85} \mathrm{Ce}_{0.15} \mathrm{CuO}_{4+\mathrm{x}}$ has no selectivity for NO against CO . However, the Bifamility materials are good candidates for the development of Taguchi-type $\mathrm{NO}_{\mathrm{x}}$ gas sensors. $\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{Sr}_{2} \mathrm{CuO}_{6+\mathrm{x}}, \mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{Sr}_{2} \mathrm{CaCu}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8+\mathrm{x}}$, and $\mathrm{Bi}_{1.8} \mathrm{~Pb}_{0.2} \mathrm{Sr}_{2} \mathrm{Ca}_{2} \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{10+\mathrm{x}}$ exhibit good stability, reproducibility, and high selectivity against CO and $\mathrm{CO}_{2} . \mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{Sr}_{2} \mathrm{CaCu}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8+\mathrm{x}}$ exhibits the best response behavior. Composites of $\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{Sr}_{2} \mathrm{CaCu}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{8+\mathrm{x}}$ with NiO or $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ show improved selectivity to NO against CO. The addition of $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ does not affect the selectivity, but does enhance the response rate. Lithium intercalation also improved the selectivity to NO against CO. The detection mechanisms have been reported by Huang et al., ${ }^{134-137}$ and they are based on reactions (5.96) and (5.100).

## D. MULTICOMPONENT MATERIALS

Since the initial observations that the dispersion of small particles of nonconducting alumina in lithium iodide leads to a dramatic increase of the lithium ion conductivity, ${ }^{139,140}$ a number of studies have been devoted to the electrical properties of these multicomponent systems. ${ }^{141-147}$

In general, it is found that the increase in isothermal ionic conductivity is greater the smaller the size of the dispersed inert particle, and that the conductivity-composition curve exhibits a maximum between 10 and $40 \mathrm{~mol} \%$ of inert particles. It is generally accepted that
the conductivity enhancement can be ascribed to an increase in the defect concentration responsible for extrinsic conduction in a narrow space charge layer in the conducting compound at the grain boundary interface. This "composite effect" occurs mainly at low temperatures. ${ }^{148}$ Surprisingly, practically all the studies on this composite effect have dealt with cation conducting compounds. A few studies have been reported on YSZ-alumina composites, ${ }^{149-151}$ but the results have not led to firm conclusions. In a more systematic study, Filal et al. ${ }^{152}$ have investigated single crystalline, polycrystalline, and composites of YSZ. For composites comprising YSZ ( $9.9 \mathrm{~mol} \%$ ) and Mg-doped $\alpha$-alumina of submicron size, a maximum is observed for $2 \mathrm{~mol} \% \alpha-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$. At $250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ the grain boundary conductivity is increased by a factor of 3.7 and at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ by a factor of 2 . While the composite effect has been established for stabilized zirconia, a mechanism is lacking. For $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ composites the composite effect has been observed by Fujitsu et al. ${ }^{153}$ Here, a complication may occur, as the ionic radii of oxide and fluoride ions are comparable, and $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ exhibits anti-Frenkel disorder. As a consequence, $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$, or more general the alkali earth fluorides, are easily contaminated with oxide ions to form the defects $\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{F}}^{\prime}+\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{F}}^{\cdot}$. If at the interface $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ this doping effect occurs, it may mask space charge effects.

Maier ${ }^{154,155}$ has calculated the concentration profiles of minority charge carriers in ideal space charge regions. In addition, the solid state electrochemical Hebb-Wagner polarization technique was extended to the case of multicomponent materials. ${ }^{154}$ The behavior of the minority carriers in the space charge regions is determined by the profiles of the majority carriers. By applying the theoretical model on the composite $\mathrm{AgCl}-\gamma-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, Maier ${ }^{155}$ found that both the electron and the electron hole conductivity increased, compared to single-phase AgCl . In contrast, a shift in charge carrier type is observed on going from pure LiI (electron holes) ${ }^{139}$ to $\mathrm{LiI}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ (electrons). ${ }^{145}$ Similar observations were reported for $\beta$-AgI-alumina composites. Pure $\beta$-AgI exhibits electron conduction, ${ }^{146}$ while the composite has electron hole conduction. As has been pointed out by Maier, ${ }^{154}$ the extra space charge contribution due to the minority charge carriers depends sensitively on impurity levels, and these are not specified in the studies on LiI- and $\beta$-AgI-based composites.

In order to shed more light on these phenomena, Maier ${ }^{156}$ has calculated the defect concentrations for ionic and electronic defects in space charge regions as a function of temperature and component activities.

Using the model compound, of which the bulk defect chemistry is treated in Section II.A. 1 of this chapter, Maier constructed a Kröger-Vink diagram of the model compound MX for bulk and boundary layer. His results are schematically included in Figure 5.2, and presented in Figure 5.6.

In this example it has been assumed that the bulk structure extends up to the top surface (interface) layer, in which the excess charges are accommodated and in which the potentials and defect concentrations have discretely different values. For the top layer a linear geometry is assumed. Reaction (5.12) holds for the top layer. As long as one or both ionic point defects determine the charge density alone, both ionic point defect concentrations can be handled as a constant (cf. Reaction (5.15)), as is done for the bulk. In the Kröger-Vink diagram the same slopes arise for the ionic point defects, i.e., 0 , and $\pm \mathrm{H}$ for the electronic defects, i.e., Equations (5.28) and (5.30). Due to the space charge effect the ranges of validity are different. In the present example a positive space charge potential has been assumed, which implies accumulation of $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{M}}^{\prime}$ and $\mathrm{e}^{\prime}$, and depletion of $\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\cdot}$ and $\mathrm{h}^{\cdot}$.

The model has been used to calculate the point defect profiles as a function of component activity and temperature for the multicomponent system $\mathrm{AgCl}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$.

For an acceptor-doped metal oxide, Maier presents a qualitative Kröger-Vink diagram assuming a positive space charge potential. Here, the extrinsic regime is increased due to depletion of $\mathrm{e}^{\prime}$ and accumulation of $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}} \cdot \cdot{ }^{156}$ At low $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ values the curves are approaching the bulk behavior. This particular situation is observed for $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ in $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ ambients, and has been used to discuss the sensing behavior of $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$ in a Taguchi sensor.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-186.jpg?height=1243&width=1091&top_left_y=122&top_left_x=234)
FIGURE 5.6. Kröger-Vink diagrams of MX with Frenkel disorder ( $-=$ bulk, --- = boundary layer).

A large number of problems in solid state electrochemistry are determined by the defect chemistry in boundary regions rather than in the bulk. Taking the defect chemistry in space charge layers into account, unusual partial pressure dependences of electronic conductivity, which cannot be understood in terms of bulk defect chemistry, can be made plausible. Göpel and Lampe ${ }^{157}$ observed for acceptor-doped ZnO , with a defect chemistry quite similar to $\mathrm{SnO}_{2}$, an accumulation layer for $\mathrm{e}^{\prime}$ and as exponents for the $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ dependence of the conductivity -0.25 (bulk) and $-0.15 \pm 0.03$ (space charge layer). The bulk value is easily explained by a constant ionic point defect concentration in the acceptor regime.

If a strong depletion of oxide ion vacancies occurs in the space charge layer, electrons should form the majority carriers there. Maier ${ }^{156}$ found under these conditions and under the assumptions that no frozen-in profiles exist and a low mobility for the acceptor impurities, for the $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ dependence of the space charge conductivity theoretically the exponent -J , which is concordant with the experimental value.

## III. CONCLUDING REMARKS

Defect chemistry is a chemistry within the solid state that is analogous to the long-familiar chemistry in the liquid phase, and arises from departures from the ideal crystal structure which are thermodynamically unavoidable, the point defects. While defect chemistry foundations were established over 60 years ago, this area of chemistry enables one to date to
describe in a unified manner a wide variety of phenomena, based on the dynamical behavior of ionic and electronic species, in electroceramics, MIECs, chemically active ceramics, optoceramics, photographic materials, scale growth on metals, and many more.

It has been demonstrated that the classical equilibrium defect chemical concepts derived for binary compounds can be applied to ternary and multinary compounds. In the case of multicomponent materials, the space charge effects will become very important in cases in which the dimensions are no longer large compared with the thickness of the space charge layers, as in extremely thin films or in structural and functional ceramics with crystallites of nanometer dimensions. The formation of latent images in silver halide photography represents a prelude to effects of point defects in nanostructured materials, and is related to enlarged concentrations of point defects in boundary layers.

To date the development of nanostructured materials is receiving widespread attention, and it is believed that classical defect chemical concepts will contribute to the pace of advance in our understanding of improved or new properties of either single-phase or multiphase materials. With regard to multiphase materials the ceramic-metal composites (cermets) with percolation-type conductivity behavior like YSZ-Pd or $\mathrm{BaCeO}_{3}$-Pd, which exhibit enhanced diffusion of oxide ions and protons, respectively, represent a novel class of gas-separation membranes. While mixed oxide, proton, and electronic conductivity may occur in these cermets, the mechanisms of mass and charge transport will largely be governed by the dynamical behavior of ionic point defects. Hence, classical defect chemistry is invaluable for the field of solid state electrochemistry.
