# SURVEY OF TYPES OF SOLID ELECTROLYTES 

## Tetsuichi Kudo

## CONTENTS

I. Introduction
II. Oxide Ion Conductors
III. Fluoride Ion Conductors
IV. Silver and Copper Ion Conductors
V. Sodium and Potassium Ion Conductors
VI. Lithium Ion Conductors
VII. Proton Conductors
VIII. Polymer Solid Electrolytes

References

## I. INTRODUCTION

Solid electrolytes are a class of materials exhibiting high ionic conductivity like electrolyte solutions, though they are in the solid state. Their history dates back to 1834 when Michael Faraday observed high electrical conductivity of $\mathrm{PbF}_{2}$ at high temperature. In the early part of this century, cation conduction in silver halides has been extensively studied, contributing to the establishment of solid state electrochemistry. Since the sodium sulfur battery employing $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$-conductive $\beta$-alumina ceramics was proposed in the 1960s, the practical importance of solid ionic conductors has been widely noticed. Nowadays, as also emerges from other chapters in this handbook, these materials are indispensable for many kinds of electrochemical devices such as sensors, high-temperature fuel cells, etc. In this chapter a survey is given of different types of solid electrolytes.

## II. OXIDE ION CONDUCTORS

Up to their melting points $\mathrm{CeO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{ThO}_{2}$ adopt the cubic fluorite ( $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ ) type of structure which can be derived from the CsCl structure by removing its cations alternately. Thus the packing of oxygens in these compounds is not close and there are 8-coordinated cubic interstices. Probably due to such structural looseness, they easily form solid solutions with alkaline earth or rare earth oxides in an unusually wide range of composition. In the case of the $\mathrm{CeO}_{2}-\mathrm{Gd}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ system, for example, the range for a single fluorite-type phase extends to a composition of $\mathrm{Gd} /(\mathrm{Ce}+\mathrm{Gd})=0.5$ (in molar ratio). Pure $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$ has three polymorphs as a function of temperature: a monoclinic form ( $<1100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), a tetragonal form with a distorted

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-193.jpg?height=1001&width=689&top_left_y=155&top_left_x=434)
FIGURE 6.1 Conductivity ( $\sigma$ ) of some oxide ion conductors plotted against 1/T. (Adapted from Arai, H., Bull. Ceram. Soc. Jpn., 1992, 27, 100. With permission.)

1. $\left(\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}\right)_{0.9}\left(\mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)_{0.1}$
2. $\left(\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}\right)_{0.87}(\mathrm{CaO})_{0.13}$
3. $\left(\mathrm{ThO}_{2}\right)_{0.93}\left(\mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)_{0.07}$
4. $\left(\mathrm{CeO}_{2}\right)_{0.8}\left(\mathrm{GdO}_{15}\right)_{0.2}$
5. $\left(\mathrm{CeO}_{2}\right)_{0.8}\left(\mathrm{SmO}_{15}\right)_{0.2}$
6. $\left(\mathrm{HfO}_{2}\right)_{0.88}(\mathrm{CaO})_{0.12}$
7. $\left(\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)_{0.75}\left(\mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)_{0.25}$
8. $\left(\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)_{0.75}\left(\mathrm{WO}_{3}\right)_{0.25}$
fluorite structure ( $<2400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), and a cubic form at higher temperature. However, incorporation of 15 to $25 \mathrm{~mol} \%$ of CaO gives a cubic fluorite-type solid solution which is structurally stable up to its melting point and is thus called stabilized zirconia. Similar stabilized cubic phases are also formed with $\mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ or other rare earth oxides.

In these solid solutions aliovalent guest cations such as $\mathrm{Ca}^{2+}$ or $\mathrm{Y}^{3+}$ reside on the sites for host cations ( $\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}$ ), generating oxide ion vacancies ( $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{\bullet \bullet}$ ) for electrical neutrality. Therefore, they can be formulated, for instance, as $\mathrm{Zr}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Ca}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{O}_{2-\mathrm{x}}$ and $\mathrm{Zr}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{O}_{2-\mathrm{x} / 2}$, containing x and $\mathrm{x} / 2$ mole of $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{\bullet \bullet}$ per formula unit, respectively. High oxide ion conductivity is found at high temperature, as shown in Figure 6.1. ${ }^{1}$ The latter is due to a highly defective anion sublattice and, in addition, its loose structure is far from close packed. As shown in Figure 6.2, ${ }^{2}$ the conductivity is accordingly increased with increasing the concentration of aliovalent guest cations at the initial stage of doping. However, further doping decreases conductivity after a relatively sharp maximum. This phenomenon, i.e., leveling off of the ionic conductivity, is often explained as due to the formation of defect pairs according to

$$
V_{O}^{\bullet \bullet}+C a_{Z r}^{\prime \prime}=\left(C a_{Z r}-V_{O}\right)^{x}
$$

which cannot contribute to electric conduction because they are effectively neutral. A drastic decrease in the heavily doped region may be interpreted as caused by ordering of vacancies or formation of a superstructure in the anion sublattice, equivalent to the arrangement in the

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-194.jpg?height=1046&width=765&top_left_y=164&top_left_x=396)
FIGURE 6.2 Composition dependency of conductivity ( $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) of solid solutions in the $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}-\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ (or -MO) system. (Adapted from Tannenberger, H., Proc. J. Int. Etude Piles Combust., 1965, 19.)

C-type rare earth oxide like $\mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$. The conductivity of $\mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is orders of magnitude lower than that of the stabilized zirconia phases. In fact, there is a compound $\mathrm{CaZr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$, resembling the C-type oxide in structure, and calcia-stabilized zirconia (CSZ) tends to disproportionate very slowly into a $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$ phase, and this compound at high temperature. ${ }^{3}$

The ionic conductivity of $\mathrm{CeO}_{2}$-based solid solutions such as $\mathrm{Ce}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Gd}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{O}_{2-\mathrm{x} / 2}$ is remarkably higher than that of CSZ and yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ), as shown in Figure 6.1. However, their ionic transference number $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{O}^{2-}}$ starts to decrease at relatively high oxygen partial pressure $\left(\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}\right)$, because $\mathrm{Ce}^{4+}$ is reduced much more easily than $\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}$, generating conduction electrons by the following defect reaction:

$$
O_{O}^{x}=1 / 2 O_{2}+V_{O}^{\bullet \bullet}+2 e^{\prime}
$$

Applying the law of mass action to this reaction, the electron concentration in equilibrium is given by

$$
\left[e^{\prime}\right]=k(T) P_{O_{2}}^{-1 / 4}
$$

where $\mathrm{k}(\mathrm{T})$ is a parameter depending solely on the temperature, because [ $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{\bullet \bullet}$ ] can be regarded as substantially unchanged with reduction for doped $\mathrm{CeO}_{2}$ in which a great number of vacancies are already generated by the dopant addition. In such a case, the ionic conductivity is independent of $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ for the same reason, and the ionic transference number $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{O}^{2-}}\left(=\sigma_{\mathrm{i}} /\left(\sigma_{\mathrm{i}}+\right.\right. \sigma_{\mathrm{e}}$ )) may be expressed in a form,

TABLE 6.1
$\mathrm{Po}_{2}{ }^{*}$ (Pressure at which $\boldsymbol{t}_{0^{2-}}=\mathbf{1} / \mathbf{2}$ ) of Mixed Conductors Based on CeO
| Composition | $P_{O_{2}}{ }^{*}(\mathrm{~atm})$ |  |  |  |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
|  | $\mathbf{5 0 0}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}} \mathbf{C}$ | $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ | $850^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ | $\mathbf{1 , 0 0 0}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}} \mathrm{C}$ |
| $\mathrm{Ce}_{0.9} \mathrm{Ca}_{0.1} \mathrm{O}_{1.9}$ | $10^{-20}$ | $-10^{-13}$ | $10^{-9}$ | - |
| $\mathrm{Ce}_{0.905} \mathrm{Y}_{0.095} \mathrm{O}_{1.95}$ | $10^{-26}$ | $10^{-17}$ | $10^{-13}$ | $10^{-10}$ |
| $\mathrm{Ce}_{0.9} \mathrm{Gd}_{0.1} \mathrm{O}_{1.95}$ | - | $1.2 \times 10^{-19}$ | $1.7 \times 10^{-15}$ | - |
| $\mathrm{Ce}_{0.5} \mathrm{Gd}_{0.5} \mathrm{O}_{1.75}$ | - | $1.5 \times 10^{-16}$ | $6.5 \times 10^{-14}$ | - |
| (CSZ) |  | $-10^{-34}$ | $-10^{-30}$ | $-10^{-26}$ |
| $t_{O^{2-}}=1+\left(P_{O_{2}} / P_{O_{2}}^{*}\right)^{-1 / 4-1}$ |  |  |  |  |


where $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{*}$ is a parameter associated with $\mathrm{k}(\mathrm{T})$. As is evident from Equation (6.4), $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{*}$ at a given temperature corresponds to the oxygen partial pressure at which $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{O}^{2-}}=1 / 2$. As given in Table 6.1, ${ }^{4}$ the value of $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{*}$ for $\mathrm{CeO}_{2}$-based solid solutions at $850^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is higher than $10^{-15} \mathrm{~atm}$, while that for CSZ is estimated to be as low as $10^{-30} \mathrm{~atm}$. This is the reason why CSZ and YSZ are more useful for fuel cell application than the more conductive $\mathrm{CeO}_{2}$-based solid electrolytes. The latter are useful, for example, as electrocatalysts, in which mixed conduction plays an essential role. ${ }^{5}$

Bismuth sesquioxide, $\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, exhibits a high oxide ion conductivity at high temperature without doping of aliovalent cations. The oxide transforms from the monoclinic $\alpha$ form to the cubic $\delta$ form at $729^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. This transition is accompanied by a jump of conductivity from $\sim 10^{-3}$ to $\sim 1 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$. The $\delta$ phase has a structure related to fluorite, but with a complicated disorder in the oxygen sublattice. It is a kind of statistical distribution of oxygen vacancies which gives rise to the high ionic conductivity of $\delta-\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} .{ }^{6}$ This highly conductive phase can be stabilized to lower temperatures by incorporation with $\mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{Gd}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{WO}_{3}$, etc., as shown in Figure 6.1. Of these $\left(\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)_{0.75}\left(\mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)_{0.25}$ exhibits an oxide ion conductivity as high as $\sim 10^{-2} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at a temperature as low as $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Unfortunately, these materials undergo reduction as easily as $\mathrm{CeO}_{2}$-based solid electrolytes, and their ionic transference number is decreased rapidly in reducing atmospheres. An oxygen pressure higher than $10^{-13} \mathrm{~atm}$ is needed for $\left(\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)_{0.75}\left(\mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)_{0.25}$ to maintain $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{O}^{2-}}>0.99$ at $600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

Some $\mathrm{ABO}_{3}$ compounds with the perovskite-type structure exhibit relatively high oxide ion conductivity or diffusivity due to their oxygen deficient nonstoichiometry. They are built of a $\mathrm{ReO}_{3}$-type framework, in which a larger A ion is accommodated in an interstice coordinated by 12 oxygens while a smaller B ion resides on a 6 -coordinated Re-site. This arrangement is structurally very stable so that a large part of the A and/or B ions can be replaced by various aliovalent ions without a change in the basic structure, and thus a large number of oxygen vacancies can be generated.

The ionic conductivity of perovskite ( $\mathrm{CaTiO}_{3}$ ) itself has been reported ${ }^{7}$ to be about $10^{-3} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, which is lower than that of fluorite-type solid solutions like YSZ, but much higher than oxides based on close-packed oxygen arrays. A nonstoichiometric, but isostructural compound, $\mathrm{CaTi}_{0.7} \mathrm{Al}_{0.3} \mathrm{O}_{2.85}$, formed by substituting Al for Ti in the mother perovskite, shows a high conductivity almost comparable to CSZ. ${ }^{8}$ As reported previously by Takahashi and Iwahara, ${ }^{9}$ a series of perovskite-type compounds based on $\mathrm{LaAlO}_{3}$, in which oxygen vacancies are generated by the replacement of its A ion (La) with an aliovalent cation like Ba, also show a high ionic conductivity. This was probably the first report about
perovskite-type oxide ion conductors. Recently, it has been found that a similar type of compound, $\mathrm{Nd}_{0.9} \mathrm{Ca}_{0.1} \mathrm{Al}_{0.5} \mathrm{Ga}_{0.5} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, exhibits a conductivity as high as $0.1 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C} .^{10}$ It is interesting to note that compounds like $\mathrm{BaCe}_{0.9} \mathrm{Nd}_{0.1} \mathrm{O}_{3-\delta}$, known as a proton conductor, turn into an oxide ion conductor under a certain condition at elevated temperature ( $\sim 1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ).

Some perovskite-type oxides composed of lanthanide elements (as an A-site cation) and transition metals (B-site) show relatively fast oxide ion transport properties, though their electronic conductivities are very high (often even metallic). Electrochemically measured chemical diffusion coefficients of oxide ions in $\mathrm{Nd}_{0.8} \mathrm{Sr}_{0.2} \mathrm{CoO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{La}_{0.8} \mathrm{Sr}_{0.2} \mathrm{CoO}_{3}$ are $\sim 10^{-11} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}^{11}$ and $\sim 10^{-13}$ at $76^{\circ} \mathrm{C},{ }^{12}$ respectively. It has also been reported that $\mathrm{La}_{0.79} \mathrm{Sr}_{0.2} \mathrm{MnO}_{3}$, which is used as an interconnector material for solid oxide fuel cells, shows a chemical diffusion coefficient in the range of $10^{-8}$ to $10^{-6} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ between 700 and $860^{\circ} \mathrm{C} .^{13}$ For that purpose, such a high diffusivity of oxide ions is considered to be a problem. Mixed conduction of these perovskites is discussed in more detail in Chapters 7 and 14 of this handbook.

The crystal structure of brownmillerite ( $\mathrm{Ca}_{2} \mathrm{AlFeO}_{5}$ ) is closely related to that of perovskite, being an oxygen-deficient perovskite structure with vacancies ordered along the [101] direction. Some compounds with this structure have been investigated as an oxide ion conductor. However, they do not show a high conductivity until they experience a transition from a vacancy ordered state to a statistically disordered state. For example, $\mathrm{Ba}_{2} \mathrm{In}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ shows a conductivity jump of about 2 orders of magnitude at the transition temperature $\left(930^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right) .{ }^{14}$ The ionic conductivity after transition is as large as $0.1 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}-1$. On the other hand, the conductivity of $\mathrm{Sr}_{2} \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ at $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is as low as that of stoichiometric perovskite compounds, because its oxygen sublattice is not completely disordered up to this temperature. ${ }^{7}$ It is interesting to note that $\mathrm{BaBi}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{3} \mathrm{MO}_{14.5}(\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{Ga}$, In, and Sc$)$ compounds that consist of intergrowths between the Aurivillius phase ( $\mathrm{Bi}_{4} \mathrm{Ti}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ ) and brown millerite layers of $\mathrm{BaMO}_{2.5}$ show a high conductivity ( $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}-1$ at $900^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) after a similar order-disorder transition. ${ }^{15}$

## III. FLUORIDE ION CONDUCTORS

In general, the fluoride ion is thought to be more mobile in the solid state than the oxide ion because it is monovalent and its radius is smaller. Indeed, a fluorite-type solid solution of fluorite ( $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ ) itself with $1 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{NaF}$ shows a $\mathrm{F}^{-}$conductivity of $2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $350^{\circ} \mathrm{C},{ }^{16}$ which is far higher than the $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ conductivity of CSZ at the same temperature. It is noted that, as shown in Figure 6.3, ${ }^{17}$ even pure $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ exhibits considerable conductivity.

The $\beta$ form of $\mathrm{PbF}_{2}$, also having the fluorite structure and consisting of cations ( Pb ) with high polarizability, exhibits a much higher conductivity ( $\sim 1 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) than $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$. As shown in Figure 6.4, ${ }^{18} \beta$ - $\mathrm{PbF}_{2}$ undergoes a diffuse transition from a low- to a high-conductive phase at around $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the conductivity of the latter being almost the same as that of its molten phase. During this transition its basic crystal structure is unchanged. According to neutron diffraction, ${ }^{19}$ there is a large number of anti-Frenkel pairs in $\beta-\mathrm{PbF}_{2}$ at low temperature. It is therefore believed that the conduction in the low-conductive phase is due to $\mathrm{F}^{-}$hopping via such defects. On the other hand, the "super ionic" behavior in the highconductive phase is interpreted as due to a semifused state of the $\mathrm{F}^{-}$sublattice associated with large anharmonic thermal vibrations along the [111] direction. ${ }^{20}$ In fact, a specific heat anomaly accompanying the transition has also been observed.

A variety of mixed fluorides with fluorite-type or related structures have been derived from $\mathrm{PbF}_{2}$. Of these, $\mathrm{PbSnF}_{4}$ is the best $\mathrm{F}^{-}$conductor to date in a relatively low-temperature region. As shown in Figure 6.3, its conductivity shows a kink around $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, which was previously thought to be due to a structural transition. Recently, however, it has been attributed to the transition in the conduction mechanism as in the case of $\mathrm{PbF}_{2}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-197.jpg?height=1121&width=898&top_left_y=155&top_left_x=333)
FIGURE 6.3 Conductivity of some fluoride ion conductors as a function of $1 / \mathrm{T}$. (Adapted from Kawamoto, Y., Bull. Ceram. Soc. Jpn., 1992, 27, 105. With permission.)

In contrast with their oxide counterparts, $\mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{PbF}_{2}$ can form fluorite-type solid solutions with fluorides of higher-valency metals such as $\mathrm{YF}_{3}, \mathrm{BiF}_{3}, \mathrm{UF}_{4}$, etc. They are expressed, for example, by $\mathrm{Ca}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{F}_{2+\mathrm{x}}$, in which the excess $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ions are accommodated in the interstitial sites, forming various kinds of clusters with $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{F}}^{*}$, depending on the concentration of $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{i}}{ }^{\prime}$. They are usually good $\mathrm{F}^{-}$solid electrolytes, and the conductivity of $\mathrm{Pb}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Bi}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{F}_{2+\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{x}=$ 0.25 ) is shown in Figure 6.3 as an example.

Another class of crystalline $\mathrm{F}^{-}$conductors consists of the rare earth trifluorides with the tysonite-type structure as represented by $\mathrm{LaF}_{3}$, which exhibits a conductivity of $3 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ already at room temperature. ${ }^{21}$ In its trigonal structure, there are three crystallographically nonequivalent $\mathrm{F}^{-}$sites in a ratio of $12: 4: 2$. ${ }^{22}$ However, according to ${ }^{19} \mathrm{~F}$ NMR, ${ }^{23}$ two of these sites are equivalent for ionic motion; consequently two kinds of sites exist in a ratio of 12:6. At low temperature, $\mathrm{F}^{-}$diffusion takes place via Schottky-type defects generated in minority sites. At elevated temperatures, however, hopping between the two sites also takes place. Tysonite-type solid solutions are formed with divalent metal fluorides such as $\mathrm{BaF}_{2}$ or $\mathrm{EuF}_{2} ; \mathrm{La}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Eu}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{F}_{3-\mathrm{x}}$, for example, has anion vacancies. The conductivity of these phases is higher than that of pure $\mathrm{LaF}_{3}$ as shown in Figure 6.3.

Apart from crystalline phases, various glassy solid $\mathrm{F}^{-}$conductors have also been developed. A glass in the $\mathrm{ZrF}_{4}-\mathrm{BaF}_{2}$ system, the first example of such conductors, exhibits a conductivity of $\sim 10^{-6} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. ${ }^{24}$ Extensive studies have been performed with this system, including short-range structural analyses, ${ }^{19} \mathrm{~F}$ NMR, simulation based on molecular dynamics, etc. According to the results, there are basically two kinds of fluorine ions, i.e., a

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-198.jpg?height=868&width=654&top_left_y=159&top_left_x=453)
FIGURE 6.4 Conductivity of $\beta-\mathrm{PbF}_{2}$ as a function of 1/T. (Adapted from Benz, R., Z. Phys. Chem., N.F., 1975, 95, 25. With permission.)

bridging fluorine (forming a bonding $\mathrm{Zr}-\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{Zr}$ ) and a nonbridging one ( $\mathrm{Zr}-\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{Ba}$ ). The positional exchange takes place much easier between the latter fluorine-ions. ${ }^{25,26}$ It is therefore believed that $\mathrm{F}^{-}$can migrate in the glassy structure through channels connecting positions of the nonbridging fluorine.

Recently, high $\mathrm{F}^{-}$conductivities have been reported for glass phases based on $\mathrm{GaF}_{3}, \mathrm{FeF}_{3}$, and $\mathrm{InF}_{3}$. Of these, $35 \mathrm{InF}_{3} \cdot 30 \mathrm{SnF}_{2} \cdot 35 \mathrm{PbF}_{2}$ shows the highest conductivity to date, $\sim 10^{-3} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, which is higher than that of $\beta-\mathrm{PbF}_{2}$ at the same temperature. ${ }^{27}$ Some oxyfluoride glasses, for example, the composition $45 \mathrm{PbF}_{2} \cdot 45 \mathrm{MnF}_{2} \cdot 10 \mathrm{SiO}_{2}$, also show good conductivity of $\sim 10^{-6} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C} .^{28,29}$

## IV. SILVER AND COPPER ION CONDUCTORS

Sodium chloride-type crystals of AgCl and AgBr show notable conductivity at relatively low temperature due to transport of $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$via Frenkel defects. In a pure sample, conduction takes place mainly by migration or diffusion of interstitial $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$( $\mathrm{Ag}_{\mathrm{i}}$ ), because its mobility is much higher than that of vacancies ( $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{Ag}}^{\prime}$ ). According to measurements of the correlation factor $\mathrm{f}\left(=\mathrm{D}_{\mathrm{T}} / \mathrm{D}\right)$, ${ }^{30}$ this is not by the direct interstitial mechanism, but the indirect one (interstitialcy mechanism) involving collinear jumps of interstitial and lattice silver ions. When doped with a small amount of aliovalent halides such as $\mathrm{CdBr}_{2}$, they decrease in conductivity because of the resulting shift (decrease) of the equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{Ag}_{\mathrm{i}}$. Heavier doping increases the conductivity as the vacancy diffusion mechanism becomes more important than the interstitialcy mechanism.

Silver iodide, AgI , adopts the wurtzite-type structure at room temperature. This $\beta$ form of AgI transforms into the $\alpha$ form (bcc) at $147^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ with a jump of conductivity over more than 3 orders of magnitude, as given in Figure 6.5. The silver ion conductivity after the transformation is $\sim 1 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$, which is almost the same as that of the molten phase (m.p. $552^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) and even comparable to an $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ solution. Cuprous iodide, CuI , undergoes a similar

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-199.jpg?height=912&width=898&top_left_y=155&top_left_x=331)
FIGURE 6.5 Temperature dependency of conductivity for typical silver ion conductors, AgI and $\mathrm{RbAg}_{4} \mathrm{I}_{5}$, as compared with those for CSZ ( $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ) and an aqueous solution of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)$.

transition at $430^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and turns into a very good $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}$conductor. A common feature of both compounds is that they are composed of extremely polarizable ions.

The ionic conduction of $\alpha-\mathrm{AgI}$ (or CuI ) is quite different from that of AgBr or of CSZ . In the latter phases conduction is generated by point defects, whereas in the former it is due to partial occupation of crystallographic sites, which is sometimes called "structural disorder." Thus ionic conductivity is enabled without use of aliovalent dopants. Strock's ${ }^{31}$ model of $\alpha-\mathrm{AgI}$ proposed in 1934 is that the unit cubic cell (a $=5.0 \AA, \mathrm{z}=2$, space group: Im 3 m ) is constructed by the bcc arrangement of the $\mathrm{I}^{-}$sublattice and two $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ions are statistically distributed over 42 sites (i.e., 6b, 12d, and 24h) among anions (average structure model). Recent studies revealed that $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ions are normally situated on the tetrahedrally coordinated 12 d sites with a large amplitude anharmonic vibration, tending to move toward the adjacent 12d position via the 24 h intermediate sites. Such a structural situation in $\alpha-\mathrm{AgI}$ could be taken as a "half-fused" state where only the cation sublattice loses its crystallographic order. Indeed, AgI and CuI show extraordinarily large entropy changes when they transform from the $\beta$ - into the $\alpha$ form, as shown in Table 6.2, which are comparable to or even larger than those for melting. ${ }^{32}$ Very high cationic conductivity in the $\alpha$ (high-temperature) forms of $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}, \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{Se}, \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{HgI}_{4}, \mathrm{CuBr}$, etc., is also explained as due to the average structure of the cation sublattice.

In 1961, $\mathrm{Ag}_{3} \mathrm{SI}$ was found to have a very high ionic conductivity ( $0.01 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ ) at room temperature. ${ }^{33}$ After this finding, various room-temperature silver and copper ion conductors have been developed based on either AgI or CuI . A typical example is $\mathrm{RbAg}_{4} \mathrm{I}_{5}(\mathrm{RbI} \cdot 4 \mathrm{AgI}),{ }^{34}$ which shows a conductivity as high as diluted $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$, as shown in Figure 6.5. According to single-crystal X-ray analysis, the compound has a complex structure called the $\beta$-Mn type (cubic, a = $11.2 \AA, \mathrm{z}=4$ ), in which $20 \mathrm{I}^{-}$ions form 56 tetrahedra sharing each face and in which $16 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ions are statistically distributed. ${ }^{35}$ Silver ions can migrate easily to adjacent vacant tetrahedral sites through a common face of the tetrahedra. This situation is therefore similar to $\alpha-\mathrm{AgI}$ in that the structure is composed of face-sharing coordination tetrahedra

Data from O'Keefe, M. and Hyde, B.G., Philos. Mag., 1976, 33, 219.
and the tetrahedral sites thus formed are only partially occupied by $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ions. Thermodynamically, $\mathrm{RbAg}_{4} \mathrm{I}_{5}$ is metastable below $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, tending to disproportionate into $\mathrm{Rb}_{2} \mathrm{AgI}_{3}$ and AgI , though the rate is very low. A copper version of this compound has been synthesized by substituting partially Cl for I , i.e., $\mathrm{Rb}_{4} \mathrm{Cu}_{16} \mathrm{I}_{7} \mathrm{Cl}_{13},{ }^{36}$ exhibiting a conductivity higher than $\mathrm{RbAg}_{4} \mathrm{I}_{5}$ above $\sim 50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, as shown in Figure 6.6. Room-temperature conductivities of various silver and copper ion conductors are summarized in Table 6.3. ${ }^{37}$
$\Delta S_{\mathrm{t}}$ and $\Delta S_{\mathrm{m}}$ ) for AgI

Some glass phases based on AgI also show a high $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$conduction at room temperature. For example, a glass with the composition $3 \mathrm{AgI} \cdot \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{MoO}_{4}$ obtained by quenching a molten mixture of AgI and $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{MoO}_{4}$ exhibits a conductivity of $0.02 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ with the $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ transference number being unity. ${ }^{48}$ Although details of the mechanism are not completely understood, the glassy structure itself is held responsible for the high conductivity because most of these compounds show a decrease in conductivity by 3 orders of magnitude when crystallized at high temperature. ${ }^{49}$ Conductivities of glassy silver conductors are listed in Table 6.4. ${ }^{50}$

## V. SODIUM AND POTASSIUM ION CONDUCTORS

The $\beta$-alumina family, a series of compounds in the $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ system, is one of the most important groups of solid electrolytes not only because it is practically useful for advanced batteries, but also because it is a typical two-dimensional ionic conductor, from which or by analogy with which, various kinds of solid electrolyte materials can be derived.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-201.jpg?height=717&width=718&top_left_y=155&top_left_x=422)
FIGURE 6.6 Conductivity of $\mathrm{RbAg}_{4} \mathrm{I}_{5}$ and $\mathrm{Rb}_{4} \mathrm{Cu}_{16} \mathrm{I}_{7} \mathrm{Cl}_{13}$ as a function of $1 / \mathrm{T}$. (Adapted from Yamamoto, O., Bull. Ceram. Soc. Jpn., 1992, 27, 128. With permission.)

TABLE 6.4
Conductivity and Transference Number of Typical Ag ${ }^{+}$Conductive Glasses
| Composition | $\sigma_{\mathrm{Ag}}+\left(\mathbf{2 5}^{\circ} \mathbf{C}\right) / \Omega^{-\mathbf{1}} \mathbf{c m}^{-\mathbf{1}}$ | $\boldsymbol{t}_{\text {ion }}$ | Ref. |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $4 \mathrm{AgI}-\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ | $2.2 \times 10^{-2}$ | 1.0 | 49 |
| $3 \mathrm{AgI}-\mathrm{Ag}_{4} \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$ | $4 \times 10^{-3}$ | $\sim 1.0$ | 51 |
| $3 \mathrm{AgI}-\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{MoO}_{4}$ | $2.1 \times 10^{-2}$ | 1.00 | 40 |
| $3 \mathrm{AgI}-\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{WO}_{4}$ | $3.6 \times 10^{-2}$ | 1.00 | 40 |
| $3 \mathrm{AgI}-\mathrm{Ag}_{4} \mathrm{SiO}_{4}$ | $5.4 \times 10^{-3}$ | - | 40 |
| $6 \mathrm{AgI}-3 \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ | $5.5 \times 10^{-3}$ | - | 40 |
| $6 \mathrm{AgI}-3 \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ | $\left(8.5 \times 10^{-3}\right)$ | 1.00 | 52 |


The principal members are the $\beta$ and $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ phase, the ideal compositions of which are $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O} \cdot 11 \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\left(\mathrm{NaAl}_{11} \mathrm{O}_{17}\right)$ and $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O} \cdot 5 \cdot 33 \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, though they are usually nonstoichiometric. For $\beta$-alumina this is generally represented by $\mathrm{Na}_{1+\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Al}_{11} \mathrm{O}_{17+\mathrm{x} / 2}$, where x is typically about 0.2. On the other hand, the $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ phase in the binary $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ system is unstable, and incorporation of aliovalent cations such as $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$ is needed for stabilization; then, the composition is represented, for example, by $\mathrm{Na}_{1+\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Mg}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Al}_{11-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{O}_{17}$, where x is typically $2 / 3$.

The crystal structure of $\beta$-alumina was solved in 1937, ${ }^{53} 30$ years before the discovery of its high $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$conductivity in 1967. ${ }^{54,55}$ It is based on a hexagonal unit cell ( $\mathrm{a}=5.58 \AA, \mathrm{c}= 22.5 \AA$, space group: $\mathrm{P}_{3} / \mathrm{mmc}$ ), where the $\mathrm{Al}_{11} \mathrm{O}_{16}$ blocks in a spinel-like arrangement (spinel block) and the $\mathrm{NaO}^{+}$layers are stacked alternately along the c-axis. The $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ structure (a = $5.59 \AA, \mathrm{c}=33.95 \AA, \mathrm{z}=3$, space group: R 3 m ) is constructed of a similar alternate stacking of the spinel blocks and NaO layers, but the sequences of the oxygen layers in the spinel block are different. ${ }^{56,57}$ In both phases, migration of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$takes place in the NaO layers, which is often expressed by a honeycomb arrangement, as shown in Figure 6.7. The sites marked with BR and $\mathrm{a}-\mathrm{BR}$ are nonequivalent for the $\beta$ phase due to the difference in their distances from oxygens in the spinel block, and its sodium ions are normally situated on the former. However, the excess $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions usually present in this phase are located on the mO (mid-

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-202.jpg?height=454&width=602&top_left_y=155&top_left_x=479)
FIGURE 6.7 Structure of the ( NaO ) layer in $\beta$-alumina and migration paths for sodium ions. Normally, $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$is situated on the BR site.

oxygen) sites with pairing with a $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$relocated from a BR site. It is believed that diffusion of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$takes place in a manner such that a mO- $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$moves to the adjacent mO site via a-BR, to generate a new pair with a $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$pulled out from the BR. This is a kind of interstitialcy diffusion, the correlation factor for which has been calculated to be $3 / 5$, agreeing with the observed value of $\mathrm{f}=0.61$ for Ag-substituted $\beta$-alumina. ${ }^{58}$ The existence of ion pairs has also been confirmed by quasielastic light-scattering measurement. ${ }^{59}$ In contrast with the $\beta$ phase, the two kinds of sites ( $B R$ and a-BR) in the $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ phase are equivalent and $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions statistically occupy these sites. Diffusion for this case is believed to be due to the vacancy mechanism. ${ }^{60}$ Nevertheless, unusually fast ionic motion in this phase cannot be interpreted by a simple hopping model, as in the case of CSZ.

Conductivity of a typical single crystal of $\beta$-alumina is as high as $\sim 0.01 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ along the conduction plane (perpendicular to the c-axis). ${ }^{61}$ It is anisotropic because of the two-dimensional nature of the structure and several orders of magnitude lower along the c-axis. The $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ phase exhibits a conductivity 1 order of magnitude higher than the $\beta$ phase. Sintered polycrystalline $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ phase, often used for the Na/S batteries, shows a poorer conductivity at room temperature, but its conductivity reaches a level high enough for practical use at high temperature ( $\sim 300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), as shown in Figure 6.8. ${ }^{62}$

Sodium ions in either the $\beta$ or the $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ phase can be easily exchanged for various cations because of fast ionic diffusion in the layer. Ion exchange is usually carried out by immersing sample crystals in a fused salt of the cation to replace $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$. For example, $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$in $\beta$-alumina is totally replaced by $\mathrm{K}^{+}$in fused $\mathrm{KNO}_{3}$ at 300 to $350^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$; K - $\beta$-alumina thus obtained shows a $\mathrm{K}^{+}$conductivity of $6.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. A variety of ionic conductors in the $\beta$-alumina family have been similarly obtained, as listed in Table 6.5. It is interesting to note that the conductivities of the mother compound and its Ag derivative ( Ag - $\beta$-alumina) are much higher than those of other derivatives of monovalent cations. This is explained by the barrier for diffusion having a minimum at an ionic radius near that of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$or $\mathrm{Ag}^{+} .{ }^{60}$ A positive pressure effect on the conductivity has been observed for K - $\beta$-alumina, ${ }^{67}$ as the radius of K is too large for the spacing of the conduction layer.

In the $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ phase, trivalent cations like $\mathrm{Eu}^{3+}$ can also be substituted for $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$. For instance, when a slab of this crystal is heated in $\mathrm{EuCl}_{3}$ powder for 24 h , almost all the Na is replaced by Eu. ${ }^{68}$ This vapor-phase ion exchange utilizing fast ion transport is a typical example of materials synthesis by "soft chemistry." Unfortunately, rare earth $\beta$ "-aluminas are not good ionic conductors, but they are promising candidates as crystals for solid state lasers. ${ }^{69}$

As $\mathrm{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ and $\boldsymbol{\alpha}-\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ are isostructural with $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, they form Ga- (or Fe-) versions of $\beta$ - and $\beta^{\prime \prime}$-alumina. The ionic conductivity of $\mathrm{Na}-\beta^{\prime \prime}-\mathrm{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is reported to be $6 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C},{ }^{70}$ which is somewhat higher than that of its Al counterpart. However, compounds with $\beta$ - $\mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ are mixed conducting. ${ }^{71}$ Utilizing the volatile nature of $\mathrm{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$-based compounds, a thin film form of Na- $\beta-\mathrm{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ has been obtained by sputtering a mixture of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}-4 \mathrm{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-203.jpg?height=818&width=635&top_left_y=155&top_left_x=462)
FIGURE 6.8 Conductivity of NASICON and sintered $\boldsymbol{\beta}^{\prime \prime}$-alumina as a function of $1 /$ T. (Adapted from Clearfield, A., Subramanian, M.A., Wang, W., and Jerus, P., Solid State Ionics, 1983, 9/10, 895. With permission.)

TABLE 6.5
Conductivity of Various $\quad \boldsymbol{\beta}$ (or $\boldsymbol{\beta}^{\prime \prime}$ )- $\mathbf{A l}_{2} \mathbf{O}_{3}$ Single Crystals (in conduction plane)
| Incorporated cation | Ionic radius of cation( $\boldsymbol{\AA}$ ) | $\sigma_{\mathrm{i}}\left(\Omega^{-1} \mathbf{c m}^{-1}, 25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right.$ or $\left.200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ | Ref. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ | 0.95 | $1.4 \times 10^{-2}\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ | 61 |
| $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ | 0.60 | $1.3 \times 10^{-4}\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ | 63 |
| $\mathrm{K}^{+}$ | 1.33 | $6.5 \times 10^{-5}\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ | 63 |
| $\mathrm{Rb}^{+}$ | 1.48 | $\left(1.2 \times 10^{-6}\right)\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ | 63 |
| $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ | 1.26 | $0.67 \times 10^{-2}\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ | 61 |
| $\mathrm{TI}^{+}$ | 1.49 | $2 \times 10^{-6}\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ | 63 |
| $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$ | 1.40 | $5 \times 10^{-3}\left(25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ | 64 |
| $\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}$ | 1.35 | $2 \times 10^{-5}\left(200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ | 65 |
| $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}$ | 1.32 | $6 \times 10^{-2}\left(200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ | 66 |
| $\mathrm{Gd}^{3+}$ | 1.11 | $4 \times 10^{-9}\left(200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ | 65 |


and $\mathrm{NaGaO}_{2}$ under an $\mathrm{Ar} / \mathrm{O}_{2}$ atmosphere and subsequent annealing at $1100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, exhibiting a $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$conductivity of $\sim 10^{-2} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C} .^{72}$ In addition, $\beta-\mathrm{Ga}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is useful as a host structure for proton conductors (see Section VII).

The NASICON (Na super ionic conductor) family is another important series of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ conductors. Original NASICON, discovered in 1976 through careful structural examination of oxyacid salt frameworks with three-dimensional tunnels, ${ }^{73}$ is a solid solution in the $\mathrm{NaZr}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}-\mathrm{Na}_{4} \mathrm{Zr}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SiO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ system and can be represented by $(1-\mathrm{x} / 3) \mathrm{NaZr}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3} \cdot(\mathrm{x} / 3) \mathrm{Na}_{4} \mathrm{Zr}_{2}\left(\mathrm{SiO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ or $\mathrm{Na}_{1+\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Zr}_{2} \mathrm{P}_{3-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Si}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{O}_{12}$. A great variety of modified NASICONs have been synthesized by replacing Zr with, for example, $\mathrm{Co}, \mathrm{Ti}, \mathrm{V}$, and/or by substituting As or Ge for P or Si .

One of the end members, $\mathrm{NaZr}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}$, itself has a framework structure provided with three-dimensional channels suitable for ionic conduction. However, its conductivity is not excellent ( $\sim 10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) because one of the two kinds of Na sites ( Na 1 and Na 2 ) along the conduction channel is preferentially occupied with $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions and all are fixed at Na1. The structure of NASICON, rhombohedral at high temperature and monoclinic at low

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-204.jpg?height=530&width=708&top_left_y=152&top_left_x=427)
FIGURE 6.9 Frequency dependency of the real part of the complex conductivity measured for some priderite compounds, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{Ti})_{8} \mathrm{O}_{16} .(\mathrm{V}): \mathrm{B}=\mathrm{Mg} ;(\mathrm{M}): \mathrm{Al} ;(\mathrm{n}): 0.4 \mathrm{Mg}+0.6 \mathrm{Al}$. (Adapted from Doyama, Y., Funatani, H., Matsui, N., Yoshikado, S., and Taniguchi, I., Proc. 18th Symp. on Solid State Ionics in Japan, Fukuoka, 1992, 114. With permission.)

temperature, consists of a similar framework based on corner sharing of a $\mathrm{ZrO}_{6}$ octahedron with a $\mathrm{PO}_{4}$ or $\mathrm{SiO}_{4}$ tetrahedron, in which two kinds of Na sites exist, the Na 2 sites being separated into two nonequivalent sites in the monoclinic form. As x is increased, the $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ ions also partially occupy Na2 sites, giving the compound a better conductivity. It is believed that diffusion takes place due to exchange of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$between Na 1 and Na 2 sites. The maximum conductivity is obtained at $\mathrm{x}=2$, at which composition the activation energy for ionic conduction shows a minimum. Hydrothermally synthesized NASICON, with approximately this composition, shows a conductivity comparable with that of $\beta^{\prime \prime}$-alumina, ${ }^{74}$ as shown in Figure 6.8. A kink in the conductivity is due to the transition from monoclinic to rhombohedral. Though NASICON shows excellent conductivity, there is the problem that they are unstable with respect to liquid sodium.

Compounds in the hollandite family also serve as $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$or $\mathrm{K}^{+}$conductors. Hollandite is a mineral with a composition $\mathrm{Ba}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Mn}_{8} \mathrm{O}_{16}(\mathrm{x} \leq 2)$, in which $\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}$ ions are located in onedimensional tunnels formed along the c-axis of a pseudotetragonal framework structure composed of $\mathrm{MnO}_{6}$ octahedra. Mixed titanates, $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{Ti})_{8} \mathrm{O}_{16}$ (priderite, A : alkaline metal, B : $\mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Al}, \mathrm{Ga}$, etc.), are isostructural and exhibit one-dimensional ionic conduction along the tunnel. For example, a single crystal sample of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{x}}(\mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Ti})_{8} \mathrm{O}_{16}(\mathrm{KMTO})$ shows $\mathrm{K}^{+}$conductivity as high as $0.1 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at 293 K along the c-axis in the very high-frequency region, though it rapidly decreases as the measuring ac frequency is lowered, as shown in Figure 6.9. ${ }^{75}$ Highfrequency conductivity may represent short-range motion of $\mathrm{K}^{+}$between bottlenecks in the tunnel. It is believed that such bottlenecks are generated due to large impurity ions, like Rb, and/or excess oxygens occupying the tunnel sites. Moreover, it is noted that the shortest diameter of the tunnel is about $2.4 \AA$, which is not large enough for $\mathrm{K}^{+}$migration. Analogous compounds, such as $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Ga}_{8} \mathrm{Ga}_{8+\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Ti}_{16-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{O}_{56}$, show a better conductivity because their tunnel is larger in diameter. ${ }^{76}$

Glassy $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$conductors have also been investigated extensively. They are described in the following section together with $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$conductive glasses.

## VI. LITHIUM ION CONDUCTORS

From a practical point of view, $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$-conducting solid electrolytes are especially important because they enable the development of a solid state lithium battery with high energy density; thus, a variety of materials as represented by those shown in Figure $6.10^{77}$ have so far been investigated as a $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$conductor.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-205.jpg?height=994&width=906&top_left_y=155&top_left_x=328)
FIGURE 6.10 Conductivity of some lithium ion conductors as a function of $1 / \mathrm{T}$. (Adapted from Adachi, G. and Aono, H., Bull. Ceram. Soc. Jpn., 1992, 27, 117. With permission.)

The simplest example is LiI, which shows not an excellent, but still considerable conductivity of $\sim 5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at room temperature, while other lithium halides are almost insulators, probably because of the less polarizable nature of their halogen ions. It is noteworthy that LiI was the first practical solid electrolyte used in a $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{I}_{2}$-complex cell in 1972, in which a layer of LiI was formed by contact of the negative and the positive electrodes. The layer was so thin that the resistance was small enough for low current uses, such as in a cardiac pacemaker.

Doping LiI with aliovalent impurities such as $\mathrm{CaI}_{2}$ increases its conductivity for the same reason as described before, the conductivity of $\mathrm{LiI}-1 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{CaI}_{2}$ being $\sim 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C} .{ }^{78}$ Besides such a classical approach, it was found in 1973 that addition of a fine insulator $\left(\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right)$ particles into the LiI matrix as a dispersed second phase enhanced conductivity much more. ${ }^{79}$ Since then, much research has been directed toward this phenomenon. Such a dis-persed-type solid electrolyte (often called "composite electrolyte") is prepared, for example, by heating a well-blended mixture of anhydrous LiI and activated $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ powder with high specific surface areas ( $>100 \mathrm{~m}^{2} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$ ) in a very dry atmosphere at elevated temperature $\left(\sim 600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$. The cooled product is ground and pressed into a green pellet, which is used as a solid electrolyte sample. Typical $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$conductivities of $\mathrm{LiI}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ measured as a function of $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ content are shown in Figure 6.11. ${ }^{80}$ The conductivity increases with increasing $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ content up to about $40 \mathrm{~mol} \%$, at which it reaches a maximum value more than 2 orders of magnitude higher than that of pure LiI at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The beneficial effect of $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ addition becomes smaller at higher temperatures. It has been pointed out in this connection that a plot of conductivity vs. the volume fraction of $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ instead shows a linear relationship before showing a maximum at $30 \% .^{81}$ Decreases in conductivity seen in the higher content region are due to the blocking effect of insulating $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-206.jpg?height=907&width=774&top_left_y=150&top_left_x=394)
FIGURE 6.11 Conductivity isotherms for the $\mathrm{LiI}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ composite system. (Adapted from Poulsen, F., Andersen, N.H., Kindl, B., and Schoonman, J., Solid State Ionics, 1983, 9/10, 119. With permission.)

This conductivity enhancement is not due to the defect generation through classical homogeneous doping (such as $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}=2 \mathrm{Al}_{\mathrm{Li}}^{\prime \prime}+\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{Li}}^{\prime}+3 \mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$ ), since the effect of $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ extends far beyond its solubility limit in LiI . It is instead believed that defects ( $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{Li}}^{\prime}$ and/or $\mathrm{Li}_{\mathrm{i}}$ ) generated by space charge effects at the ionic conductor ( LiI )/insulator ( $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ ) interface are responsible. Therefore, the finer the particle size of the dispersed insulator, the greater the enhancement effect. ${ }^{82}$ In addition, the activation energy for conduction of every $\mathrm{LiI}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ composite is usually very close to that of bulk LiI, excluding the possibility that enhanced conduction takes place using a newly generated migrating path at the interface. Smaller effects at higher temperature may be explained as being due to a reduction of the thickness of the space charge layer (the Debye length). Similar phenomena are also observed with AgCl , $\mathrm{AgBr}, \mathrm{CuCl}$, etc., in contact with insulating compounds, serving as a generally applicable method to develop new solid electrolytes. The mechanism of the phenomenon is discussed in more detail in Reference 83.

Another $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$conductor binary compound is $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{~N}$, the crystal structure of which is built up of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{~N}$ layers in hexagonal arrangement stacked along the c-axis with the rest of the Li in between. Intralayer migration of the latter $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$( Li 2 ) is responsible for ionic conduction. Thus its single crystal shows highly anisotropic conductivity, i.e., $1.2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ along the layers, while it is about 2 orders of magnitude lower along the c-axis. ${ }^{84}$ The conductivity of polycrystalline samples is typically $7 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at room temperature. However, the reported conductivities of this compound depend on the synthetic method or sample history, suggesting a structure sensitivity, i.e., involvement of defects in the Li2 conducting layer. In fact, $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{~N}$ intentionally doped with hydrogen exhibits a higher conductivity due to defects of Li 2 introduced by partial reduction of $\mathrm{N}^{3-}$ to $\mathrm{NH}^{2-}$. It has also been reported that $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{~N}$ so carefully prepared that it is not contaminated shows a conductivity as low as $10^{-5} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ regardless of the crystallographic direction. ${ }^{85}$

Though $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{~N}$ has an excellent conductivity, there is a disadvantage from a practical point of view, in that its decomposition voltage is as low as $\sim 0.45 \mathrm{~V}$, which means that it is thermodynamically impossible to construct a battery with emf exceeding this value. To overcome this problem, a number of derivatives of $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{~N}$ have been synthesized and tested as $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$conductors. The first example is a compound in the $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{LiCl}$ system, ${ }^{86}$ which shows a high decomposition voltage ( $\sim 2.5 \mathrm{~V}$ ), but its conductivity is much poorer than that of the mother compound. A more successful example is $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{~N}-\mathrm{LiI}-\mathrm{LiOH}(1: 2: 0.77)$ with the decomposition voltage between 1.6 to 1.8 V , which, at the same time, exhibits a high conductivity of $\sim 0.01 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ as $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{~N}$ with the electronic transference number being smaller than $10^{-5} .{ }^{87}$ The high conductivity of this compound probably originates from its structure based on the bcc arrangement of the anion ( $\mathrm{N}^{3-}$ and $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$) sublattice, which is the same as that of $\alpha-\mathrm{AgI}$.

More complex lithium compounds such as $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CdCl}_{4}$ in the spinel phase also show remarkable $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$conductivity at high temperature. ${ }^{88,89}$ Their spinel structure is the inverse one where half of the lithium ions are accommodated at the tetrahedral interstices of the cubic close packing (сср) array of anions and the other half are distributed statistically over the octahedral sites together with divalent cations ( $\mathrm{Cd}, \mathrm{Mg}, \mathrm{Mn}$, etc.). Nonstoichiometric compounds based on these chloride spinels, expressed as $\mathrm{Li}_{2-2 \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{M}_{1+\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Cl}_{4}$, are also formed and show higher conductivity than the stoichiometric ones, as shown in Figure 6.12. ${ }^{90}$ It is noted that the conductivity of $\mathrm{Li}_{2-2 \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Cd}_{1+\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Cl}_{4}(\mathrm{x}=0.05)$ has a record of $0.35 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Kinks seen in the conductivity curves are due to the phase transition from spinel to a defective NaCltype structure, in which the anion sublattice remains unchanged.

Now let us survey oxide lithium ion conductors. Most of them are based on oxyacid salts of lithium such as $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$ or $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{SiO}_{4}$. The $\gamma_{\text {II }}$ type of $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$ itself shows a small but not negligible conductivity at high temperature, as shown by the line for $\mathrm{x}=0$ in Figure 6.13. ${ }^{91}$ This structure belongs to the orthorhombic system (pseudohexagonal), in which the oxygens are packed into a hexagonal close packing (hcp) array, and both Li and P occupy its tetrahedral interstices, to form a framework with $\mathrm{PO}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{LiO}_{4}$ tetrahedra connected with each other. All $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions are used for building the structure such that they are only slightly mobile in pure $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$. On incorporation of other oxyacid anions like $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{SiO}_{4}$, however, the conductivity of the $\gamma$ II phase is remarkably increased as shown in Figure 6.13, because excess mobile $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ ions are introduced into the octahedral interstices as a result of solid solution formation $(1-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4} \cdot \mathrm{xLi}_{4} \mathrm{SiO}_{4}$ or $\mathrm{Li}_{3+\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{P}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Si}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{O}_{4}$. The conductivity has a maximum at $\mathrm{x}=0.5$, where half of the octahedral site in the conduction plane is filled. Replacement of P with V or As and/or of Si with Ge or Ti gives analogous solid solutions. ${ }^{92}$ Of these, the $\mathrm{As}-\mathrm{Ti}$ and $\mathrm{V}-\mathrm{Ti}$ systems show a higher conductivity of $\sim 5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at room temperature, probably because their interstices for migration are enlarged by the larger cations. A solid electrolyte known as LISICON is also based on the $\gamma_{\text {II }}-\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$ structure.

The compounds $\mathrm{LiZr}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ and $\mathrm{LiTi}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ are isostructural with $\mathrm{NaZr}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}$, the mother compound of NASICON mentioned earlier. They form NASICON-like solid solutions with $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{M}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}$, which are expressed, for example, by $(1-\mathrm{x} / 2) \mathrm{LiTi}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3} \cdot(\mathrm{x} / 2) \mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{M}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ or $\mathrm{Li}_{1+\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Ti}_{2-\mathrm{x}}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}$, where M is a trivalent cation such as $\mathrm{Sc}, \mathrm{Al}, \mathrm{In}$, etc. ${ }^{93,94}$ Most of these exhibit a much higher $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$conductivity than the end members, as shown in Figure 6.14, ${ }^{77}$ for the same reason, at least partly, as explained for NASICON. Especially, the conductivity of $\mathrm{Li}_{1+\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Al}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Ti}_{2-\mathrm{x}}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}(\mathrm{x}=0.3)$ reaches a value of $7 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at room temperature, being almost equal to that of $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{~N}$. It has been pointed out that improved intergranular contact in these solid solutions is also responsible for their higher conductivity.

Oxide and sulfide glasses are extensively being investigated as $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$conductors. Simple binary glasses in the $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ system show a conductivity of $\sim 10^{-6} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at room temperature, while the phosphate system is somewhat less conductive at the same $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ content. At the meta composition ( $1: 1$, i.e., $\mathrm{LiPO}_{3}, \mathrm{LiBO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{SiO}_{3}$ ), their structures

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-208.jpg?height=1218&width=839&top_left_y=159&top_left_x=359)
FIGURE 6.12 Temperature dependence of conductivity for spinel-type $\mathrm{Li}_{2-2 \mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Cd}_{1+\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Cl}_{4}$. (Adapted from Kanno, R., Takeda, Y., and Yamamoto, O., J. Electrochem. Soc., 1984, 131, 469. With permission.)

are, in principle, formed by long chains of repeat units, for example, $-\mathrm{O}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{2}\right)$ - (i.e., cornersharing $\mathrm{PO}_{4}$ tetrahedra). It is suggested that the phosphate glasses exhibit a lower conductivity than others because the fraction of oxygens with a full negative charge is the smallest of these three systems. ${ }^{95}$ Adding $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ as a network modifier, replacing $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, gives $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$-conductive glasses, though their conductivities are usually lower, as shown in Figure $6.15^{96}$ for the $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ system (as an end composition of the system $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ ).

As appears from Figure 6.15 the partial replacement of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ with $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ gives rise to a dramatic decrease in conductivity. Whereas the total cation concentration in the system is kept constant, conductivity at the midcomposition is 3 orders of magnitude lower than that expected from the Vegard-type additive rule. This phenomenon is not special for this ternary system and is called the "mixed alkaline effect". In contrast, addition of a co-glass former often enhances conductivity, and this is called the "mixed former (or anion) effect". For example, glasses in the $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{SiO}_{4}-\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{BO}_{3}$ system show a maximum conductivity at the composition 1:1, which is about 1 order of magnitude higher than that expected from the Vegard interpolation. ${ }^{97}$ A similar effect is induced by partial replacement of a modifier by another containing a different anion. The conductivity of $\mathrm{LiCl}-\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ spans the range $10^{-2}$ to

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-209.jpg?height=740&width=538&top_left_y=155&top_left_x=512)
FIGURE 6.13 Plot of $\sigma \mathrm{T}$ vs. $1 / \mathrm{T}$ where $\sigma$ is the conductivity of solid solutions in the ( $1-\mathrm{x}$ ) $\mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4} \cdot \mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{SiO}_{4}$. (Adapted from the Hu, Y.-W., Raistrick, I.D., and Huggins, R.A., J. Electrochem. Soc., 1977, 124, 1240. With permission.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-209.jpg?height=1043&width=865&top_left_y=1085&top_left_x=331)
FIGURE 6.14 Composition dependence of conductivity ( $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) of solid solutions in the $(1-\mathrm{x} / 2) \mathrm{LiTi}_{2}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3} \cdot(\mathrm{x} / 2) \mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{M}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{3}$ system. (Adapted from Adachi, G. and Aono, H., Bull. Ceram. Soc. Jpn., 1992, 27, 117. With permission.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-210.jpg?height=696&width=840&top_left_y=152&top_left_x=361)
FIGURE 6.15 Conductivity isotherms for glasses in the $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ system in which the total alkaline content ( $\mathrm{Na}+\mathrm{Li}$ ) is kept constant at $30 \% .(\mathrm{V}): 300^{\circ} \mathrm{C} ;(\mathrm{v}): 280^{\circ} \mathrm{C} ;(\mathrm{n}): 260^{\circ} \mathrm{C} ;(\mathrm{m}): 240^{\circ} \mathrm{C} ;(\mathrm{m}): 220^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. (Adapted from Kawamura, J., Mishima, S., Sato, R., and Shimoji, M., Proc. 13th Symp. on Solid State Ionics in Japan, Tokyo, 1986, p. 21. With permission.)

$10^{-3} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, which is higher than that of the respective binary systems. The origin of the "mixed alkaline effect" as well as of the "mixed anion effect" is still under debate.

Binary sulfide glasses in the system $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{~S}-\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{3}$ or $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{~S}-\mathrm{SiS}_{2}$, for example, show a higher conductivity than their oxide counterparts because $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ is larger and more polarizable. That for the former exhibits a conductivity as high as $10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at room temperature. ${ }^{98}$ Doping with LiI enhances their conductivity probably due to the same mixed anion effect as in the case of the oxides. Room temperature conductivities of some ternary sulfide glasses in the $\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{Sn}-\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{~S}-\mathrm{LiI}$ ( $\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{Si}, \mathrm{P}$, etc.) systems are shown in Figure 6.16. ${ }^{95}$
$\mathrm{Li}^{+}$conductors in noncrystalline polymeric phases are discussed in Section VIII.

## VII. PROTON CONDUCTORS

Solid proton conductors are of great importance in relation to the development of fuel cells, sensors, and electrochromic devices. As shown in Figure 6.17, ${ }^{99}$ these can be divided into two groups according to the temperature range in which they serve as a solid electrolyte. Members of the low- or moderate-temperature-type group are some solid state acids and the family of $\beta$-aluminas ion exchanged with protons. The high-temperature group consists of oxides belonging to the perovskite family.

Hydrated solid state inorganic acids more or less conduct protons in their crystal structure. Of these, $\mathrm{H}_{3}\left[\mathrm{PMo}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{40}\right] \cdot \mathrm{nH}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{n} \sim 29)$, a kind of heteropolyacid, shows a remarkably high conductivity ( $\sim 0.1 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ ) at room temperature. ${ }^{100}$ This compound has a cubic crystal structure, in which two diamond-type sublattices, $\left[\mathrm{PMo}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{40}\right]^{3-}$ (a Keggin-type polyanion) and $\left[\mathrm{H}_{3} \cdot 29 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]^{3+}$ (a cationic cluster), are interpenetrated. ${ }^{101}$ The latter cluster is formed by a complex hydrogen bonding network, which is connected to the network of the adjacent cluster. Thus the migration path of a proton utilizing such a hydrogen bonding network spreads over an entire section of the crystal. It is believed that proton conduction in this case takes place according to the Grotthus-type mechanism of proton transfer from a $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$molecule to an adjacent $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ molecule by the tunneling effect in the hydrogen bonding, followed by the rotation of molecules for the next transfer. Proton conduction by this type of mechanism is

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-211.jpg?height=934&width=915&top_left_y=152&top_left_x=319)
FIGURE 6.16 Ionic conductivity ( $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) vs. LiI content ( X ) in some ternary glass systems $\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{Sn}-\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{~S}-\mathrm{LiI}$ ( $\mathrm{M}=$ Si, P, Ge, As, and $\mathrm{n}=2$, 3). (Adapted from Magistris, A., Fast Ion Transport in Solids, Scrosati, B. et al., Eds., NATI ASI Series, Kluwer Academic Publishers, Dordrecht, 1993, 213. With permission.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-211.jpg?height=806&width=739&top_left_y=1287&top_left_x=415)
FIGURE 6.17 Conductivity of some proton conductors as a function of $1 / \mathrm{T}$. The overlapping area between $\mathrm{BaCeO}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{SrCeO}_{3}$ indicates solid solutions based on these compounds. (Adapted from Iwahara, H., Bull. Ceram. Soc. Jpn., 1992, 27, 112. With permission.)

characterized by a small activation energy ( $\sim 10 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ). As shown in Figure 6.17, the conductivity of $\mathrm{H}_{3}\left[\mathrm{PMo}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{40}\right] \cdot \mathrm{nH}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{n} \sim 29)$ depends very slightly on temperature, though the measuring range is narrow. At higher temperature or under dry conditions, its conductivity is drastically reduced because of loss of water. Some other acidic crystals such as $\mathrm{Sb}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \cdot \mathrm{nH}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and $\mathrm{HUO}_{2} \mathrm{PO}_{4} \cdot 4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ show considerable conductivity in humid atmospheres. ${ }^{102}$

Compounds in the $\beta$-alumina family turn into proton conductors when their $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions are ion exchanged with $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$and/or $\mathrm{NH}_{4}{ }^{+}$. Derivatives from the $\beta^{\prime \prime}$-phase usually show a higher conductivity than those from the $\beta$ phase, as shown in Figure 6.17. The highest conductivity of $10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ has been reported for the compound $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}\right)_{2 / 3} \mathrm{Mg}_{2 / 3} \mathrm{Al}_{31 / 3} \mathrm{O}_{17}$, which is synthesized by soaking $\beta^{\prime \prime}$-alumina in fused ammonium salt for a long time. ${ }^{103}$ Ionic conduction in this class of compounds, of course, occurs within layers between the spinel blocks, but it is still unclear whether protons are transported by a Grotthus-type mechanism or by a "vehicular" mechanism in which they are assumed to migrate as multiatomic ions such as $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$or $\mathrm{NH}_{4}^{+}$. Gallium analogs of $\beta$ - or $\beta^{\prime \prime}$-alumina are ion exchanged more smoothly, and $\mathrm{NH}_{4}-\beta$-gallates thus formed show higher conductivity than their alumina counterparts, probably because the spacing of the conduction plane is wider for these compounds. ${ }^{104}$

A thin film form of proton conductors attracts much attention from a practical point of view, especially as an ion-conducting layer for electrochromic devices. One of the promising candidates is an amorphous film of $\mathrm{Ta}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \cdot \mathrm{nH}_{2} \mathrm{O}$, which is formed on a substrate by spin coating of a peroxo-polytantalate solution. ${ }^{105}$ The structure of this noncrystalline compound consists of anionic clusters fragmented out of $\mathrm{L}-\mathrm{Ta}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ with cationic species like $\mathrm{H}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)^{+}$ linking them. Its conductivity measured after treatment at $80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is reported to be $4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ with a small activation energy ( $\sim 12 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ), suggesting the Grotthus mechanism being applicable. Moreover, the conductivity remains almost constant down to such a low humidity as corresponds with a dew point of $-60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Feasibility of an electrochromic cell

$$
\mathrm{WO}_{3}\left|\mathrm{Ta}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \cdot \mathrm{nH}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right| \mathrm{HIrO}_{2}
$$

has also been demonstrated.
Since high proton conductivity of perovskite-type oxides based on $\mathrm{SrCeO}_{3}$ was discovered in 1981, ${ }^{106}$ this class of high-temperature-type proton conductors has been extensively investigated. Mother compounds like $\mathrm{SrCeO}_{3}$ or $\mathrm{BaCeO}_{3}$ are not good conductors in themselves unless they are doped with aliovalent cations, to form a solid solution $\mathrm{SrCe}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{O}_{3-\delta}$ where $\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{Sc}, \mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{Yb}$, etc. The solid solutions are merely p -type semiconductors without a hydrogen source like $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ or $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$; the electron hole conductivity of $\mathrm{SrCe}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Yb}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{O}_{3-\delta}(\mathrm{x}=0.05)$, for example, in dry air is $0.01 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. When they are contacted with hydrogen sources, protons are introduced into their structure by the reaction,

$$
H_{2}+2 h^{\cdot}=2 H^{+}
$$

or

$$
V_{O}^{\bullet \bullet}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O}_{O}^{x}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}
$$

generating proton conduction much superior to that due to electron holes. It is noted that the former reaction reduces the concentration of electron holes at the same time. The conductivity of $\mathrm{SrCe}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Yb}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{O}_{3-\delta}(\mathrm{x}=0.05)$ in contact with $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ at $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is about $0.02 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$, which has been confirmed as being almost solely due to proton migration by the fact that, on passing current through a electrochemical cell of type

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-213.jpg?height=1253&width=861&top_left_y=152&top_left_x=347)
FIGURE 6.18 Conductivity of perovskite-type solid solutions as a function of 1/T. (Adapted from Iwahara, H., Bull. Ceram. Soc. Jpn., 1992, 27, 112. With permission.)

hydrogen evolves at its anode in obedience to Faraday's law. ${ }^{107}$ Similar experiments have also shown that protons are not transported as $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$, but as $\mathrm{H}^{+}$itself because no formation of water is observed at the anode. The conductivity of electron holes under these conditions has been reported to be 2 orders of magnitude lower than that of protons. ${ }^{108}$ In Figure 6.18, ${ }^{99}$ conductivities of compounds in this family measured in a hydrogen atmosphere are shown as a function of temperature. It is noted that at high temperatures ( $\sim 1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), a contribution from oxide ion conduction becomes considerable, especially in the case of compounds in the $\mathrm{BaCeO}_{3}$ series.

The detailed mechanism of proton conduction in these perovskite-type compounds is still controversial. Recent infrared spectroscopy studies have shown that protons exist in their structure as an OH species, though the O to H distance is considerably longer than usual, suggesting a contribution of hydrogen bonding. ${ }^{109}$ It is of interest to compare them with $\mathrm{H}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{ReO}_{3}$, which is built up of a structurally equivalent framework and exhibits very fast proton transport, though electronic conduction is overwhelming. According to neutron diffraction, its hydrogen atoms are statistically located at 72 equivalent sites that are on a sphere about
$1 \AA$ apart from each oxygen. ${ }^{110}$ It is thus reasonable to think that protons in a $\mathrm{ReO}_{3}$ - or perovskite-type framework are transported by hopping from an OH group to one of the nearest oxygens.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-214.jpg?height=250&width=1032&top_left_y=321&top_left_x=263)
FIGURE 6.19 Scheme of ionic transport in an amorphous polymer matrix. (Adapted from Ogata, N., Dodensei Kobunshi (Electrically Conductive Polymers), Ogata, N., Ed., Kodansha, 1990, 137. With permission.)

## VIII. POLYMER SOLID ELECTROLYTES

Some vinyl fluoride-based polymers with side chains of perfluorosulfonic acid (the Nafion family) are important ion-exchange membrane materials used in practice for electrolysis of NaCl and in certain fuel cells. They show a proton conductivity of $0.01 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at room temperature. However, such fast ionic transport occurs only when they are swollen with water. It is therefore not appropriate to call them solid electrolytes in the true sense of the word. It was in 1970 that anionic conductivity, though not high, was reported for crown ether complexes such as dibenzo-18-crown-6:KSCN, in which cations are trapped by the ligand. ${ }^{111} \mathrm{~A}$ few years later much higher cationic (instead of anionic) conduction was found in complexes of a chain-like polyether such as PEO or PPO with alkaline salts; here, PEO stands for poly(ethyleneoxide), $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{O}\right)_{n}$, and PPO for poly(propyleneoxide). ${ }^{112,113}$ These were the first examples of "true" polymer solid electrolytes and were followed by a great number of studies. Polymeric electrolytes are advantageous in practice because they are easily processed and formed into flexible films.

Polymer electrolytes such as (PEO) ${ }_{\mathrm{n}} \cdot \mathrm{LiCF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$ are obtained as a film, for example, by casting a mixed solution of PEO and $\mathrm{LiCF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$ and then evaporating the solvent completely. Films thus formed are usually composed of three regions: a crystalline phase of a PEO-MX(alkaline salt) complex, a crystalline phase of PEO itself, and a noncrystalline solid solution of PEO with MX. Previously it was assumed that the first complex phase was responsible for ionic conduction, because X-ray studies showed a structure in which cations were accommodated in a helical tunnel of PEO seemingly suitable for ionic conduction. More recently, however, it is believed that transport of ions takes place mainly in the noncrystalline phase, in which an alkaline salt like LiX is electrolytically dissociated as if it were dissolved in a polar liquid and $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions are coordinated (or solvated) by oxygens in the ether chain. Cation transport occurs as a result of a sequence of association and deassociation steps of $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{O}$ accompanied by local thermal motion of the polymer chains, as shown in Figure 6.19. ${ }^{114}$

The conductivity of $(\mathrm{PEO})_{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \mathrm{LiCF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{x}=12)$ and $(\mathrm{PEO})_{\mathrm{x}} \cdot \mathrm{LiClO}_{4}(\mathrm{x}=20)$ is very high at high temperatures (i.e., 3 to $5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $125^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), but is drastically decreased as the temperature is lowered ( $\sim 10^{-8} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ). ${ }^{115}$ The unexpectedly low conductivity at low temperature is mainly due to crystallization of the polymer chains. To prevent this, various crosslinked derivatives of PEO or PPO have been investigated as an alternative polymer matrix. A typical example is a PEO network prepared by a crosslinking reaction of triol-type PEO with tolylene-2,4-diisocyanate. Composites of this PEO derivative and $\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}$ show high conductivity even at room temperature, as shown in Figure 6.20. ${ }^{116}$ It is noted that solid electrolytes based on a crosslinked polymer are advantageous for applications also because their films have improved mechanical properties and thermal stability.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-215.jpg?height=909&width=604&top_left_y=155&top_left_x=479)
FIGURE 6.20 Temperature dependence of ionic conductivity for PEO- $\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}$ complexes. (Adapted from Watanabe, M., Nagano, S., Sanui, K., and Ogata, N., Solid State Ionics, 1986, 18/19, 338. With permission.)

As shown in Figure 6.20, the plots of conductivity vs. $1 / \mathrm{T}$ do not obey the Arrheniustype dependence. The observed convex dependence is characteristic of noncrystalline phases showing conductivity according to the foregoing mechanism. The conductivity for this case is usually well fitted by a function derived from the free volume theory, called the Williams-Landel-Ferry's (WLF) relationship,

$$
\log \left[\sigma(\mathrm{T}) / \sigma\left(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{g}}\right)\right]=\mathrm{C}_{1}\left(\mathrm{~T}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{g}}\right) /\left[\mathrm{C}_{2}+\left(\mathrm{T}-\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{g}}\right)\right]
$$

where $\sigma(T)$ is the conductivity at temperature $T, T_{g}$ the glass transition temperature, and $C_{1}$, $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ are constants. ${ }^{117}$ Sometimes the VTF relationship ${ }^{118}$ obtained from a similar free volume model is also used for curve fitting. Successful fitting with these types of relationship suggests that ionic transport in polymers and liquid phases is similar.

Besides PEO and PPO, many other macromolecules, even including inorganic polymers like poly(phosphazene), ( $\left.-\mathrm{N}=\mathrm{PCl}_{2}-\right)_{\mathrm{n}}$, can serve as a base material for this class of solid electrolyte. A compound, poly(bis-(methoxyethoxyethoxide)phosphazene), synthesized through the reaction of polyphosphazene with a Na salt of 2-(2methoxyethoxy)ethanol, forms amorphous complexes with $\mathrm{LiBF}_{4}, \mathrm{AgCF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$, etc., each of which shows very high conductivity for a polymer electrolyte, as shown in Figure 6.21, ${ }^{119}$ probably because of its very low $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{g}}$.

Very recently, new solid ionic conductors, so-called "polymer in-salt" materials, ${ }^{120}$ have been reported, in which lithium salts are mixed with small quantities of the polymers PEO and PPO, while conventional polymer electrolytes ("salt-in-polymer") contain only one Li per about ten repeat units of ether. The reported conductivity in the $\mathrm{AlCl}_{3}-\mathrm{LiBr}-\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}-\mathrm{PPO}$ system is as high as $0.02 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at room temperature.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-216.jpg?height=884&width=842&top_left_y=155&top_left_x=356)
FIGURE 6.21 Ionic conductivity ( $\mathrm{S} \mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ ) as plotted in $\ln (\sigma \mathrm{T})$ vs. $1 / \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{K})$ for $\left[\mathrm{M}\left(\mathrm{CF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}\right)_{\mathrm{x}}\right]_{0.25} \cdot \mathrm{MEEP}$ complexes. $\mathrm{MEEP}=\left[\mathrm{NP}\left(\mathrm{OC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{OC}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{OCH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]_{\mathrm{n}}$ and $\mathrm{M}=\mathrm{Ag}$, Li, Na, and Sr. (Adapted from Blonsky, P.M. and Shriver, D.F., Solid State Ionics, 1986, 18/19, 258. With permission.)

## I. INTRODUCTION

Solid mixed ionic-electronic conductors (MIECs) exhibit both ionic and electronic (electron/hole) conductivity. Naturally, in any material there are in principle nonzero electronic and ionic conductivities ( $\sigma_{\mathrm{e}}, \sigma_{\mathrm{i}}$ ). It is customary to limit the use of the name MIEC to those materials in which $\sigma_{\mathrm{i}}$, and $\sigma_{\mathrm{el}}$ do not differ by more than 2 orders of magnitude. It is also customary to use the term MIEC if $\sigma_{\mathrm{i}}$ and $\sigma_{\mathrm{el}}$ are not too low ( $\sigma_{\mathrm{i}}, \sigma_{\mathrm{el}} \geq 10^{-5} \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ ). Obviously, these are not strict rules. There are processes where the minority carriers play an important role despite the fact that $\sigma_{\mathrm{i}} / \sigma_{\mathrm{el}}$ exceeds those limits and $\sigma_{\mathrm{i}}, \sigma_{\mathrm{el}}<10^{-5} \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$. For example, the small electronic conductivity in a "purely" ionic conductor ( $\sigma_{\mathrm{i}} @ \sigma_{\mathrm{e} 1}$ ), i.e., in a solid electrolyte (SE), is a necessary condition for ion permeation through the SE and therefore, e.g., shortens the lifetime of a battery based on this SE.
