## I. INTRODUCTION

Chemical sensors are increasingly used in numerous applications, such as analysis setups, chemical processes, food industry, environmental control (water, air, industrial wastes, combustion monitoring, etc.), and biomedical applications (analysis and monitoring). Other new fields of applications could arise in the near future; for instance, integrated home systems. Electrochemical sensors are particularly attractive because the chemical quantities which are measured are directly transduced in electrical signals. As for electronic components, two types can be distinguished: active sensors, which give a voltage (potentiometric sensors), and passive sensors, for which an electrical source is necessary to apply a signal, the response of which is analyzed afterwards (amperometric, coulometric, and conductometric sensors).

We will limit our description of electrochemical sensors to those using a solid state material with an ionic conductivity playing a major role in the detection mechanism. These sensors are not always assembled in "all-solid state" technology, but in most of the devices such an assembling technique could be considered.

## A. BRIEF HISTORY

Basic electrochemical concepts were established in the 19th century by Volta (classification of redox couples), Faraday (relation between electricity and quantity of matter), Kohlrausch (conductivity measurements), and Nernst (thermodynamic approach), but the first sensors were developed at the end of the century and at the beginning of the 20th century. The pH electrode using a glass membrane was discovered by Cremer ${ }^{1}$ in 1906. Industrial development was achieved only about 20 years later. After a first approach made by Nikolskii ${ }^{2}$ in 1937, a theoretical model was established by Eisenman ${ }^{3}$ to explain interfering phenomena in sensors using a glass membrane. The use of the pH electrode for $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO} 2}$ measurement was proposed by Severinghaus and Bradley ${ }^{4}$ in 1957. After the ion-selective electrode (ISE) for the $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ion, introduced in 1937, ${ }^{5}$ other ISEs based on silver salts or on a $\mathrm{LaF}_{3}$ monocrystalline membrane have been developed in the 1960s. ${ }^{6,7}$ Ceramics were first used for $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ analysis in molten salts in $1960 .^{8}$

Since the work of Kiukkola and Wagner, ${ }^{9}$ solid oxide cells have been used for thermodynamic measurements. The first oxygen gauges were tested in 1961. ${ }^{10,11}$ Similar sensors based on other solid electrolytes have been proposed for halogens, ${ }^{12,13}$ sulfur oxides, ${ }^{14}$ and hydrogen. ${ }^{15}$

Amperometric and coulometric oxygen sensors are more recent. ${ }^{16,17}$ The first conductometric sensors using a $\mathrm{TiO}_{\mathrm{x}}$-based material were discovered in the $1970 \mathrm{~s} .^{18}$

## B. CURRENT TRENDS

At the present time, research tends toward the use of new ionic materials, amorphous ${ }^{19}$ or crystallized, to improve the selectivity properties. ${ }^{20}$ New systems coupling amperometric and potentiometric measurements are proposed. ${ }^{21}$ In this field, digital electronic development is a tool which is well suited for sophisticated devices. The miniaturization techniques are actually developed for mass production. The basic idea is to adapt microelectronic technology to the fabrication of chemical sensors. This is the main trend, whatever the kind of sensor. Solid ionic conductors (SIC) are very suitable for these methods of fabrication. Thin- or thick-layer techniques, using physical or chemical deposition methods, can be used. ${ }^{22-26,70,71}$ We can mention the first work of Velasco et al. ${ }^{26}$ concerning oxygen sensors. Since the development of the ion-sensitive field effect transistor by Bergveld ${ }^{27}$ in the 1970s, several ways have been proposed to deposit an ionic membrane on the gate of field effect transistors. The first device of this type, using a silver halide, was proposed by Buck and Hackerman. ${ }^{28}$

## C. PREFATORY NOTE

This article cannot be an exhaustive review on all the solid state electrochemical sensors which have been developed up to now. The literature in this field is abundant and we in particular mention some recent reviews. ${ }^{29-41}$ The goal of this paper is to explain, using solid state electrochemical concepts, the working principle of solid state electrochemical sensors and to show their limits of utilization. This theoretical treatment is illustrated by some examples. Field effect chemical transistors are not described here, and we refer to a recent review. ${ }^{42}$

## II. DIFFERENT KINDS OF SENSORS

## A. POTENTIOMETRIC SENSORS

## 1. Fundamental Principles

The fundamental principles of potentiometric sensors based on solid electrolytes have been reviewed in several papers. ${ }^{37,43-50}$ Potentiometric sensors are based on the existence of an equilibrium at the interface between a SIC and the analyzed medium, by exchange of electrochemical species. This hypothesis is acceptable (after a transient response) if there is
no net dc current. We classify different kinds of sensors based on the nature of the interfacial exchange, and therefore it is useful to give a description of elementary steps in an electrochemical sensor.

## a. Ionic Junction of the First Kind

In this case, only a single electrochemical species ( $\mathrm{X}^{\text {z }+}$, mobile ion) is exchanged between the SIC and the analyzed solution (sol). Typical examples are $\mathrm{LaF}_{3} / \mathrm{F}^{-}$, stabilized zirconia $/ \mathrm{O}^{2-}$, $\mathrm{AgCl} / \mathrm{Ag}^{+}$, or $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$-based glass $/ \mathrm{Na}^{+}$interfaces in ISE. The fundamental hypothesis can be expressed as

$$
\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{SIC}}^{z+} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{sol}}^{z+}
$$

The equilibrium condition is $\Delta \tilde{\mathrm{G}}=0$, e.g.,

$$
\tilde{\mu}_{\mathrm{X}^{z+}(\mathrm{SIC})}=\tilde{\mu}_{\mathrm{X}^{z+}(\mathrm{sol})}
$$

where $\tilde{\mu}_{\mathrm{X}^{2+}}$ is the electrochemical potential of $\mathrm{X}^{z+}$ which can be written in terms of the chemical potential $\mu$ and the electrical energy $\mathrm{zF} \varphi$ in the considered phase (see also Chapter 2 of this handbook), by the relation

$$
\tilde{\mu}_{\mathrm{X}^{2+}}=\mu_{\mathrm{X}^{2+}}+\mathrm{zF} \varphi
$$

with

$$
\mu_{\mathrm{X}^{2+}}=\mu_{\mathrm{X}^{2+}}^{\mathrm{o}}+\mathrm{RT} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{X}^{\mathrm{z}+}\right)
$$

$\mu^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{x}}{ }^{+}$being the standard chemical potential, $\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{X}^{\mathrm{z}+}\right)$ the activity of $\mathrm{X}^{\mathrm{z}+}, \mathrm{T}$ the absolute temperature, and R and F have their usual meaning. For dilute phases, concentration and activity can be assumed to be equal. For concentrated phases, interactions between species must be taken into account by introducing an activity coefficient which can be calculated, for example, from the Debye-Hückel theory (see some common books of electrochemistry). ${ }^{51,52}$

Equations (10.1b) to (10.3) give the Galvani potential between both phases (SIC and sol),

$$
\begin{array}{r}
\varphi_{\text {SIC }}-\varphi_{\text {sol }}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{zF}}\left(\mu_{\mathrm{x}^{z+}(\text { sol })}^{0}-\mu_{\mathrm{x}^{z+}(\mathrm{SIC})}^{0}\right)+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{zF}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{X}^{z+}\right)_{\text {sol }} \\
-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{zF}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{X}^{z+}\right)_{\text {SIC }}
\end{array}
$$

If the concentration of the mobile ions in the SIC ( $a\left(\mathrm{X}^{z+}\right)_{\text {SIC }}$ ) is high, its activity can be considered to be constant and so the Galvani potential is directly related to the activity of ions in the analyzed solution ( $a\left(X^{z+}\right)_{\text {sol }}$ ). Figure 10.1a shows a schematic diagram of $\varphi, \mu$, and $\mu ̃$ variations. The Galvani potential cannot be measured directly, and a longer thermodynamic chain is necessary up to both metal connections. A more detailed description is given later (see also Chapter 2 of this handbook).

## b. Ionic Junction of the Second Kind

In this case, the mobile ion of the SIC reacts with the analyzed ion to give a complex or an insoluble chemical product at the interface

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-329.jpg?height=1485&width=666&top_left_y=117&top_left_x=420)
FIGURE 10.1. Schematic presentation of electrical ( $\varphi$ ), chemical ( $\mu$ ), and electrochemical ( $\tilde{\mu}$ ) potential variations in ionic junctions: (a) first kind, (b) second kind.

$$
\mathrm{y} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{SIC}}^{\mathrm{z}+}+\mathrm{zB}_{\mathrm{sol}}^{\mathrm{y}-} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{~B}_{\mathrm{z}}
$$

and the thermodynamic relation is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{x}^{\mathrm{z}+(\mathrm{SIC})}}+ & \mathrm{yRT} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{X}^{\mathrm{z}+}\right)_{\mathrm{SIC}}+\mathrm{yzF} \varphi_{\mathrm{SIC}}+\mathrm{z} \mu_{\mathrm{B}^{\mathrm{y}-}(\mathrm{sol})}^{\mathrm{o}} \\
& +\mathrm{zRT} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{~B}^{\mathrm{y}-}\right)_{\mathrm{sol}}-\mathrm{yzF} \varphi_{\mathrm{sol}}=\mu_{\mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{~B}_{\mathrm{z}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{z}}$ forms a separate phase, its chemical potential is equal to the standard potential $\mu^{\circ}$. The Galvani potential is given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_{\text {SIC }}-\varphi_{\text {sol }}=\frac{1}{y z \mathrm{~F}}\left(\mu_{\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{~B}_{z}}^{\mathrm{o}}\right. & \left.-\mathrm{yH}_{\mathrm{x}^{z+}(\mathrm{SIC})}^{\mathrm{o}}-\mathrm{z} \mu_{\mathrm{B}^{y-}(\text { sol })}^{\mathrm{o}}\right) \\
& -\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{zF}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{X}^{z+}\right)_{\text {SIC }}-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{yF}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{~B}^{y-}\right)_{\text {sol }}
\end{aligned}
$$

As in case a , if the concentration of $\mathrm{X}^{\mathrm{z}+}$ in the SIC is high, the voltage is proportional to the logarithm of the activity of the analyzed ion. Of course, the variation is a function of the numerical value of y (according to its sign). Typical examples are AgCl membranes and $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}-\mathrm{AgCl}$ mixtures to determine the activity of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions. If AgCl is a separate phase, Equation (10.5c) can be applied and the Nernst equation is obeyed well. If AgCl is dissolved in another phase, the value of the chemical potential $\mu_{\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{B}_{\mathrm{z}}}$ may not be constant and Equation (10.5c) would no longer be valid.

The thermodynamic chain is more complex in the case of a ternary compound. To illustrate this, we consider the $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}-\mathrm{MS} / \mathrm{M}^{2+}$ interface (for example, $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}, \mathrm{Cd}^{2+}$, or $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}$ ISEs will operate according to this mechanism). A schematic view is given in Figure 10.1b. In this case, the SIC is a conductor for $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ions. Equilibrium at the interface is ensured by the following exchange reactions, in a macroscopic description,

$$
\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{sol}}^{2+}+\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{sIC}}^{2-} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{MS}
$$

and, in the bulk of the membrane, near the interface

$$
2 \mathrm{Ag}_{\text {SIC }}^{+}+\mathrm{S}_{\text {SIC }}^{2-} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}
$$

The Galvani potential is now obtained as

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_{\mathrm{SIC}}-\varphi_{\mathrm{sol}}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~F}}\left(\mu_{\mathrm{M}^{2+}(\mathrm{sol})}^{\mathrm{o}}-2 \mu_{\mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{SIC})}^{\mathrm{o}}+\mu_{\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}}-\mu_{\mathrm{MS}}\right) \\
-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{~F}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right)_{\mathrm{SIC}}+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{M}^{2+}\right)_{\mathrm{sol}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The voltage obeys the Nernst equation with respect to $\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{M}^{2+}\right)_{\text {sol }}$ only if

- the concentration of $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$in the SIC is sufficiently high to assume that $\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right)_{\text {SIC }}$ is constant,
- $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ is the dominant phase and $\mu_{\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}}=\mu^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}}$
- $\mu_{\mathrm{MS}}$ is constant, e.g., MS is a separate phase or is sufficiently concentrated in $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ (or other solid solvent like a glass)

Similar conclusions are obtained if we use a microscopic model. The $\mathrm{M}^{2+}$ ion is assumed to be dissolved in a silver salt according to the following reaction, using the Kröger-Vink notation,

$$
\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{Ag}}^{\prime}+\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{sol}}^{2+} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Ag}}^{\cdot}
$$

The Galvani potential is then given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_{\text {SIC }} & -\varphi_{\text {sol }}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{~F}}\left(\mu^{\mathrm{o}}{ }_{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{Ag}}^{\prime}(\mathrm{SIC})}+\mu_{\mathrm{M}^{2+}(\text { sol })}-\mu_{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Ag}}^{\circ}(\mathrm{SIC})}\right) \\
& -\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{Ag}}^{\cdot}\right)_{\mathrm{SIC}}+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{Ag}}^{\prime}\right)_{\mathrm{SIC}}+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{M}^{2+}\right)_{\text {sol }}
\end{aligned}
$$

This description shows that

- the $\mathrm{M}^{2+}$ ion concentration in the solid matrix needs to be buffered, and
- the $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$vacancy concentration must be high.


## c. Electrode Reaction of the First Kind

One material of the junction is an electronic conductor, generally a metal or a semiconductor which shows catalytic properties. This type of junction is the transducing part from the ionic conductor to the metallic connection in ISE devices. It is also the gas electrode in potentiometric gauges.

In most simple cases, the SIC and the metal have a common chemical species and the following equilibrium can be written:

$$
\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{SIC}}^{\mathrm{n}+}+\mathrm{n} \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{M}}^{-} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{M}
$$

M has two functions: it is a neutral chemical species and acts as a donor (or acceptor) of electrons. The Galvani potential is then given by

$$
\varphi_{M}-\varphi_{\text {SIC }}=\frac{1}{n F}\left(\mu_{M^{\mathrm{n}+(S I C)}}^{0}-\mu_{M}^{0}+n \mu_{e}\right)+\frac{R T}{n F} \ln a\left(M^{\mathrm{n}+}\right)_{\text {SIC }}
$$

The concentration of electrons is generally very high, so $\mu_{\mathrm{e}}$ is constant. With the usual assumption that $\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{n}+}\right)_{\text {SIC }}$ is high, the Galvani potential is constant during the measurement. Equations (10.8b) and (10.4) or (10.5b), (10.5c), (10.6c), and (10.7b) give a Nernst equation for the metal potential as a function of the activity of the analyzed ion. Of course, if M is not a pure phase, for instance, an amalgam or a solid solution, $\mu^{\circ}{ }_{\mathrm{m}}$ must be replaced by $\mu_{\mathrm{m}}$ in Equation (10.13) and the activity of M in this solution must be taken into account. This activity must be constant during the measurements (no chemical modifications may take place by faradaic effects due to parasitic currents).

In the case of a gas electrode, three phases are in contact (Figure 10.2a): inactive metal (M), gas ( $\mathrm{X}_{2}$ ), and SIC (for instance, conducting $\mathrm{X}^{2-}$-ions). The equilibrium reaction is given by

$$
\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{SIC}}^{\mathrm{z}-} \Leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{X}_{2}+\mathrm{z} \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$

and the Galvani potential

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_{\mathrm{M}}-\varphi_{\mathrm{SIC}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{zF}} \quad \frac{1}{2} \mu_{\mathrm{X}_{2}}^{0}-\mu_{\mathrm{X}^{\mathrm{z}-(\mathrm{SIC})}}^{0}+\mathrm{z}_{\mathrm{e}} & -\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{zF}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{X}^{\mathrm{z}-}\right)_{\mathrm{SIC}} \\
& +\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{zF}} \ln \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{X}_{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

which corresponds with the Nernst equation for $\mathrm{X}_{2}$ analysis.
![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-332.jpg?height=353&width=640&top_left_y=122&top_left_x=436)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-332.jpg?height=339&width=494&top_left_y=615&top_left_x=439)
(b)

FIGURE 10.2. Schematics of electrode reactions: (a) first kind, (b) second kind.

## d. Electrode Reaction of the Second Kind

As for an ionic junction of the second kind, the mobile ion of the SIC reacts with the analyzed species, forming an intermediate phase (separate phase or dissolved species). Simple examples are halide gauges ( $\mathrm{I}_{2}, \mathrm{Cl}_{2}, \mathrm{Br}_{2}$ ) using a cationic conductor. The equilibrium reaction is given by

$$
\frac{\mathrm{z}}{2} \mathrm{X}_{2}+\mathrm{nA}_{\text {SIC }}^{\mathrm{z}+}+\mathrm{nz} \mathrm{e}^{-} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{z}}
$$

and the Galvani potential

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_{\mathrm{M}}-\varphi_{\mathrm{SIC}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~F}} \quad \frac{1}{2 \mathrm{n}} \mu_{\mathrm{X}_{2}}^{\mathrm{o}} & +\frac{1}{\mathrm{z}} \mu_{\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{z}+(\mathrm{SIC})}}^{\mathrm{o}}+\mu_{\mathrm{e}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{nz}} \mu_{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{x}_{\mathrm{z}}} \\
& +\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{zF}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{\mathrm{z}+}\right)_{\mathrm{SIC}}+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{nF}} \ln \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{X}_{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

As in the previous case, the same conclusions are obtained: the voltage obeys the Nernst equation if $\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{z}+}\right)_{\text {SIC }}$ and $\mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{z}}\right)$ are constant during measurement. The second condition is justified if $\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{z}}$ is a separate phase or if it is dissolved in a solid and its concentration is buffered.

In some sensors, two gases react simultaneously. A typical example is a $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ gauge in air using a cationic conductor to give a carbonate at the interface (Figure 10.2b). In such a case, the equilibrium reaction is more complex,

$$
2 \mathrm{ze}^{-}+2 \mathrm{zA}_{\text {SIC }}^{\mathrm{z}+}+\frac{\mathrm{z}}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}+\mathrm{zCO}_{2} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{~A}_{2}\left(\mathrm{CO}_{3}\right)_{\mathrm{z}}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-333.jpg?height=281&width=628&top_left_y=126&top_left_x=436)
FIGURE 10.3. Concentration profile in a nonhomogeneous SIC (linear model).

and a similar derivation gives

$$
\begin{gathered}
\varphi_{\mathrm{M}}-\varphi_{\mathrm{SIC}}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~F}} \quad \frac{1}{4} \mu_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{\mathrm{o}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{z}} \mu_{\mathrm{A}^{z+}(\mathrm{SIC})}^{\mathrm{o}}+\mu_{\mathrm{e}}+\frac{1}{2} \mu_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}^{\mathrm{o}}-\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{z}} \mu_{\mathrm{A}_{2}\left(\mathrm{CO}_{3}\right)_{\mathrm{z}}} \\
+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{zF}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{\mathrm{z}+}\right)_{\mathrm{SIC}}+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{4 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{CO}_{2}}
\end{gathered}
$$

The voltage obeys the Nernst equation for $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ if $\mathrm{A}_{2}\left(\mathrm{CO}_{3}\right)_{\mathrm{z}}$ has a constant activity (when it is, e.g., a separate phase) and also if the oxygen pressure is fixed, for instance in air. In usual $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ sensors, the species A is currently silver or an alkaline ion and so $\mathrm{z}=1 .{ }^{95-98}$ Other sensors following this principle can be proposed.

## e. Ionic Exchange at Interfaces

If a SIC is in contact with an active species, it can penetrate into the bulk by exchange followed by diffusion. It is then called an interfering species. The SIC becomes nonhomogeneous in composition due to the penetration of the interfering species. If the concentration of the mobile ion in the SIC is sufficiently high, it does not change much in use. A linear diffusion profile is assumed in Figure 10.3. At the interface between the SIC and the external medium, there is the following equilibrium:

$$
\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{SIC}}+\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{ext}} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{ext}}+\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{SIC}}
$$

where I is the normal species and K the interfering one. The subscript "ext" is used to indicate the external medium (solution or electrode material). This reaction is shown only qualitatively, and is written for neutral species. Stoichiometric coefficients would be necessary if I and K have different valences. The interfacial potential between the SIC ( $\beta$, see Figure 10.3) and the external medium is fixed by the equilibrium

$$
\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{SIC}} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{ext}}
$$

The potential is given by

$$
\varphi_{\beta}-\varphi_{\mathrm{ext}}=\mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{~F}} \ln \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}(\mathrm{ext})}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}(\beta)}
$$

where $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}$ includes the usual constants ( $\mu^{\circ}$, etc.) and $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}$ are the concentrations (we suppose that the solid solution is not sufficiently disturbed to change the activity coefficient). A similar relation can be obtained with the interfering species.

Without a dc current, the sum of the partial current densities is equal to zero and the general relation is

$$
\sum_{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{z}_{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{FC}_{\mathrm{j}} \tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{j}} \frac{\mathrm{~d} \tilde{\mu}_{\mathrm{j}}}{\mathrm{dx}}=0
$$

where $\tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{j}}$ is the electrochemical mobility. Using Equation (10.2), the gradient of $\varphi$ is given by

$$
\frac{d \varphi}{d x}=-\frac{R T}{F} \frac{\sum_{j} z_{j} \tilde{u}_{j} \frac{d C_{j}}{d x}}{\sum_{j} z_{j}^{2} C_{j} \tilde{u}_{j}}
$$

To simplify the derivation, we consider the example for which there are only two ions (I and K ). The electroneutrality relation then reads

$$
\mathrm{z}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{z}_{\mathrm{k}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{k}}=\mathrm{z}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{C}^{0}
$$

where $\mathrm{C}^{\circ}$ is the initial concentration of the ion I . In this case, the following differential relation holds

$$
\frac{\mathrm{dC}_{\mathrm{k}}}{\mathrm{dx}}=-\frac{\mathrm{z}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{z}_{\mathrm{k}}} \frac{\mathrm{dC}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{dx}}
$$

By integration of Equation (10.16), taking into account Equations (10.17) and (10.18), the variation of $\varphi$ between two points in the bulk of the SIC, in a linear model, we obtain

$$
\varphi_{\beta}-\varphi_{\alpha}=\frac{R T}{F} \frac{\left(\tilde{u}_{k}-\tilde{u}_{i}\right)}{\left(z_{i} \tilde{u}_{i}-z_{k} \tilde{u}_{k}\right)} \ln \frac{C_{i}(\beta)\left[z_{i} \tilde{u}_{i}-z_{k} \tilde{u}_{k}\right]+z_{k} C^{0} \tilde{u}_{k}}{C_{i}(\alpha)\left[z_{i} \tilde{u}_{i}-z_{k} \tilde{u}_{k}\right]+z_{k} C^{0} \tilde{u}_{k}}
$$

For a potentiometric sensor, the measured potential between the SIC bulk ( $\alpha$ ) and the external medium can be calculated according to

$$
\varphi_{\mathrm{SIC}}-\varphi_{\mathrm{ext}}=\left(\varphi_{\alpha}-\varphi_{\beta}\right)+\left(\varphi_{\beta}-\varphi_{\mathrm{ext}}\right)
$$

in which the term $\varphi_{\beta}-\varphi_{\text {ext }}$ corresponds to the Galvani potential as defined in Equation (10.14).

## f. Electrochemical Chain

Equations (10.14), (10.19), and (10.20) allow one to calculate the emf developed between two phases which are nonhomogeneous. In a potentiometric sensor, two metal electrodes are necessary, which are connected to a voltmeter. A schematic view is given in Figure 10.4. To obtain the SIC voltage, it is necessary to realize a reversible junction with a metal connection as described in Section II.A.1.c. A direct exchange is not always easy, and therefore a more complex chain must be elaborated by adding an intermediate system such as:

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-335.jpg?height=90&width=816&top_left_y=124&top_left_x=342)
FIGURE 10.4. Schematics of an electrochemical chain (ion-selective electrode).

- an ionic conducting material which can exchange common ions with the SIC and the connection material (exchange of the first or second kind),
- a mixed (ionic and electronic) conducting material which can exchange ions with the SIC and electrons with the metal.

In ionic sensors, such systems are called ionic bridge or internal reference. As the second electrode, an external reference is used; for instance, a calomel electrode with a liquid junction.

A fundamental assumption for evaluation of the voltage is that the SIC and other intermediate materials are equipotential. The electrochemical potential of the mobile ion is assumed to be constant throughout the material. The chemical potential can be considered to be constant if the mobile ion concentration in the SIC is high. From Equation (10.2) it follows that the electrochemical potential is constant when $\varphi=$ constant.

For potentiometric devices, the sensor voltage is given by an algebraic relation including all phases and interface voltages. The interface voltages are given by Galvani potentials previously established and the difference of electrical potential in a phase is supposed to be negligible under steady-state conditions, except if it is a nonhomogeneous material. We will discuss the possible fluctuations of the electrical potential in such a material later.

## 2. Ionic Sensors

Table 10.1 summarizes the characteristics of common ISEs and a number of new sensors in this field. We have not included in this table the liquid or polymer membrane-based electrodes which are selective, but rather fragile (for more details on such membranes see References 58,59). ISEs of the first kind are not very numerous, e.g., $\mathrm{F}^{-}$-ISE (monocrystalline membrane based on $\mathrm{LaF}_{3}$ ), $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$-ISE (silver salts), or $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$-ISE ( Na alumino-silicate glass or polycrystalline NASICON [Na super ionic conductor] membranes). Most of the ISEs are of the second kind and are based on insoluble silver salts; for example, halide ISEs ( $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{Br}^{-}$, $\mathrm{I}^{-}$), $\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}, \mathrm{Pb}^{2+}, \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$, etc. Such ISEs use mixtures of insoluble salts based on silver sulfide or silver selenide. Recently, Vlasov et al. ${ }^{60,61}$ and Neshkova ${ }^{62}$ have proposed several glasses sensitive to transition metals. Typical ISE devices are shown in Figure 10.5. Thin-layer chemical sensors based on chalcogenide glasses have also been developed. ${ }^{63}$

## a. Solid Internal Reference Systems

The usual internal systems are liquid solutions, because a salt can be dissolved to give an ionic junction. For instance, in a $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$-ISE, a NaCl aqueous solution gives an equilibrium with the $\mathrm{Na}-\mathrm{SIC}$ ( $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$exchange) and an electrode reaction with an $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgCl}$ connection ( $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ exchange). Such systems are advantageous because

- the NaCl concentration is high so that there is a good buffering effect (this point is discussed later), the electrochemical reversibility is improved (interface and electrode reaction), and the internal resistance is low; and
- the interfacial contact is very good.

Nevertheless, there are some disadvantages, such as

TABLE 10.1
Main Ion Selective Electrodes
| Ion (x) | Membrane | Concentration range | Main interfering ions $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}} \geq 10^{-2}$ | Ref. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ | $10^{-1}-10^{-7}$ | $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{Br}, \mathrm{SCN}^{-}, \mathrm{CN}^{-}, \mathrm{S}^{2-}, \mathrm{Hg}^{2+}$ | 53 |
| $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+\mathrm{AgBr}$ AgBr | $1-10^{-5}$ | $\mathrm{I}^{-}, \mathrm{SCN}^{-}, \mathrm{CN}^{-}$ | 53-55 |
| $\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+\mathrm{CdS}$ | $10^{-1-10^{-7}}$ | $\mathrm{S}^{2-}, \mathrm{Pb}^{2+}, \mathrm{Ag}^{+}$, | 53,54 |
| $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+\mathrm{AgCl}$ | $10^{-1}-510^{-5}$ | $\mathrm{CN}^{-}, \mathrm{I}^{-}, \mathrm{Br}^{-}, \mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{S}^{2-}, \mathrm{SCN}^{-}$ | 53,54 |
| $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+\mathrm{AgI}$ | $10^{-2}-10^{-6}$ | $\mathrm{I}^{-}, \mathrm{S}^{2-}, \mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ | 49,53,55 |
| $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+\mathrm{CuS}$ | $10^{-1}-10^{-8}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{Hg}^{2+}, \mathrm{S}^{2-}, \mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{Br}^{-}, \mathrm{SCN}^{-}, \mathrm{CN}^{-}$ | 49,55 |
| $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{LaF}_{3}$ | $1-10^{-6}$ | $\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ | 53,54 |
| $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{O}^{+}$ | Glass | $1-10^{-14}$ |  | 49,53 |
| $\mathrm{Hg}^{2+}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+\mathrm{AgI}$ |  | $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{Br}^{-}, \mathrm{SCN}^{-}, \mathrm{CN}^{-}$ | 56 |
| $\mathrm{I}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+\mathrm{AgI}$ AgI | $1-510^{-8}$ | $\mathrm{S}^{2-}, \mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{CN}^{-}$ | 53-55 |
| $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ | Glass | $1-50^{-7}$ | $\mathrm{H}_{3} 0^{+}, \mathrm{K}^{+}, \mathrm{Li}^{+}$, | 53, 57 |
|  | NASICON | $1-10^{-4}$ | $\mathrm{H}_{3} 0^{+}$ | 57 |
| $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+\mathrm{PbS}$ | $1-10^{-7}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{Hg}^{2+}, \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}, \mathrm{Cd}^{2+}, \mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{Br}^{-}, \mathrm{SCN}^{-}, \mathrm{CN}^{-}$ | 53-55 |
| $\mathrm{S}^{2-}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ | $1-10^{-7}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}, \mathrm{Hg}^{2+}$ | 53,54 |
| $\mathrm{SCN}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+\mathrm{AgSCN}$ | $1-510^{-6}$ | $\mathrm{I}^{-}, \mathrm{Cl}^{-}, \mathrm{OH}^{-}, \mathrm{Br}, \mathrm{CN}^{-}, \mathrm{S}^{2-}$ | 49,59 |


![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-336.jpg?height=913&width=750&top_left_y=1016&top_left_x=375)
FIGURE 10.5. Typical ISE devices.

- there are limitations in the working temperature;
- there is a restriction in the positioning (it is necessary to put the sensitive part below to maintain the liquid phase in contact with the membrane); one can remark here that to increase the freedom in use, it is possible to replace the liquid solution by a gel; and
- the systems are not suitable for miniaturization.

For these reasons, several improvements have been suggested which are described in detail in the review paper of Nikolskii and Materova. ${ }^{64}$ We can distinguish three classes of internal reference systems:
(i) metal having cations in common with the SIC. The calculation of the voltage is easy from Equations (10.8b) and (10.4) (or [10.5c] or [10.6c]). As an example, for a $\mathrm{M}^{2+}$-ISE with silver as the internal connection, the electrochemical chain is

$$
\text { connection : } \mathbf{e}^{-}, \mathbf{A g} / \mathbf{S I C}: \mathbf{A g}^{+}, \mathbf{S}^{2-}, \mathbf{M}^{2+} / \mathbf{s o l}: \mathbf{M}^{2+}
$$

We obtain,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_{\mathrm{M}}-\varphi_{\text {sol }}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~F}} \mu_{\mathrm{e}}-\mu_{\mathrm{Ag}}^{\mathrm{o}}+\frac{1}{2} \mu_{\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}}-\frac{1}{2} \mu_{\mathrm{MS}} & +\frac{1}{2} \mu_{\mathrm{M}^{2+}(\text { sol })}^{\mathrm{o}} \\
& +\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{M}^{2+}\right)_{\text {sol }}
\end{aligned}
$$

As $\varphi_{\text {sol }}$ can be related to the reference electrode potential, one can write the usual equation

$$
\varphi_{\mathrm{M}}-\varphi_{\mathrm{sol}}=\mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{M}^{2+}\right)_{\mathrm{sol}}
$$

where $\mathrm{E}^{\circ}$ includes the different constants. The aforementioned assumptions are supposed to be obeyed (essentially constant concentrations of $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ and MS or a similar assumption in a microscopic description). A similar law is obtained for the electrochemical chain,

$$
\text { connection }(\mathbf{M e}): \mathbf{e}^{-}, \mathbf{A} / \mathbf{A}_{\mathbf{X}} \mathbf{F} / \mathbf{S I C}: \mathbf{F}^{-} / \mathbf{s o l}: \mathbf{F}^{-}
$$

which is used in a $\mathrm{F}^{-}$-ISE. ${ }^{65-67}$
(ii) mixed conductor (intercalation compounds, glasses) which exchanges electrons with the connection and ions with the SIC. ${ }^{68-71}$ As a typical example, we describe here an alkaline ISE (sensitive to $\mathrm{A}^{+}$). The electrochemical chain is

$$
\text { connection }(\mathrm{Me}): \mathbf{e}^{-} / \text {mixed conductor }: \mathbf{e}^{-}, \mathbf{A}^{+} / \mathrm{SIC}: \mathbf{A}^{+} / \mathrm{sol}: \mathbf{A}^{+}
$$

and the voltage is given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_{M}-\varphi_{\text {sol }}=\frac{1}{F}\left(\mu_{e(\text { Me })}-\right. & \left.\mu_{e(\text { int })}+\mu_{A^{+}(\text {sol })}^{o}-\mu_{A^{+}(\text {int })}^{o}\right) \\
& \quad-\frac{R T}{F} \ln a\left(A^{+}\right)_{\text {int }}+\frac{R T}{F} \ln a\left(A^{+}\right)_{\text {sol }}
\end{aligned}
$$

From this equation it clearly appears that the voltage response obeys the Nernst equation only if the concentrations of $\mathrm{e}^{-}$and $\mathrm{A}^{+}$in the mixed conductor (int) are constant. Any parasitic dc current or the use of a voltmeter having too low an input impedance can change their values by a faradaic effect, which results in a drift of the cell voltage. Therefore, the initial amount of intercalated species must be high enough so that the changes in its concentration are small enough to avoid these problems.
(iii) solid solution having one ion in common with the SIC and another ion common with the internal connection (generally in an electrode reaction of the second kind), for instance a polymer or a silver halide solution. ${ }^{72-74}$ As an example of such a solid solution, we consider the following chain

$$
\text { connection }: \mathbf{e}^{-}, \mathbf{M} / \text { int }: \mathbf{M}^{\mathbf{n}+}, \mathbf{A}^{\mathbf{z}^{+}} / \text {SIC }: \mathbf{A}^{\mathbf{z}^{+}} / \text {sol }: \mathbf{A}^{\mathbf{z}^{+}}
$$

which leads to

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_{\mathrm{M}}-\varphi_{\text {sol }}= & \frac{1}{\mathrm{~F}} \mu_{\mathrm{e}(\mathrm{M})}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{nF}}\left(\mu_{\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{n}+(\text { int })}}-\mu_{\mathrm{M}}^{\mathrm{o}}\right)+\frac{1}{\mathrm{zF}}\left(\mu_{\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{z}+(\text { sol })}}-\mu_{\mathrm{A}^{\mathrm{z}+(\text { int })}}\right) \\
& +\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{n}+}\right)_{\text {int }}-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{zF}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{\mathrm{z}+}\right)_{\text {int }}+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{zF}} \ln \mathrm{a}\left(\mathrm{~A}^{\mathrm{z}+}\right)_{\text {sol }}
\end{aligned}
$$

The same remark as that concerning the amount of intercalated compound can be made about the concentrations of common ions in the internal material. The solid solution $\mathrm{AgI}-\mathrm{NaI}$ is not stable at room temperature, and a drifting voltage was observed with a $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$-ISE using such an internal reference system. ${ }^{74}$ The solid solutions $\mathrm{AgCl}-\mathrm{NaCl}$ or $\mathrm{AgBr}-\mathrm{NaBr}$ are more stable, but their conductivities are low.

## b. Interfering Phenomena

When a SIC, conducting ions I, is in contact with a solution of ions J (interfering ions), an ionic exchange reaction takes place:

$$
\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{SIC}}+\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{sol}} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{sol}}+\mathrm{J}_{\mathrm{SIC}}
$$

The solubility of J in the SIC is generally poor and limited to its surface. As a result of the ionic exchange reaction, the membrane also becomes sensitive to the ion J. Nikolskii ${ }^{2}$ has first proposed the empirical equation

$$
\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{E}^{\circ}+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{~F}} \ln \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{i}}+\sum_{\mathrm{j}} \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{i}, \mathrm{j}} \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{j}}^{\mathrm{zi} / \mathrm{z}_{\mathrm{j}}}
$$

where $\mathrm{z}_{\mathrm{i}}$ and $\mathrm{z}_{\mathrm{j}}$ are the charge numbers of ions I and J , $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{i}}$ and $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{j}}$ their activities in the solution, and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{i}, \mathrm{j}}$ the selectivity coefficients (sometimes also called interfering coefficients). The lower the values of $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{i}, \mathrm{j}}$, the higher the selectivity of the ISE. If $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{j}}$ (or $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{i}, \mathrm{j}}$ ) is very low, Equation (10.26) is equivalent to the Nernst equation which gives the voltage as a function of $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{i}}$. On the other hand, if the interfering ion J predominates, Equation (10.26) corresponds to the Nernst equation for the activity of $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{j}}\left(\mathrm{E}^{\circ}\right.$ is thus shifted).

Several models have been proposed to explain the form of Equation (10.26). For instance, in the case of insoluble membranes (silver salts), it is possible to relate the selectivity coefficient $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{i}, \mathrm{j}}$ to the ratio of solubility constants of insoluble salts such as MI and MJ (see the example of AgCl and AgI described by Morf). ${ }^{49}$

In this review we only present a model based on the ion mobilities as developed, for instance, by Eisenman. ${ }^{3-75}$ In this case, the ion J can penetrate into the SIC which is not doped initially with this species. To simplify the derivation, we consider the example of a $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$-SIC in contact with a $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$containing solution. The SIC is considered to be nonhomogeneous near the interface (see Figure 10.3). Equation (10.19) in this case becomes

$$
\varphi_{\beta}-\varphi_{\alpha}=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{~F}} \ln \frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Na}}(\alpha)\left[\tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Na}}-\tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Li}}\right]+\mathrm{C}^{\mathrm{o}} \tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Li}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Na}}(\beta)\left[\tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Na}}-\tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Li}}\right]+\mathrm{C}^{0} \tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Li}}}
$$

which is the junction potential between the bulk of the SIC and the interface. The Galvani potential between point $\alpha$ and the solution is obtained from Equation (10.4). With $\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Na}}(\alpha)= \mathrm{C}^{\circ}$, the total sensor voltage can be written as

$$
\varphi_{\text {SIC }}-\varphi_{\text {sol }}=\mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}}+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{~F}} \ln \frac{\left(\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Na}}(\beta)\left[\tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Na}}-\tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Li}}\right]+\mathrm{C}^{\mathrm{o}} \tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Li}}\right) \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Na}}(\mathrm{sol})}{\mathrm{C}^{\mathrm{o}} \tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Na}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Na}}(\beta)}
$$

The equilibrium constant of the reaction given by Equation (10.25) in this case reads

$$
\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{ex}}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Li}}(\beta) \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Na}}(\mathrm{sol})}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Na}}(\beta) \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Li}}(\mathrm{sol})}
$$

Combining Equations (10.29) and (10.17), we obtain

$$
\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Na}}(\alpha)=\frac{\mathrm{C}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Na}}(\text { sol })}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{ex}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Li}}(\text { sol })+\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Na}}(\text { sol })}
$$

Equations (10.28) and (10.29) lead to

$$
\varphi_{\mathrm{SIC}}-\varphi_{\mathrm{sol}}=\mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}^{\prime}}+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{~F}} \ln \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Na}}(\mathrm{sol})+\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{ex}} \frac{\tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Li}}}{\tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Na}}} \mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{Li}}(\mathrm{sol})
$$

This relation is similar to Equation (10.26) with a selectivity coefficient $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{Li}}$ given by

$$
\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{Li}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{ex}} \frac{\tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Li}}}{\tilde{\mathrm{u}}_{\mathrm{Na}}}
$$

This corresponds to the conclusion drawn by Eisenman: the selectivity coefficient is a function of the exchange equilibrium constant and of the ratio of the mobility of the interfering ion to that of the normal ion. The relation becomes more complex in the case of multiply charged ions, but the results remain qualitatively the same. Since this model does not take into account any kinetic aspects in the mixed ionic exchange at the interface, it must be considered as a first approximation only (a complete model is not available in the literature).

The above description may be used to explain the improvement of selectivity coefficients obtained on a crystallized membrane, as compared with glass membranes. The size of the conduction sites is nonhomogeneous in such amorphous materials, while it is well defined in size in a crystalline material. The mobility of interfering ions is therefore lower in the latter type of structure, which will favor a higher selectivity (see Equation [10.32]). This is well illustrated by NASICON, where the selectivity coefficient for alkali ions is improved by a factor of at least ten. ${ }^{57,74}$ The main interfering ions of common membranes are indicated in Table 10.1. The methods for the determination of the selectivity coefficients are described in specific papers. ${ }^{36,49,76-78}$

For $\mathrm{H}^{+}$interference, this model is not suitable, because protons do not penetrate into the membrane (their mobility is very low). The interfering phenomenon is a surface reaction with

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-340.jpg?height=711&width=642&top_left_y=126&top_left_x=427)
FIGURE 10.6. Mixed voltage in the case of two electrochemical reactions at the interface.

groups such as silanol groups, which show both acid and base reactions. Nevertheless, it is possible to model the interfering phenomenon in a similar way: the surface layer (gel) can be viewed as a thin protonic membrane. The selectivity coefficient $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{H}}$ is about 10 to 100 for a $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$membrane and about $10^{13}$ for a pH membrane (i.e., $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{Na}}=10^{-13}$ ).

## c. Influence of Electronic Conductivity

If there is no redox couple in the analyzed solution, potentiometric measurements are not influenced by the existence of an electronic conductivity in the SIC. The voltage is fixed only by the ionic exchange reaction at the interface between the SIC and the analyzed solution. Typical examples are $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$-based membranes.

On the other hand, if there are one or more redox couples in the solution, mobile ions and electrons both take part in the exchange, fixing the voltage. If the ion exchange is very fast, the voltage corresponds to that previously described in Section II.A.1.a. If the electron exchange is very fast, the Galvani potential is equal to

$$
\varphi_{\text {SIC }}-\varphi_{\text {sol }}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{~F}} \mu_{\mathrm{e}(\mathrm{sIC})}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{nF}}\left(\mu_{\mathrm{OX}(\mathrm{sol})}^{\circ}-\mu_{\text {RED}(\mathrm{sol})}^{\circ}\right)+\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{nF}} \ln \frac{\mathrm{a}(\mathrm{OX})_{\text {sol }}}{\mathrm{a}(\mathrm{RED})_{\text {sol }}}
$$

Generally, the voltage takes an intermediate value. As for a mixed voltage in traditional electrochemistry, the value is close to the Galvani potential of the couple which exhibits the fastest exchange rates (Figure 10.6). To avoid any interfering phenomenon by redox couples in the solution ( $\mathrm{O}_{2} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}$, etc.), it is better to use a membrane having a high ionic conductivity and a fast ionic exchange at the interface.

## d. Limit of Detection

Interferences can restrict the measurement of ion concentrations, if the interfering ions are highly concentrated or if their selectivity coefficients are high (see Equation [10.26]). For instance, in the preceding case, $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{Na}, \mathrm{H}}$ is about 100 for a glass membrane and if the pH of the analyzed solution is 7, the $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$detection limit (see Figure 10.7 for the definition of this parameter) is about $10^{-5} \mathrm{M}$. If the pH is 5 , this limit is about $10^{-3} \mathrm{M}$.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-341.jpg?height=421&width=725&top_left_y=122&top_left_x=389)
FIGURE 10.7. Definition of the detection limit for an ISE.

The solubility of the membrane in the solution is another limiting factor. The calculations are not specific to solid state electrochemistry; a good quantitative description is given, for example, in the case of AgX membranes. ${ }^{49}$ The order of magnitude of the detection limit of $\mathrm{X}^{-}$in the solution is about $\sqrt{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{s}}}$ ( $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{s}}$ is the solubility product of AgX ). For the general case the relation is more complex, but the conclusion remains qualitatively the same.

## 3. Gas Sensors (Electrochemical Gauges)

The Severinghaus and Bradley ${ }^{4}$ sensors use an ISE to measure an active gas pressure. The gas is supposed to be in equilibrium with an ion (typically protons) in an intermediate solution (carbonate solution for $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ measurement) which is in contact with the external medium, through a material permeable to the gas (TEFLON ${ }^{\circledR}$ ). They are described in specialized literature; we do not treat them here because they do not belong to the field of solid state electrochemistry. Some of the existing ISEs could be used in this way: for instance, a pH electrode can be used for $\mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{SO}_{2}$, and $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$; S²- ISE for $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$; Ag --ISE for HCN , F--ISE for HF ; and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$-ISE for $\mathrm{Cl}_{2} .{ }^{56}$

The variety of "traditional" solid state potentiometric gas sensors is smaller than for ISEs. Table 10.2 summarizes some of the developed electrochemical gauges. Most of them use crystalline materials with a conductivity sufficiently high at moderate temperature (a few hundred degrees centigrade) or at high temperature (above $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ). Generally, these sensors operate according to a reaction of the first kind, e.g., $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ using an oxide conductor; $\mathrm{I}_{2}, \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$, and $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$ using the respective halide conductor; and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ using a protonic conductor. Examples of devices are shown in Figure 10.8. Exceptionally, they are of the second kind, e.g., $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ or $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ using a silver or sodium conductor.

## a. Working Temperature

The choice of the working temperature depends on the conductivity of the SIC (impedance of the sensor) and on the electrode kinetics. With thin-film technologies, it is possible to reduce the SIC impedance strongly, but, unfortunately, the electrode kinetics is not affected. So, the working temperature of microsensors cannot be greatly improved with respect to that of macrosensors (traditional electrochemical gauges). The best way to increase the rate of the electrode reactions is to use catalytic materials. We may mention, for example, the use of such materials to lower the working temperature of oxygen gauges. ${ }^{104-108}$ A surface treatment has also been proposed to improve the performance of a $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$-based sensor at low temperature. ${ }^{108,109}$ In the case of a material having electronic and ionic charge carriers (mixed conductors), the electrode scheme is given on Figure 10.9. The rate of reaction is a function of the ionic conductivities of both materials (SIC and catalyst): it is fixed by the material exhibiting the lowest rate of reaction. In this field, oxides other than stabilized zirconia are now being proposed, for example, based on bismuth oxide. ${ }^{110,111}$ The redox stability domain of the SIC must be compatible with the usual pressure range. Generally, the lower pressure
