technology" of the New Sun Shine Project of the Agency of Industrial Science and Technology, the MITI. A new 10-year R\&D project with a budget of approximately Y14 billion was started. Targets of this project are (i) R\&D of 20 to 30 kW h high-performance ambienttemperature lithium batteries with long life and high energy density and (ii) research on the total system of battery application technology (Table 11.5). In order to extend the life cycle, to enhance the energy density, to enlarge the power output, to improve the reliability, and to scale up lithium secondary battery, LIBES is going to challenge to such difficult subjects as research on materials, design, and manufacturing technologies for large-capacity cells, and research and development of module batteries based on these technological elements.

## II. APPLICATIONS OF SOLID STATE IONICS TO BATTERIES

## A. HIGH-TEMPERATURE CELLS

The alkali metals lithium and sodium are attractive as battery anodes on account of their high electrode potentials and their low atomic masses, which together result in excellent values for the battery specific energy. In this chapter we consider batteries which utilize solids (fast ion conductors) or fused salts as electrolytes and which operate at temperatures of 200 to $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

Batteries that operate at elevated temperatures exhibit improved performance compared to ambient-temperature batteries. Figure 11.4 shows the cell voltage of two types of hightemperature batteries discharged at a high current. However, it is necessary to insulate them to prevent rapid heat loss to the environment.

## 1. Sodium Sulfur Batteries

The sodium sulfur battery, also known as the beta battery, owes its existence to the remarkable properties of $\beta$-alumina as a fast ion conductor for $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions. Discharge of the cell involves ionization of sodium atoms at the anode, followed by diffusion of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions through the wall of the $\beta$-alumina to the cathode where reduction of sulfur occurs. At temperatures of around $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ the ionic resistivity of $\beta$-alumina ( $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{O}-11 \mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ ) can be as low as 2 to $5 \Omega \mathrm{~cm}$, comparable to that of an aqueous sodium chloride solution, making it an ideal solid electrolyte for use in high-temperature Na batteries. The beta battery uses a molten Na negative electrode, the solid $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ion-conducting electrolyte, and a molten sul-fur-sodium polysulfide mixture as the positive electrode. At 300 to $350^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, which is the required temperature, the Na/S cells must be sealed from the atmosphere to prevent reaction with water and air.

In reality, $\mathrm{Na}^{+}-\beta-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is not a single compound, but may consist of two or more nonstoichiometric phases, each with its own characteristic crystal structure. The most impor-

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-374.jpg?height=531&width=613&top_left_y=122&top_left_x=471)
FIGURE 11.4. Cell voltage as a function of the depth of discharge for high-temperature batteries: (a) a $\mathrm{Na} / \mathrm{S}$ battery discharged at 19-A rate and (b) a $\mathrm{LiAl}-\mathrm{Al} / \mathrm{FeS}_{2}$ battery discharged at $15-\mathrm{A}$ rate. (From Julien, C . and Nazri, G.A., Solid State Batteries: Materials Design and Optimization, Kluwer, Boston, 1994. With permission.)

tant phase, which has the highest ionic conductivity, is $\beta^{\prime \prime}$-alumina of typical composition $\mathrm{Na}_{1.67} \mathrm{Mg}_{0.67} \mathrm{Al}_{10.33} \mathrm{O}_{17}$ and is the preferred phase for battery operation.

The cell voltage of a beta cell, with a central liquid sodium anode, discharged at a 19-A rate is shown in Figure 11.4a. Discharge of the cell involves ionization of sodium atoms at the anode

$$
2 \mathrm{Na} \Leftrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-},
$$

followed by diffusion of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions through the $\beta^{\prime \prime}$-alumina ceramic to the cathode where reduction of sulfur occurs in two steps:

$$
\begin{gathered}
2 \mathrm{Na}^{+}+5 \mathrm{~S}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{5} \\
\frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{5} \mathrm{Na}^{+}+1-\frac{\mathrm{x}}{5} \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{5}+\frac{2 \mathrm{x}}{5} \mathrm{e}^{-} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{5-\mathrm{x}} .
\end{gathered}
$$

The first reaction gives an open-circuit voltage of 2.08 V , whereas the second reaction gives 2.08 to $1.76 \mathrm{~V} .{ }^{8}$ In the first step, where the voltage is invariant, a mixture of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{5}$ and unreacted sulfur coexists. After complete conversion to $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{5}$, further reaction is a singlephase process in which $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{5}$ is converted progressively to $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{5-\mathrm{x}}$ by addition of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$. The cell voltage falls linearly with the Na composition and the discharge is stopped at 1.76 V , corresponding to the composition of $\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{3}$. Current characteristics are with an energy density of $200 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ and a capacity of 15 A h . The original interest has led to massive R\&D programs in Europe (Chloride Silent Power, British Rail, UK AEA, CGE, Brown Boveri Co.), in the U.S. (General Electric Co., Ford Motor Co., Dow Chemical Co., Silent Power), and in Japan (Toshiba Electric, Yuasa Battery Co.). These have been working on the development of beta batteries for EV traction and load-leveling applications. Currently, the fullsize cell is designed for a cycle of 5 h discharge and 7 h charge with a round-tip efficiency of $76 \%$. Individual cells have regularly shown lives of 1000 to 2000 cycles. Despite their very intensive development, sodium sulfur batteries have been abandoned for some time because of the inability to maintain a consistent quality. Discharge of the cell involves ionization of sodium atoms at the anode, followed by diffusion of $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$ions through the wall of the beta alumina to the cathode where reduction of sulfur occurs.

Today a new interest is being generated because high-quality, fine-grained materials are now routinely produced, and it has been found that the addition of $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$ helps refine the grain size and toughens the ceramic. Also, special sulfur electrode designs, including the use of graded resistivity shaped graphite felt current collectors, have improved the rechargeability of the sulfur electrode. A number of cell sizes and configurations have been considered, but 10 to 50 A h, inside-Na cell designs have become common in recent years. Efforts are under way in only three countries (Germany, U.S., and Japan) to develop this very promising battery for EV applications. ${ }^{17}$

A new beta cell has been developed by Beta-Power Inc. in the U.S. This cell is built with the concept of flat solid electrolyte disc instead of tubular design. This flat separator, typically 0.3 mm thick, is manufactured by a tape casting technique and allows high specific power of $600 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ and $1.65 \mathrm{~kW} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$ for a bipolar cell. This performance is a factor 5 to 6 higher than with the Na-S batteries of cylindrical cell design and is directly attributable to the thin flat plate design, which offers uniform discharge and excellent thermal conduction for sustained operation. ${ }^{18}$

Battery performance is more modest than that of an individual cell, owing in part to the need to provide a thermally insulating enclosure, and values around 100 and $130 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ are typical for a $50-\mathrm{kW}$ h battery. ${ }^{19}$ Such a battery should result in a range of about 125 miles for an EV under an urban driving schedule.

At Yuasa Battery Co. in Japan, a $50-\mathrm{kW}, 400-\mathrm{kW}$ h Na-S load-leveling battery has been constructed, ${ }^{20}$ and it has demonstrated an energy efficiency of $85 \%$ for cycle of 8 h charge and 8 h discharge, and a $48.5-\mathrm{kW} \mathrm{h} \mathrm{m}{ }^{-2}$ energy-area ratio. Test of a $10-\mathrm{kW} \mathrm{Na-S}$ battery has started in 1992 at Hitachi Works. The battery is consists of 192 cells of 252 Ah capacity each. This battery can operate at a current of 345 A and delivers an energy density of $60 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$.

## 2. Lithium Iron Sulfide Batteries

The lithium iron sulfide battery operates at about 400 to $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ using a fused halide eutectic electrolyte immobilized in the pores of a suitable separator. This battery displays a number of attractive features compared to the Na-S battery, including prismatic flat-plate construction, ability to withstand numerous freeze-thaw cycles, cell failures in short-circuit conditions, ability to withstand overcharge, and low-cost materials and construction techniques. The major disadvantage is a somewhat lower performance. Although this battery is suitable for both EV and load-leveling applications, recent attention has focused on battery designs suitable for EV propulsion. ${ }^{19}$

The most commonly used electrolytes are the $\mathrm{LiCl}^{-} \mathrm{KCl}$ binary eutectic and the LiF -$\mathrm{LiCl}-\mathrm{LiI}$ ternary lithium halides. With $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{Al}$ alloy anodes and $\mathrm{FeS}_{2}$ positive electrodes the discharge occurs in several discrete steps:

$$
\begin{gathered}
4 \mathrm{Li}+3 \mathrm{FeS}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{5}+\mathrm{FeS} \\
2 \mathrm{Li}+\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{5}+\mathrm{FeS} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FeS}_{2} \\
6 \mathrm{Li}+3 \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{FeS}_{2} \rightarrow 6 \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+3 \mathrm{Fe}
\end{gathered}
$$

giving an open-circuit voltage of $2.1,1.9$, and 1.6 V , respectively (see Figure 11.4b). The use of $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{Al}$ results in almost $50 \%$ decrease in the theoretical specific energy, but much better stability is achieved. Most development work has concentrated on the $\mathrm{LiAl} / \mathrm{FeS}$ couple, and the Varta Battery Company (Germany) has produced a series of 140-A h cells with a specific energy of $100 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ at a low discharge rate of $80 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}{ }^{-2}$, falling to density $50 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ at high rates of current density $250 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$.

The present version of $\mathrm{LiAl} / / \mathrm{LiCl}^{-} \mathrm{LiBr}-\mathrm{KBr} / \mathrm{FeS}_{2}$ utilizes a dense $\mathrm{FeS}_{2}$ electrode that only discharges to a stoichiometry corresponding to FeS (rather than to elemental Fe , which was the case of prior versions of this technology). ${ }^{17}$ The melting point of $\mathrm{LiCl}-\mathrm{LiBr}-\mathrm{KBr}$ at $310^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ permits cell operation at about $400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. These innovations have resulted in greatly improved capacity retention, and cells are cycled for more than 1000 times. These cells are also developed in the U.S. and manufactured by Eagle-Picher, by Gould, and by SAFT America. Cells of 150- to 350-A h capacity yield specific energies of 70 to $95 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ at a $4-\mathrm{h}$ discharge rate.

There are still a number of unresolved scientific questions about the chemistry of $\mathrm{LiAl} / \mathrm{FeS}$ cells and the mechanism of degradation and failure. In this system the separator is clearly a crucial component which must not only keep the electrode materials apart, but also allow good permeation of the electrolyte, and the most suitable materials are found to be boron nitride and zirconia in the form of woven cloths, but these are obviously very expensive options.

## 3. Sodium Chloride Batteries

The sodium chloride battery, which resembles the Na-S battery, is a recently developed high-temperature battery. ${ }^{22}$ The major differences between the two systems are the positive electrode, which is an insoluble metal chloride in molten $\mathrm{NaAlCl}_{4}$, and the temperature of operation, which is about $250^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The cell configuration is $\mathrm{Na} / \beta^{\prime \prime}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} / \mathrm{NaAlCl}_{4} / \mathrm{MCl}_{2}$, where M can be Fe , Ni , or possibly other transition metals. Both the $\mathrm{Na} / \mathrm{FeCl}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Na} / \mathrm{NiCl}_{2}$ cells have been shown to be electrochemically reversible, and they exhibit a number of features that may be considered as improvements over those of Na-S cells.

Individual $\mathrm{Na} / \mathrm{FeCl}_{2}$ cells have demonstrated above $130 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ and more than 1000 cycles, although their specific power is typically below $100 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$, particularly when at a low state of charge. Large EV batteries of about $25-\mathrm{kW}$ h capacity have been constructed and tested. ${ }^{22}$

The features of this system include a lower operating temperature, lower than for the beta battery, ability to withstand limited overcharge and overdischarge, cell failures in short-circuit conditions, better safety characteristics, and higher cell voltage. Disadvantages include a slightly lower specific energy, lower specific power due to the reduced ionic conductivity of $\beta^{\prime \prime}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ at the lower temperature of operation. There also have a high resistance of the positive electrode and poor wetting of the $\beta^{\prime \prime}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ by sodium at the lower temperature. On balance, $\mathrm{Na} / \mathrm{MCl}_{2}$ batteries appear to offer some attractive characteristics, and their development is being pursued.

The ZEBRA-Battery developed by AEG Co. in Germany is an advanced high-energy battery based on nickel chloride as the positive electrode and sodium as the negative electrode. The cell reaction

$$
2 \mathrm{Na}+\mathrm{NiCl}_{2} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{Ni}+2 \mathrm{NaCl}
$$

is fully reversible. No side reactions occur in the normal operations, a necessary condition for a long cycle life with stable capacity. Both electrodes are separated by a sodium ionconducting electrolyte, the ceramic $\beta^{\prime \prime}$-alumina, which develops a sufficient low resistance at temperature of about $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. At this temperature the cell shows a voltage of 2.58 V . The energy density of ZEBRA-Batteries amounts to $100 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ and $160 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$, a fairly high value to meet the required daily range of a city car. In bench life testing, seven batteries with about 2500 cells were fully charged and discharged. During this test less than $2 \%$ of the cells failed up to 1000 cycles.

## 4. Lithium Chloride Batteries

Similar designs have been used in lithium chloride batteries, which operate at a temperature of $650^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The cell has the form Li (liq.) $/ \mathrm{LiCl}$ (liq.) $/ \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$, carbon. The two electrodes, the liquid lithium anode and the porous carbon anode in which the chlorine gas is fed under pressure, are separated by a molten lithium chloride electrolyte. The overall cell reaction is

$$
\mathrm{Li}(\text { liq. })+1 / 2 \mathrm{Cl}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{LiCl}(\text { liq. }),
$$

with an EMF of 3.46 V . This system delivers a theoretical energy density of $2.18 \mathrm{~kW} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~kg}{ }^{-1}$ at the working temperature. The most serious problems with this system are, however, concerned with corrosion of cell components and the development of satisfactory seals.

## 5. Sodium-Sulfur-Glass Electrolyte

It is possible to replace $\beta$-alumina by a $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$-conducting borate glass, ${ }^{23}$ and other materials such as NASICON of composition $\mathrm{Na}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Si}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Zr}_{2} \mathrm{P}_{3-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ (with $1.8<\mathrm{x}<2.4$ ) have been crystal engineered to maximize conductivity and ease of fabrication. ${ }^{24}$

Dow Chemical Co. has manufactured a Na-S-glass battery in which the electrolyte is a $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$-borate glass formed by fibers. Cycle lives exceeding 500 cycles at $8 \%$ discharge have been obtained for individual cells, and a load-leveling battery has been designed which will use 12,500 gas-cooled cells of $0.8-\mathrm{kW}$ h capacity.

## B. POLYMERIC AND GLASS BATTERIES

Since Armand ${ }^{9}$ suggested that solid polymer electrolytes derived from PEO might be used as electrolytes in thin-film, solid electrolyte batteries, major efforts have been made to produce polymer electrolytes with high conductivities at room temperature. Polymeric electrolytes are formed by complexes between salts of alkali metals and polymers containing solvating heteroatoms. The most common examples concern complexes between PEO which forms the ( $\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}$ ) chains. The solvating heteroatom, here oxygen, acts as a donor for the cation $\mathrm{M}^{+}$. The anion $\mathrm{X}^{-}$, generally of large dimension, stabilizes the PEO- $\mathrm{M}^{+}$complex. The first polymer electrolytes had conductivities of less than $10^{-5} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, far too low for use in normal battery applications. Recently, however, electrolyte compositions have been produced which exhibit conductivity characteristics competitive with the properties of nonaqueous liquid electrolytes. ${ }^{25}$ These new materials have stimulated major development efforts in polymer electrolyte battery technology. The PEO-MX complexes may be considered as plastic electrolytes, i.e., a compromise between liquid and solid crystalline electrolytes. Most of the polymeric batteries are of the form $\mathrm{Li} /$ PEO-Li salt/IC, where IC can be an intercalation compound, but also a composite electrode including high amount of active intercalation material. From an examination of the temperature dependence of ionic conductivity for polymer complexes, one can remark the relatively high conductivity at room temperature of the complexes based on polyphosphazene, due to the low value of the vitreous transition temperature, $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{g}}$, and thus, to the flexibility of the chains of the polymer, in turn related to the character of the P-N bonds. ${ }^{26}$ Figure 11.5 shows the cell voltage of some polymer batteries which are intensively studied.

## 1. Lithium-Polymer Intercalation Compound Batteries

An all-solid state construction uses a Li salt-doped PEO film to separate the lithium electrode from an ion insertion-type positive electrode such as $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13},{ }^{25} \mathrm{TiS}_{2},{ }^{27} \mathrm{MnO}_{2}, \mathrm{Cr}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{8}$, or $\mathrm{LiV}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{8} .^{28}$ In many development cells, the positive electrode is a composite and consists of small particles of insertion compound bound together with polymer electrolyte and carbon, which improves its ionic and electronic conductivities, respectively. The composite electrode ( 50 to $75 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ thick) is deposited on a thin copper or nickel current collector less than $25 \mu \mathrm{~m}$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-378.jpg?height=621&width=809&top_left_y=124&top_left_x=377)
FIGURE 11.5. Discharge curves as a function of the cathode utilization of polymer batteries: (a) $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{PEO} / \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$, (b) $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{Al} / \mathrm{PEO} / \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$, and (c) $\mathrm{Li} /(\mathrm{PEO})_{8}\left(\mathrm{LiCF}_{3}\right)_{2} /\left[-\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{R})_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{R})_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{S}-\right]_{\mathrm{n}}$ cells having a theoretical capacity of about 3 mA h. (From Julien, C. and Nazri, G.A., Solid State Batteries: Materials Design and Optimization, Kluwer, Boston, 1994. With permission.)

and a film ( 25 to $50 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ ) of $\left[\left(\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \mathrm{O}\right)_{9} \mathrm{LiCF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]_{\mathrm{n}}$ polymer electrolyte completes the lithium cell. Another possibility is to roll the cell assembly.

A cell of this type using $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$ as the active material of the composite positive electrode $\mathrm{Li} /(\mathrm{PEO})_{9} \mathrm{LiCF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3} / \mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}+\mathrm{PEO}+\mathrm{C}$ would have a potential of about 2.8 V . During discharge, at current densities of around $1 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$, the voltage stabilizes at around 2 V . The practical energy density is in the order of $200 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$, the power density reaching $0.1 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~g}^{-1}$. The insertion process implies the movement of the sole $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ion alone, and this underlines the importance of the availability of an electrolyte with a purely $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$transport. This condition is not fulfilled in the case of $(\mathrm{PEO})_{9} \mathrm{LiCF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$, where the transport number of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}, \mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{Li}}+$, is approximately equal to $0.5 .^{29}$

The system examined in the French-Canadian Project considers the following sequence $\mathrm{Li} /(\mathrm{PEO})_{8} \mathrm{LiClO}_{4} / \mathrm{TiS}_{2}+\mathrm{PEO}, \mathrm{C}$, which is based on the intercalation of lithium into titanium disulfide lattice. ${ }^{25,30}$ The process implies the movement of $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions, this again making advisable the use of an electrolyte with an essentially pure $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$transport. This condition is not realized in $(\mathrm{PEO})_{8} \mathrm{LiClO}_{4}$, where $\mathrm{t}_{\mathrm{Li}}+$ is about $0.3 .^{31,32}$

Figure 11.5 shows the discharge curve of $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{PEO}-\mathrm{LiClO}_{4} / \mathrm{TiO}_{2}$ electrochemical cell as a function of the cycling number. Initial cell cycling was carried out between the voltage limits 3.0 and 1.2 V at a $\mathrm{C} / 8$ rate.* The primary discharge of anatase results in two distinct plateaus; the final composition is $\mathrm{Li}_{1.0} \mathrm{TiO}_{2}$, corresponding to a theoretical energy density of 565 Wh $\mathrm{kg}^{-1}$. The comparison of discharge curve of a $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{PEO} / \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$, with that of similar cells using an $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{Al}$ negative electrode, is shown in Figure 11.5. These cells have area of $3.8 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and theoretical capacity of $3.3 \mathrm{mAh} .{ }^{25}$

Polymer electrolytes which are based on simple polyethers are likely to have a wide range of electrochemical stability known as the stability window. Stability windows up to 4 V have been found for lithium-polymer electrolytes such as the $(\mathrm{PEO})_{8}-\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}$, the $(\mathrm{PEO})_{7}-\mathrm{LiBF}_{4}$, and the $(\mathrm{PEO})_{9}-\mathrm{LiCF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$ complexes. The main application envisaged is storage batteries for EV traction. Prototypes implemented to date have operated over several hundred cycles with energy efficiencies of 80 to $90 \% .^{25}$ An alternative type of a polymeric battery is one that contains an electronically conducting polymer such as polyacethylene or polypyrrole as a cathode material,

[^3]and an all-polymeric battery based on the material $[\mathrm{Na}(\mathrm{CH})]_{\mathrm{x}} / \mathrm{PEO}: \mathrm{NaI} /\left[(\mathrm{CH}) I_{3}\right]_{\mathrm{x}}$ has been described by Chiang. ${ }^{33}$ The polymer salt phases transform to resistive phases at lower temperatures, and consequently little development has been reported at normal ambient temperatures. Cells have been reported operated at $26^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ with $\mathrm{MoO}_{2}$ cathodes, from which it is projected that energy densities in the range 0.1 to $0.2 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$ may be achieved. ${ }^{25}$

Recently, Gould Co. introduced a primary lithium battery utilizing a solid electrolyte. This consists of a highly propylene carbonate (PC) plasticized film of PEO and $\mathrm{LiCF}_{3} \mathrm{SO}_{3}$. The cathode is based on $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$, while metallic lithium is used as the anode. To date the product appears to be limited in the marketplace to special applications. The first commercial available polymer electrolyte lithium rechargeable battery is being introduced by Valence Technology in San Jose (U.S.). The electrolyte consists of a radiation crosslinked polymer formed from a mixture of a liquid prepolymeric polyethylenically unsaturated compound, a radiation-inert ionically conducting liquid, and a lithium salt. The cathode is based on $\mathrm{V}_{6} \mathrm{O}_{13}$.

A solid-polymer "credit-card" battery has been developed by Yuasa Battery Co. in Japan. ${ }^{34}$ This 0.1 -mm-thick battery is composed of $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$, a solid-polymer electrolyte, and lithium, and has a volumetric energy density of $400 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$. It retains $70 \%$ of its capacity over 35 cycles of charge/discharge.

## 2. Solid Redox Polymerized Electrode Batteries

The superior mechanical properties of elastomeric electrolytes, combined with the simplicity of fabrication of thin separator films from these materials, has led to very active pursuit of solid state cells based on solid polymeric materials. However, the development of a novel class of solid state organic positive electrodes which not only operate on an entirely new principle for energy storage, but have demonstrated performances in a number of instances exceeding those for intercalation electrodes in solid state lithium and sodium batteries. These new electrodes have been called solid redox polymerization electrodes (SRPE) and are based on polysulfide polymers $\left[-\mathrm{S}(\mathrm{R})_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{R})_{\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{S}-\right]_{\mathrm{n}}$ where R is an organic radical such as $\mathrm{CH}_{3}, \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}$, $\mathrm{CF}_{3}$, etc. ${ }^{35-37}$ The redox mechanism for the positive electrode is in essence a redox dimerization/scission reaction which occurs in two steps as

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{RS}^{-}=\mathrm{RS}^{*}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \\
\mathrm{RS}^{*}+\mathrm{RS}^{-}=\mathrm{RSSR}+\mathrm{e}^{-} .
\end{gathered}
$$

The rate-limiting step in this mechanism is electron transfer. The standard rate constant is a strong function of R with higher rates of electron transfer being observed with the increasing electron-withdrawing nature of the organic moiety. ${ }^{36}$ The ability of thio-groups to undergo reversible dimerization/scission can be extended to polymers where oxidation leads either to reversible inter-intramolecular crosslinking or to reversible electro-polymerization.

All-solid state alkali metal/SRPE cells were constructed by sandwiching a polymeric electrolyte between the thin-film composite SRPE cathode and a thin foil of alkali metal, i.e., Li or Na. ${ }^{37}$ The theoretical energy densities for lithium- and sodium-based batteries using $(\mathrm{SRS})_{\mathrm{n}}$ of the SRPEs are of $990 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ (an open-circuit voltage of 3.0 V ) and $750 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ (an open-circuit voltage of 2.7 V ), respectively. The equivalent weight of this $(\mathrm{SRS})_{\mathrm{n}}$ electrode is $74 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{PEO} /(\mathrm{SRS})_{\mathrm{n}}$. Batteries are operating at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Such a cell, subjected to extended cycling tests at a discharge current density of $0.125 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$, corresponding to a rate of C/4.8, was operating at a steady-state energy density of approximately $260 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ and a steady-state power output of $160 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$. A prototype battery has been discharged at a high current density rate as shown in Figure 11.5c.

## 3. Alkali Glass Batteries

On the application level, two types of batteries using vitreous electrolytes have recently reached the predevelopment stage. The first type is the sodium sulfur battery, operating at $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in which the sodium and sulfur are in liquid form separated by 8 - $\mu$ m-thick hollow glass fibers. Each cell has a voltage of around 2 V and the vitreous electrolyte allows current densities of up to 2 to $4 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}{ }^{-2}$. Cells having capacity of 5 A h incorporating 8000 fibers have been built and tested over several operating years. The vitreous electrolyte in this case is sodium silicoborate containing a small amount of dissolved sodium chloride. The prototypes are developed in the U.S. by Dow Chemical for use in fixed electricity storage facilities. ${ }^{23}$

The second type of a glassy battery operating at ambient temperature is the lithium battery, involving a lithium metal anode and a titanium disulfide ( $\mathrm{TiS}_{2}$ ) cathode, with a vitreous solid state electrolyte. Such batteries have been developed first by SAFT in France, ${ }^{38}$ and then by Union Carbide in the U.S. ${ }^{39}$ They are commercialized by the two companies.

Lithium glasses appear to be promising alternatives to polymer electrolytes in lithium intercalation cathode systems, ${ }^{40}$ although the lack of deformability caused by their hardness can be a major problem. ${ }^{41} \mathrm{~A}$ battery based on a mixture with the composition $2.5 \mathrm{LiI}+\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{7}$ glass solid electrolyte having an ionic conductivity of $2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ has been developed by Eveready Battery Co. ${ }^{39}$ Such a battery has been designed for operating in a wide range of temperature from -55 to $125^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, which is a condition that traditional aqueous or nonaqueous systems, even polymeric batteries, cannot satisfy. The purpose of this battery is for CMOS memory backup for which energy density is not important. Questions of cell stability over years of in use service must be answered. A solid state cell having a $\mathrm{Li} / 5 \mathrm{LiI}$ $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{7} / \mathrm{TiS}_{2}{ }^{-} \mathrm{SE}$ configuration has an open circuit voltage of 2.5 V and furnishes a specific energy density of 150 to $200 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$. Such a battery, which is designed with 50 mA h capacity delivers continuous current densities up to $0.1 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}{ }^{-2}$ and allows pulsed current densities up to $10 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}{ }^{-2}$. When the temperature operating range is extended to $200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{LiI}$ is replaced by LiBr in the solid electrolytes. These cells are commercially available and are termed XR2025HT by the Eveready Battery Co. in the U.S. Performance of 40 mAh cells is highly satisfactory. Voltages of around 2 V are obtained.

## C. SOLID STATE PRIMARY LITHIUM BATTERIES

Commercially available solid electrolyte batteries use a lithium anode which is attractive because this metal is strongly electropositive. They generally exhibit high thermal stability, low rates of self-discharge (shelf life of 5 to 10 years or better), the ability to operate over a wide range of environmental conditions (temperature, pressure, and acceleration), and high energy densities of 0.3 to $0.7 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$. However, limitations associated with a complete solid state battery include relatively low power capacity due to the high impedance of most lithium-conducting solid electrolytes. The three commercial solid electrolyte battery systems are based on the solid electrolyte LiI, either formed in situ during cell manufacture or dispersed with alumina. They include the solid state chalcogenide glass battery which was described above.

## 1. Lithium-Iodine Cells

The lithium-iodine battery has been used to power more than 3.5 million cardiac pacemakers since its introduction in 1972. During this time the lithium-iodine system has established a record of reliability and performance unsurpassed by any other electrochemical power source.

The lithium-iodine battery has a solid anode of lithium and a polyphase cathode of poly2 -vinyl-pyridine (P2VP), which is largely iodine (at $90 \%$ by weight). The solid electrolyte is constituted by a thin film of LiI. The discharge reaction is given by

$$
2 \mathrm{Li}+\mathrm{P} 2 \mathrm{VP} \cdot \mathrm{nI}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{P} 2 \mathrm{VP} \cdot(\mathrm{n}-1) \mathrm{I}_{2}+2 \mathrm{LiI} .
$$

During the electrochemical reaction a thin membrane of LiI is formed and grown as the discharge proceeds. This cell has an open-circuit voltage of 2.8 V . The theoretical specific energy for the $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{LiI} / \mathrm{I}_{2}$ (P2VP) cell is $1.9 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$. The electrolyte ionic conductivity is $6.5 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and the energy density is 100 to $200 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1} .^{42}$ As the battery discharges, more lithium iodide forms and the ohmic resistance of the cell rises exponentially with discharge capacity. The behavior of the cathode is somewhat complex. As a cell discharges, the net reaction removes crystalline iodine from the cathode, causing a decrease in the resistance. This cathode resistance continues to decrease until all the crystalline iodine is consumed. If discharge proceeds beyond this composition into the single-phase region of the phase diagram, the cathode resistance increases rapidly and soon dominates the cell resistance occurs at a residual cathode weight ratio of about $8.4^{43}$

The volume change accompanying the cell discharge is $-12 \%$ if the cathode is $91 \%$ iodide by weight. This volume change may be accommodated by the formation of a porous discharge product or by the formation of macroscopic voids in the cell. Such batteries are used as power sources for implantable cardiac pacemakers, operating at $37^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. They are commercialized by Catalyst Research Co., by Wilson Greatbatch Inc., and Medtronic Inc. in the U.S. The lithiumiodine batteries have extended system lives up to 10 years for 120 - to $250-\mathrm{mA}$ h capacities. Power sources for portable monitoring or recording instruments have a nominal capacity of 15 A h or less, and most have deliverable capacities under 5 A h.

Batteries of medium capacities, i.e., up to around 1 A h , can be used for random-access memory power supplies in electronics. Similar batteries using $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Br}$ have also been built. The greater electronegativity of bromine gives rise to voltages of the order of 3.5 V and energy densities as high as $1.25 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$. Their practical application is, however, limited by the low conductivity of the LiBr films formed.

## 2. $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{LiI}-\mathrm{Al}{ }_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} / \mathrm{PbI}_{2}$ Cells

These batteries are recommended for low-rate operations and they are particularly suited for applications requiring long life under low drain or open-circuit conditions. Different cathodes have been used in these commercial solid state cells. A mixture of $\mathrm{PbI}_{2}+\mathrm{Pb}$ or $\mathrm{PbI}_{2}+\mathrm{PbS}+\mathrm{Pb}$ has been used, and a new system under development utilizes a mixture of $\mathrm{TiS}_{2}+\mathrm{S}$ or $\mathrm{As}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{3}$, which increases the energy density. The solid electrolyte is a dispersion of LiI and LiOH with alumina. Lithium ionic conductivities as high as $10^{-4} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ have been reported in such a dispersion at temperature of $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C} .{ }^{44}$

The discharge properties of these solid state batteries are characterized by an open-circuit voltage of 1.9 V and an energy density of 75 to $150 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~kg}{ }^{-1}$. A three-cell battery design delivers 6 V and offers a capacity of 140 mA h for pacemaker power sources. This system is manufactured by Duracell International. In typical CMOS memory applications the $350-\mathrm{mA} \mathrm{h}$ cell can be used to $1-\mathrm{V}$ cutoff.

## 3. $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{LiI}\left(\mathrm{SiO}{ }_{2}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right) / \mathrm{Me}_{4} \mathrm{NI}_{5}+\mathrm{C}$ Cells

This cell was evaluated for use in cardiac pulse generators and exhibited a voltage of 2.75 V and was projected to have an energy density of $0.4 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3} .{ }^{45}$ The cathode is a mixture of carbon and tetramethyl-ammonium penta-iodide ( $\mathrm{Me}_{4} \mathrm{NI}_{5}$ ).

## 4. Lithium Bromine Trifluoride Battery

Great advances have been made over the past decade based on the unconventional approach of combining alkali metals with strongly oxidizing liquid, e.g., $\mathrm{SO}_{2}, \mathrm{SOCl}_{2}$, or $\mathrm{BrF}_{3}$, which acts simultaneously as electrolyte solvent and cathode depolarizer. $\mathrm{BrF}_{3}$ is a very
reactive liquid at room temperature, and the concept of a $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{BrF}_{3}$ cell has appeared. ${ }^{45}$ The cell reaction has been represented by

$$
3 \mathrm{Li}+\mathrm{BrF}_{3} \rightarrow 3 \mathrm{LiF}+1 / 2 \mathrm{Br}_{2} .
$$

Thus no electrolyte salt is necessary because lithium was found to be stable in $\mathrm{BrF}_{3}$ due to the formation of the protective surface layer.

Upon cell activation a potential of 5 V is established which increases them to over 5.1 V , giving theoretical energy densities of $2680 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ and $4480 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$. A typical discharge of a $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{BrF}_{3} / \mathrm{C}$ cell is achieved at $5 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}{ }^{-2}$ current density and capacity of $5 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$ has been measured. The cell performance is controlled by the buildup of the reaction product layer at the anode-electrolyte interface, leading to an increase in impedance. The $\mathrm{BrF}_{3}$ electrolyte can also be modified by dissolution of various fluorides, e.g., $\mathrm{LiAsF}_{6}, \mathrm{LiPF}_{6}, \mathrm{LiSbF}_{6}$ or $\mathrm{LiBF}_{4}$. Such a cell is actually strongly developed by EIC Laboratories Inc. in the U.S.

## D. SOLID STATE SECONDARY LITHIUM BATTERIES

A question can be raised at this point as to the usefulness of a rechargeable solid state secondary cell with such a low mA h output. Matsushita Electric Industrial Co. (Japan) has developed a $20-\mathrm{mm}$-diameter secondary cell of the type $\mathrm{LiSn}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Bi}_{\mathrm{y}} / \mathrm{LiClO}_{4}$ in PC/C (activated carbon) which has a $1-\mathrm{mA}$ h rating. ${ }^{46}$ More recently, Bridgestone and Seiko have jointly developed a Li/polyaniline (AL2016) secondary cell with a capacity of 3 mA h .

At Eveready Battery Co. (U.S.) a $\mathrm{Li} /$ chalcogenide glass $/ \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$ carbon is under active development. ${ }^{47}$ The target of the R\&D is the CMOS memory backup market. This cell is based on phosphorous chalcogenide glasses $\mathrm{Li}_{8} \mathrm{P}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{0.25} \mathrm{~S}_{13.75}$ mixed with LiI and on a solidsolution composite electrode $\mathrm{TiS}_{2}$-solid electrolyte-black carbon with percentages by weight of $51: 42: 7$. The cathode capacity ranges from 1.0 to 9.5 mA . The cell packaging is a standard-sized XR2016 coin cell. The resistance of the cell at $21^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is between 25 and $100 \Omega$, depending upon where the cell is in the charge/discharge cycle. More than 200 cycles have been obtained in experimental cells. It seems that the phosphorous chalcogenide-based electrolytes have additional advantages beyond their plasticity and ease of fabrication and use. The use of vitreous electrolyte network formers $\mathrm{SiS}_{2}, \mathrm{~B}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{3}, \mathrm{GeS}_{2}$, etc., is suspect from the viewpoint of interface stability. ${ }^{47}$

## E. SECONDARY INSERTION CATHODE LITHIUM BATTERIES

Of particular interest for secondary-battery electrodes is the case of an electron donor system. The first experimental investigation of this concept includes the electrochemical reaction which takes place in a $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Li}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$ cell in the composition range $0 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq 1$ (see Figure 11.6).

The redox reactions may occur on the host cation, the host anion, or the guest species. For example, insertion of lithium into the oxospinel $\mathrm{Li}\left[\mathrm{Mn}_{2}\right] \mathrm{O}_{4}$ reduces the host cation to give $\mathrm{Li}_{1+\mathrm{x}}\left[\mathrm{Mn}_{1-\mathrm{x}}^{4+} \mathrm{Mn}_{1+\mathrm{x}}^{3+}\right] \mathrm{O}_{4}$, whereas insertion into the thiospinel $\mathrm{Cu}\left[\mathrm{Cr}_{2}\right] \mathrm{S}_{4}$ reduces the host anion to give $\mathrm{Li}_{\mathrm{x}}+\mathrm{Cu}^{+}\left[\mathrm{Cr}_{2} 3+\right] \mathrm{S}_{4}(7-\mathrm{x})-$. In the case of the chevrel phase $\mathrm{Li}^{+}\left(\mathrm{Mo}_{6} \mathrm{~S}_{8}\right)^{-}$, further insertion of lithium results in the formation of lithium trimers, thus reducing the guest species: ${ }^{48}$

$$
2 \mathrm{xLi}^{+}+2 \mathrm{xe}^{-}+\mathrm{Li}^{+}\left(\mathrm{Mo}_{6} \mathrm{~S}_{8}\right)^{-} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{x}\left(\mathrm{Li}_{3}\right)^{+}\left(\mathrm{Mo}_{6} \mathrm{~S}_{8}\right)^{-}+(1-\mathrm{x}) \mathrm{Li}^{+}\left(\mathrm{Mo}_{6} \mathrm{~S}_{8}\right)^{-} .
$$

Furthermore, the topotactic insertion/extraction reactions may occur by diffusion in onedimensional channels as in the hexagonal tungsten bronze, in two-dimensional as in layered transition metal dichalcogenides, or in three-dimensional as in the close-packed spinel structure. There are a large number of candidate materials which can be used in lithium insertion

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-383.jpg?height=616&width=587&top_left_y=122&top_left_x=483)
FIGURE 11.6. Schematic diagram of a solid state electrolyte battery providing power to an external circuit. The electrochemical redox process is that for an intercalation material, $\mathrm{Li}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$. (From Julien, C. and Nazri, G.A., Solid State Batteries: Materials Design and Optimization, Kluwer, Boston, 1994. With permission.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-383.jpg?height=772&width=1083&top_left_y=937&top_left_x=235)
FIGURE 11.7. Energy density vs. cell voltage of various Li/intercalation compounds. (From Julien, C. and Nazri, G.A., Solid State Batteries: Materials Design and Optimization, Kluwer, Boston, 1994. With permission.)

batteries. As shown in Figure 11.7, one can classified these materials as a function of the delivered cell voltage vs. Li. The most attractive family comes from the lithiated transition metal oxide which are now used in lithium metal-free batteries.

The use of the light, highly electropositive alkali metals, such as lithium, for the negative electrode and an ion insertion compound for the positive electrode gives attractive possibilities for high-energy density cells. The electrolyte generally consists of a solution of a lithium salt, e.g., $\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}, \mathrm{LiCl}$, or $\mathrm{LiBF}_{4}$ in an organic solvent such as dimethyoxyethane (DME), PC, or dimethyl-tetrahydrofuran (THF).

Lithium organic liquid electrolyte batteries have recently gained considerable importance as high-energy density power sources for a variety of terrestrial and space applications.

FIGURE 11.8. Cycle life vs. energy density of some secondary lithium batteries: (1) $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ (C-size Molicel), (2) $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ (SAFT), (3) $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ (AA-size Molicel), (4) $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$ (Eveready), $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mo}_{6} \mathrm{~S}_{8}$ (Molicel), (6) $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{NbSe}_{3}$ (AT\&T), and (7) $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2} / \mathrm{Li}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{C}_{6}$ (Sony Energytec). (From Julien, C. and Nazri, G.A., Solid State Batteries: Materials Design and Optimization, Kluwer, Boston, 1994. With permission.)

Commercially available organic liquid electrolyte primary batteries include $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{SO}_{2}, \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$, $\mathrm{Li} /(\mathrm{CF})_{\mathrm{x}}, \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{MnO}_{2}, \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{FeS}_{2}, \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{CuS}$, and $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{CuO}$. Prototypes of organic liquid electrolyte secondary batteries such as the $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ systems, depending upon cell sizes, can deliver specific energies of 90 to $140 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ and volumetric energy densities of 0.16 to $0.2 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$. A cell utilizing the THF-LiAsF ${ }_{6}(1.5 \mathrm{M})$ solution ran more than 225 cycles when cycle at a depth of discharge of $60 \%$. This cell, having a capacity of 5 A h , had a current density of $0.5 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2} .^{49}$ The characteristics of the major types of ambient-temperature lithium insertion cathode batteries are summarized in Table 11.6.

Rechargeable batteries of various types have now been developed around the world. They are considered in the following sections. The volumetric energy density in Wh dm ${ }^{-3}$ vs. cycle life of some advanced commercial battery systems is shown in Figure 11.8.

## 1. Li/TiS ${ }_{2}$ Battery

Most recent effort have been devoted to the development of small cells or batteries using $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Li}^{+}-\mathrm{LOE} / \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$ geometry where $\mathrm{Li}^{+}-\mathrm{LOE}$ is a lithium liquid organic electrolyte.

A spirally wound AA-size Li/TiS ${ }_{2}$ cell has been constructed by Grace Co. (U.S.). At $200-\mathrm{mA}$ discharge rate, 1 A h is delivered to $1.7 \mathrm{~V} .^{49}$ In C -size, a $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$ cell built by EIC Laboratories Inc. (U.S.), a capacity of 1.6 A h is obtained. This cell operates in the temperature range from -20 to $+20^{\circ} \mathrm{C} .^{50}$ Other systems are constructed using solid vitreous or polymeric electrolytes, as it has been shown above. A miniature cell was manufactured by Exxon in two sizes with capacities of 25 and 90 mAh .

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-385.jpg?height=745&width=781&top_left_y=120&top_left_x=386)
FIGURE 11.9. Electrochemical characteristics of Molicel employing $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ electrode (A-type), $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ electrode (B-type) compared to Ni-Cd cells. (From Julien, C. and Nazri, G.A., Solid State Batteries: Materials Design and Optimization, Kluwer, Boston, 1994. With permission.)

## 2. Li/MoS ${ }_{2}$ Battery

As the first commercially available rechargeable lithium power source system, the Li/MoS ${ }_{2}$-type battery (MOLICEL ${ }^{\text {™ }}$ ) is manufactured by Moli Energy Ltd. in Canada. This cell delivers performance and operating features far superior to conventional secondary batteries (Figure 11.9). This battery uses a lithium anode, a PC-based electrolyte solution, and a processed $\mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ cathode. ${ }^{47}$ The discharge reaction is

$$
\mathrm{xLi}^{+}+\mathrm{xe}^{-}+\mathrm{MoS}_{2} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{MoS}_{2} .
$$

The electrolyte is a nonaqueous solution which permits high electrolyte-to-electrode ion transfer. Sustained drain rates of several amperes at a cell voltage between 2.3 and 1.3 V can be obtained. The energy density is in the 60 - to $65-\mathrm{W} \mathrm{h} \mathrm{kg}^{-1}$ range at a discharge rate of $\mathrm{C} / 3$ (approximately 800 mA ). The total capacity of a C-size cell is 3.7 A h and the number of realizable cycles is dependent upon the charge and discharge conditions. Within a wide range of conditions a cell can be expected to deliver in excess of 150 complete cycles. ${ }^{52} \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ batteries are especially manufactured as the power source for pocket telephones in Japan.

## 3. Li-NbSe ${ }_{3}$ Battery

A more energetic lithium rechargeable system under study and development at AT\&T is the $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{NbSe}_{3}$ system, termed FARADAY cell. ${ }^{53}$ The AA-size cylindrical cell is designed for operating over 200 cycles at a typical current of 400 mA to a cutoff capacity of 0.7 A h . The cell reaction may be written

$$
3 \mathrm{Li}^{+}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-}+\mathrm{NbSe}_{3} \Leftrightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{3} \mathrm{NbSe}_{3} .
$$

The ability to incorporate three lithium gives a relatively high theoretical energy density of $1600 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$ for the Li-NbSe ${ }_{3}$ couple. A practical energy density of $200 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{dm}{ }^{-3}$ is achieved with the possibility of 350 cycles of charge/discharge.

## 4. $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{V}{ }_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ Battery

a $\mathrm{M}=$ military application, $\mathrm{C}=$ consumer, $\mathrm{S}=$ space.

[^4]A coin-type lithium- $\mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ battery to provide all the power necessary for single-cell memory backup of microprocessor-based electronic equipment has been developed by Matsushita Micro Battery Co. in Japan. The cathode is constructed by a mixture of vanadium pentoxide and $5 \%$ carbon black. During the discharge a depth of $\mathrm{x}=3$ is obtained with an average voltage of about 2 V . The battery has a relatively flat discharge curve at an output voltage of 3 V and an electrical capacity of approximately 36 mAh at a discharge rate of 1 mA . ${ }^{54}$ It also proved able to withstand up to 50 deep charge/discharge cycles and up to 1000 shallow charge/discharge cycles for which the depth of discharge set around $10 \%$. A C-size $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{V}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ cell is also constructed by SAFT in France (see Table 11.7). This cell delivers a capacity of $1.4 \mathrm{Ah} .{ }^{55}$

## 5. $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{MnO}{ }_{2}$ Battery

Many organizations (Moli Energy Ltd., Sony Energytec, Sanyo, Fuji, NTT, Matsushita, Varta, SAFT, etc.) embarked on ambitious programs to develop small rechargeable Li cells including a manganese dioxide cathode as a stepping stone to the ultimate goal of a technology scalable to EV-sized batteries.
$\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ is currently being investigated as a positive material for high-energy density, lowcost lithium secondary batteries. Although initial stages of the investigation showed that $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ had limited rechargeability, various improvements have been recently made. ${ }^{56-59}$ It was found that a composition of lithium containing manganese dioxide (CDMO) exhibits superior rechargeability, and this led to the development of a high-energy density flat-type lithium secondary battery. This type of cell is manufactured by Sanyo in Japan. CDMO is prepared from Li salts and $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ by a heat-treatment method, and the material structure consists of $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{3}$ and $\lambda-\beta-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ "composite dimensional manganese oxide". A recent report has shown that $\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{MnO}_{3}$ is an electrochemically inactive material and the $\mathrm{Li}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Mn}_{2-\mathrm{y}} \mathrm{O}_{4}(0 \leq \mathrm{x} \leq$
1.33) is a electrochemically spinel phase. The end members of this system are the stoichiometric spinel compound $\mathrm{Li}_{4} \mathrm{Mn}_{5} \mathrm{O}_{12}$ and the defect spinel $\lambda-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$.

Rechargeable Li-MnO2 AA-size cells are manufactured by Moli Energy Ltd. under the name MOLICEL ${ }^{\mathrm{TM}}$ (see Figure 11.9). This cell uses an electrolyte consisting of $1 \mathrm{M}-\mathrm{LiAsF}_{6}$ in PC/ethylene carbonate (1:1). A typical cycle life between 3.4 and 2.4 V has been achieved with 180 and 60 mA discharge and recharge currents, respectively. Minor capacity fluctuations throughout the cycle life have been detected at an operating temperature of $24^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

AA-size cells constructed by Battery Technologies Inc. in the U.S. show very good endurance tests. These cells accumulate up to 400 cycles on the $24-\Omega$ test and up to 250 cycles on the $10-\Omega$ test. ${ }^{60}$ The Li-MnO2 cells developed by Sanyo Electric Co. in Japan are termed ML 2430 and ML 2016 with nominal capacity of 70 and 20 mA h, respectively. About 200 cycles are obtained at a capacity of 45 mA . The flat-type battery ML 2430 with a nominal voltage of 3 V can operate over 3000 cycles at a low discharge capacity of 1 mA h . European efforts are also directed toward $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ secondary batteries. ${ }^{61}$ Researches are developed by SAFT in France and Varta in Germany (see Table 11.7).

## 6. Other Items

A new AA-size $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{Mo}_{6} \mathrm{~S}_{8}$ rechargeable cell has been developed to the prototype stage by Moli Energy Ltd. ${ }^{51}$ This cell can deliver about 2 W h of energy and is capable of a high-rate discharge up to about the 2C rate. This AA-size cell delivers $100 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ with a typical life of 200 cycles.

The material $\mathrm{LiI}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ is an example of a polyphase solid electrolyte. ${ }^{60}$ The intimate mixing of small particles of an ion-conducting phase with a nonconducting phase has been found to enhance the total conductivity. Although explanations for the mechanisms of lithium ion transport in these polyphase electrolytes remain controversial, there is general agreement that the mechanism is associated with the high specific surface area of the interparticle contact region. It should be noted that the $\mathrm{LiI}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ polyphase electrolyte attains a specific conductivity of $10^{-3} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ around $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, and at this temperature is sufficiently plastic to ensure good interfacial contact with solid insertion electrode materials. This material has been incorporated in the solid state cell $\mathrm{Li}-\mathrm{Si} / \mathrm{LiI}\left(\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}\right) / \mathrm{TiS}_{2}, \mathrm{Sb}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{3}$, Bi with operated at $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. Preliminary design studies indicated that practical energy densities of 200 and $500 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ could be realized with this cell, but only limited cycling data have been reported, and the high temperature of operation appears to be a disadvantage.

From a specific energy point of view, all-solid state batteries with insertion cathodes have the advantage that overall process only includes a minimum of components, but severe limitations were met in the design of batteries. In order to optimize the interfacial area and hence reduce its resistance, a composite electrode is commonly used in which the electronic and ionic conductors are mixed as powders and subjected to heat or pressure treatment in order to form the electrode. ${ }^{61}$

## F. LIQUID ELECTROLYTE PRIMARY LITHIUM BATTERIES

Four main groups of systems may be distinguished: polycarbon fluorides, sulfides, oxosalts, and oxides. Oxosalt batteries are in a range of commercial power sources based on silver chromate, $\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4}$, with the discharge process

$$
2 \mathrm{Li}+\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Ag}+\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{CrO}_{4},
$$

The nominal voltage is 3.5 V and specific energy of $200 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$ or $575 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{dm}{ }^{-3}$ to a $2.5-\mathrm{V}$ cutoff is estimated. Button cells are produced by SAFT. Sulfide electrodes have the advantage over the corresponding oxides that most of them are good electronic conductors, and hence sulfide-based batteries do not usually require the addition of carbon into the cathode. Batteries
based on cupric sulfide cells (three in series) have been developed for use in a cardiac pacemaker. Reduction of CuS takes place in two steps,

$$
2 \mathrm{CuS}+2 \mathrm{Li} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{~S},
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-388.jpg?height=479&width=656&top_left_y=353&top_left_x=452)
FIGURE 11.10. Discharge curves for lithium-organic batteries: (a) $\mathrm{Li} /\left(\mathrm{CF}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)_{\mathrm{n}}$ button cell under load of $13 \mathrm{k} \Omega$, (b) $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ button cell (Varta CR 2025) under load of $15 \mathrm{k} \Omega$, and (c) $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{CuO}$ button cell (SAFT LC 01) at $75-\mathrm{k} \Omega$ load. (From Julien, C. and Nazri, G.A., Solid State Batteries: Materials Design and Optimization, Kluwer, Boston, 1994. With permission.)

$$
\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+2 \mathrm{Li} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{~S},
$$

so that the discharge curve exhibits two plateaus at 2.12 and 1.75 V .
Figure 11.10 shows discharge curve of various lithium-liquid electrolyte primary batteries: $\mathrm{Li} /\left(\mathrm{CF}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)_{\mathrm{n}}, \mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$, and $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{CuO}$. These discharge curves have been obtained under load of $13-75 \mathrm{k} \Omega$.

## 1. Lithium-Polycarbon Fluorides Cell

Polycarbon fluorides of the general formula $\left(\mathrm{CF}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)_{\mathrm{n}}$ can be obtained by direct fluorination of carbon black or other varieties of carbon at high temperatures. A specific energy density of $2600 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{~kg}{ }^{-1}$ can be achieved with these materials. Lithium cells with polycarbon fluoride cathodes have an open-circuit voltage in the range of 2.8 to 3.3 V , depending on the composition of the cathode material. A typical cell reaction may be written

$$
n x \mathrm{Li}+\left(\mathrm{CF}_{\mathrm{x}}\right)_{\mathrm{n}} \rightarrow n \mathrm{C}+n x \mathrm{LiF},
$$

Cells based on polycarbon fluorides are manufactured commercially in various forms. This system is developed by Matsushita Electric Industrial Co. and is designed as a BR 435 cylindrical cell. New advanced cells constructed by Nippon Steel Co. use carbon fibers as electrodes and are found rechargeable batteries. Cells for military applications have been produced in the U.S. by Eagle Picher and by Yardney Electric. There are spiral-wound cylindrical cells with a largest cell capacity of 5 A h .

## 2. Lithium Oxide-Compounds Cell

In many cases the discharge mechanisms involved in lithium oxide cells are still not fully understood. The discharge reaction can be described as a formal displacement process,

$$
2 \mathrm{Li}+\mathrm{MO} \rightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{M},
$$

where MO is an oxide material such as $\mathrm{CuO}, \mathrm{MnO}_{2}, \mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ or $\mathrm{Pb}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$. The theoretical capacities of various oxides are listed in Table 11.8. $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ primary cells are manufactured by Sanyo as button cells. The open-circuit voltage is in the range 3.0 to 3.5 V and a practical energy density of $500 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{dm}{ }^{-3}$ is obtained. Varta has developed a CR 2025-type cell and SAFT has manufactured a LM 2020-type cell.

TABLE 11.8
ProductandTheoretical Capacity of Oxide Materials where
Displacement Reactions are Involved
| Active <br> material | Product | Capacity <br> (Ah kg $^{-1}$ ) |
| :--- | :--- | :---: |
| CuO | Cu | 670 |
| $\mathrm{MnO}_{2}$ | $\mathrm{Mn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ | 310 |
| $\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ | Bi | 350 |
| $\mathrm{~Pb}_{3} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ | Pb | 310 |


From Julien, C. and Nazri, G.A., Solid State Batteries: Materials Design and Optimization, Kluwer, Boston, 1994. With permission.

The Li/CuO button cell (LC01) constructed by SAFT exhibits a single step which may be attributed to the simple displacement reaction:

$$
2 \mathrm{Li}+\mathrm{CuO} \rightarrow \mathrm{Li}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Cu} .
$$

This $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{CuO}$ cell has an open-circuit voltage of 1.5 V and has the highest specific energy of all solid cathode lithium-based cells. Practical value of $750 \mathrm{~Wh} \mathrm{dm}^{-3}$ is obtained. The liquid electrolyte varies from manufacturer to manufacturer, but $\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}$ in dioxolane is very often used. The cylindrical cells manufactured by SAFT have practical capacities in the range 0.5 to 3.9 A h .

## G. SILVER AND COPPER BATTERIES

The development of silver or copper anode batteries as early as the 1960s was due to the existence of good silver or copper cation-conducting electrolytes and the relatively simple manufacturing processes involved.

## 1. Silver Cells

Crystalline solid electrolyte cells of $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ions were early reported by Takahashi and Yamamoto, ${ }^{62}$ Foley, ${ }^{63}$ and Owens ${ }^{64}$ in complete detail. Following the initial discovery of $\mathrm{Ag}_{4} \mathrm{RbI}_{5}$ solid electrolyte by Owens in 1970, a range of other related materials were developed. ${ }^{65}$ This material exhibits an unusually high ionic conductivity of $0.26 \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. This permits cell discharge at much higher current drains than those available with LiI-based cells. The best-known example is the battery operating according to the electrochemical chain $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{RbAg}_{4} \mathrm{I}_{5} / \mathrm{RbI}_{3}$, giving an open-circuit voltage of 0.66 V at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and a practical energy density of $4.4 \mathrm{~W} \mathrm{~h} \mathrm{~kg}^{-1}$. The selection of $\mathrm{RbI}_{3}$ as the cathode material rather than iodine is due to the parasitic reaction possible between the iodine and the electrolyte, leading to the formation of a poorly conducting film.

Silver cell using fast ion-conducting glasses in the systems $\mathrm{AgI}-\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{Ch}-\mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{Ch}$, where Ch is $\mathrm{O}, \mathrm{S}$, or Se , have been characterized. ${ }^{66}$ The glass with composition $7 \mathrm{AgI}-1 \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{Se}^{-2} \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{Se}_{5}$ exhibits an ionic conductivity of $10^{-2} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at room temperature. Value of open-circuit voltage of the cell $\mathrm{Ag} / 7 \mathrm{AgI}-1 \mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{Se}-2 \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{Se}_{5} / \mathrm{I}_{2}+\mathrm{C}$ implies that the cell reaction is essentially
the same as that of $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{AgI} / \mathrm{I}_{2}{ }^{62}$ Both anode and cathode materials were composite, i.e., a mixture of active material and solid electrolyte in powder form. A current density of $1 \mathrm{~mA} \mathrm{~cm}{ }^{-2}$ can be used without serious polarization. The reason for the voltage decrease at higher current densities is attributed to cathodic and anodic polarization; improving the electrode assembly could serve the higher current densities in such a cell. ${ }^{67}$

Another type of solid state cell utilizing $\mathrm{Ag}^{+}$ion-conducting glasses has been reported by Minami. ${ }^{68}$ This cell is constructed with a $\mathrm{NbS}_{2}$ intercalation cathode in the pellet form, and a flash-evaporated film of the glass $\mathrm{AgI}-\mathrm{Ag}_{2} \mathrm{O}-\mathrm{B}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ which has a ionic conductivity of $10^{-2} \mathrm{~S} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$ at room temperature. The open-circuit voltage ranged from 0.35 to 0.39 V . The discharge-charge cycles at current density of $3.3 \mu \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$ were repeated several times, and no degradation was observed under such conditions. This type of cell is a secondary battery. ${ }^{68}$

Organo-mineral compounds such as $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{4} \mathrm{NI}_{9}$ or $\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{4} \mathrm{NI}_{5}$ can be used to increase the energy density slightly. Furthermore, the ionic conductivity of the reaction products $\left(\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{4} \mathrm{~N}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Ag}_{13} \mathrm{I}_{15}$ is high, offering good charge transfer continuity. ${ }^{69}$ The $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{I}_{2}$ batteries were inherently deficient in two significant areas, voltage and size; consequently no commercial application resulted.

## 2. Copper Cells

It is possible to replace silver by copper in many of the silver electrolytes. Cells based on a particular $\mathrm{CuI} /$ organic iodide electrolyte have been described. ${ }^{70}$ Those involving $\mathrm{Cu}_{16} \mathrm{Rb}_{4} \mathrm{I}_{7} \mathrm{Cl}_{13}$ have been recently reviewed. ${ }^{71}$

The first report for the cells with copper ion conductors was presented by Takahashi and Yamamoto. ${ }^{72}$ They examined four types of solid state cells with the following electrochemical chains: $\mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{X} / \mathrm{X} / \mathrm{CuBr}_{2}+\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Graphite}$, and $\mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{X} / \mathrm{X} / \mathrm{Ch}, \mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{Graphite}$, where X is $7 \mathrm{CuBr}- \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{~N}_{4} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Br}$ and Ch is a chalcogen ( $\mathrm{S}, \mathrm{Se}$. or Te ). The open circuit voltages of these cells were $0.8,0.448,0.373$, and 0.258 V , respectively, at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The value in the initial open-circuit voltage of the second cell is in good agreement from the free-energy variation of the reaction

$$
\mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{CuBr}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CuBr} .
$$

However, the cell performance is time dependent, the open-circuit voltage decreased gradually, and the cell resistance increased. The anode capacity of these cells was 17 mA h , and the cathode capacities 340,130 , and 84 mA h, respectively. The rapid decrease in the potential of cell including sulfur discharged at a rate of $54 \mu \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$ is due to the high resistance of sulfur. The best performance was obtained with cell including selenium; a capacity of 26 mA $h$ was found to the cut-off voltage of 0.26 V , corresponding to a cathode efficiency of $18 \%$.

Recently, copper solid electrolyte cells have been designed using intercalation cathodes. The first approach for the rechargeable cells with a copper ion conductor has been done by Lazzari and Razzini ${ }^{73}$ and by Scrosati and Voso. ${ }^{74}$ The cells consisted of a high copper ionconducting solid, $0.94 \mathrm{CuBr}-0.06 \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{~N}_{4} \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{Br}$, and an intercalation cathode $\mathrm{Cu}_{0.4} \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$, and an intercalation anode $\mathrm{Cu}_{0.8} \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$. In this cell, the kinetic limitations noticed with copper electrodes should be avoided by the use of intercalated electrodes on both sides with resulting improved cycling capabilities. Discharges at a rate of $20 \mu \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$ have been carried out over 135 cycles without any appreciable deterioration.

Kanno et al. ${ }^{75}$ have studied a series of cells using intercalation metal chalcogenide material, $\mathrm{MX}_{2}$, as cathode and Chevrel phase as anode, of the type $\mathrm{Cu}_{4} \mathrm{Mo}_{6} \mathrm{~S}_{8} / \mathrm{Rb}_{4} \mathrm{Cu}_{16} \mathrm{I}_{6.8} \mathrm{Cl}_{13.2} / \mathrm{MX}_{2}$. Cathodes were composite, the anode was a mixture of equal amounts of $\mathrm{Cu}_{4} \mathrm{Mo}_{6} \mathrm{~S}_{8}$ and electrolyte, and the cathode was fabricated in the proportion 2:3 of $\mathrm{MX}_{2}$ compound and electrolyte. The discharge capacity of $\mathrm{NbS}_{2}$ is remarkable. The cathode utilization is over $30 \%$ in e/ $\mathrm{NbS}_{2}$ to the cut-off voltage of 0.3 V . According to the electron diffraction study, $\mathrm{Cu}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{NbS}_{2}(0<\mathrm{x}<0.9)$ forms a continuous nonstoichiometric compound over the whole composition range, showing ordered and disordered arrangements in copper
metal, depending on composition and temperature. ${ }^{76}$ So, the formation of a wide-range solid solution is advantageous to the efficiency of a secondary battery.

The electrochemical chain $\mathrm{Cu} / \mathrm{Rb}_{4} \mathrm{Cu}_{16} \mathrm{I}_{6.8} \mathrm{Cl}_{13.2} / \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$ constitutes a cell with a capacity of 7.8 mA h using $0.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{TiS}_{2}$ an was discharged at a rate of $75 \mu \mathrm{~A} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2}$. During the discharge at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ the cell voltage is 0.59 V and a current of $10 \mu \mathrm{~A}$ allows 100 cycles. $7^{77}$ The copper cell manufactured by Matsushita in Japan has a life of up to 300 cycles. Japan Synthetic Rubber Co. received attention for a solid state secondary cell based on $\mathrm{RbCu}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{3} \mathrm{I}_{2} .{ }^{78}$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-391.jpg?height=581&width=931&top_left_y=452&top_left_x=311)
FIGURE 11.11. Schematic representation of a metal-free lithium battery (or rocking-chair battery) during the discharge. The $\mathrm{Li}^{+}$ions rock between the two intercalation materials: $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Li}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{C}_{6}$ electrodes. The immediate advantages expected for such a battery are the high energy density and the safety behavior. (From Julien, C. and Nazri, G.A., Solid State Batteries: Materials Design and Optimization, Kluwer, Boston, 1994. With permission.)

## III. LITHIUM METAL-FREE RECHARGEABLE BATTERIES

## A. PRINCIPLE

During the past 15 years nonaqueous lithium cells have been developed especially in the $3-\mathrm{V}$ primary systems. The practical lithium cells, such as $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{MnO}_{2}, \mathrm{Li} /(\mathrm{CF})_{\mathrm{n}}$, and so forth, have been expanding their use in electronic devices.

It is now recognized that ambient-temperature nonaqueous rechargeable lithium cells using lithium metal as the negative electrode exhibit problems which hinder their wide utilization in the consumer market. These problems arise mainly from three factors:
(i) The short cycle life for deeply discharged cells; the dendritic formation during charging creates dead-lithium which reduces considerably the battery cycle life. ${ }^{79}$ The dead-Li, which is electrochemically inactive, is reported to be chemically active; ${ }^{80}$ the freshly deposited lithium dendrites react with the electrolyte and become isolated from the bulk lithium metal, resulting in lower coulombic efficiencies of lithium electrodes. ${ }^{79}$ The main result is that Li batteries generally have a lower cycle life than Ni-Cd cells. Even in commercial $\mathrm{Li} / \mathrm{MoS}_{2}$ there is a three- to fourfold stoichiometric excess of Li compared to that needed to fully intercalate the cathode. ${ }^{81}$ If the Li cycling efficiency is $99 \%$, then $1 \%$ of the cycled Li is lost on each cycle. Therefore, to obtain 200 deep discharge cycles, at least a threefold excess of Li is required.
(ii) The high cost of the technology due to the need for assembly equipment to be located in dry rooms, the relatively expensive separators, and the need for excess Li metal.
(iii) The unsafe operating characteristics due to the high reactivity of lithium metal. ${ }^{82}$

To alleviate this problem, an innovative secondary lithium cell system (Figure 11.11) has been proposed that uses another intercalation compound as the negative electrode. ${ }^{83-94}$ Among the alternative materials that could replace lithium metal, carbon provides the best compromise between large specific capacity and reversible cycling behavior, ${ }^{90,91}$ but other materials have also been proposed (see Table 11.9).

TABLE 11.9
Characteristics of Some Lithium Metal-Free Rechargeable Batteries

| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{WO}_{2}$ | $\mathrm{LiClO}_{4}-\mathrm{PC}$ | $\mathrm{TiS}_{2}$ | 0.1 | 68 | Rome Univ. |
| $\mathrm{MoO}_{2}$ | $\mathrm{LiAsF}_{6}-\mathrm{PC}$ | $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ | 0.1 | - | AT\&T |
| $\mathrm{WO}_{2}$ | $\mathrm{LiAsF}_{6}-\mathrm{PC}$ | $\mathrm{LiCoO}_{2}$ | 0.1 | - | AT\&T |
| $\mathrm{Li}_{x} \mathrm{C}_{6}$ | Li-HPE ${ }^{\text {b }}$ | $\mathrm{LiMn}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}$ | 0.8 | 800 | AT\&T |

|  |  |  |  |  |  |

| ${ }^{c}$ Thin-film battery. |  |  |  |  |  |
