## I. CHARGE TRANSFER AND THE NATURE OF OVERPOTENTIALS IN SOLID STATE ELECTROCHEMISTRY

During the last 3 decades solid electrolyte galvanic cells have been studied intensively both for sensor applications ${ }^{1-5}$ and for their potential use as power-producing devices. ${ }^{6}$ Other emerging applications include their use for chemical cogeneration, i.e., the simultaneous production of power and chemicals ${ }^{7-12}$ and for enhancing and controlling the catalytic properties of metal and metal oxide catalysts via the effect of non-faradaic electrochemical modification of catalytic activity (NEMCA) or in situ controlled promotion of catalysis. ${ }^{13-27}$ All these applications depend crucially on electrocatalytic (net charge transfer) reactions occurring primarily at the solid electrolyte/electrode/gas three-phase boundaries (tpb) and on catalytic (no net charge transfer) reactions occurring primarily on the gas-exposed electrode surface. Improving our current understanding of electrocatalysis and catalysis in solid electrolyte galvanic cells is of considerable theoretical and practical importance. Some fundamental electrochemical definitions and relations of importance for this subject are introduced and discussed in this chapter. For a more complete treatment see Chapters 2 and 5 of this handbook.

## A. GENERAL CONSIDERATIONS

The operating principle of a current state-of-the-art power-producing solid electrolyte fuel cell is shown schematically in Figure 13.1. The positive electrode (cathode) acts as an electrocatalyst, i.e., it promotes the electrocatalytic (net charge transfer) reduction of $\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ to $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ :

$$
\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{O}^{2-}
$$

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-443.jpg?height=587&width=799&top_left_y=92&top_left_x=382)
FIGURE 13.1. Operating principle of a solid oxide fuel cell. (Reprinted with permission from Vayenas, C.G., Bebelis, S., and Kyriazis, C.C., CHEMTECH, 1991, 21, 422. Copyright 1991 The American Chemical Society.)
or, in Kröger-Vink notation:

$$
\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+2 \mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{\bullet \bullet}+4 \mathrm{e}^{\prime} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{O}}
$$

where $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}^{*}$ stands for an $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ vacancy in the yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) lattice and $\mathrm{O}_{\mathrm{O}}$ denotes an oxygen anion $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ in the lattice.

Although several metals, such as Pt and Ag , can also act as electrocatalysts for Reaction (13.1), the most commonly used cathodic electrocatalysts in solid oxide fuel cells (SOFC) are perovskites such as $\mathrm{La}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Sr}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{MnO}_{3-\delta}$ and $\mathrm{La}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Sr}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{CoO}_{3-\delta}$. These materials are mixed ionic-electronic conductors (for a more extensive discussion see Chapter 7 of this handbook) and thus the electrocatalytic sites are not restricted to the geometric tpb, but can also exist on the gas-exposed electrode surface as well, forming an electrocatalytically active zone which can, in principle, extend over the entire gas-exposed electrode surface.

The negative electrode (anode) acts as an electrocatalyst for the reaction of $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ with the fuel, e.g., $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ :

$$
2 \mathrm{H}_{2}+2 \mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$

A Ni-YSZ cermet is used as the anodic electrocatalyst in state-of-the art SOFC for two reasons: first because Ni is an active electrocatalyst for Reaction (13.3) and the porous cermet structure consisting of Ni and YSZ particles, typically $1 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ in size, has a large tpb length, and the electrocatalytic Reaction (13.3) takes place at the tpb. Consequently the use of Ni in a Ni-YSZ cermet to carry out electrocatalytic reactions is analogous to the use of Ni or other metals in a highly dispersed form on $\mathrm{SiO}_{2}$ or $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ (supported catalysts) to carry out catalytic reactions. ${ }^{28}$ The second reason for using a Ni-YSZ cermet as the anode is that Ni is also an active catalyst for the steam-reforming reaction:

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}
$$

and for the water-gas-shift reaction:

$$
\mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}
$$

This permits efficient SOFC operation with $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$, and also natural gas, as the fuel. Direct electrocatalytic $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ oxidation at the tpb is too slow for practical SOFC units with any known electrocatalyst, but, due to the catalytic properties of the Ni surface, $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ is converted to $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ via Reactions (13.4) and (13.5) and the latter is then easily oxidized electrocatalytically at the tpb.

Fuel cells such as the one shown in Figure 13.1 convert $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ to $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ or $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ to $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ and produce electrical power with no intermediate combustion cycle. Thus their thermodynamic efficiency compares favorably with thermal power generation which is limited by Carnot-type constraints. One important advantage of solid electrolyte fuel cells is that due to their high operating temperatures (typically 800 to $1000^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), they offer the possibility of "internal reforming" via Reactions (13.4) and (13.5) which allows for the use of fuels such as $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$ or natural gas without a separate external reformer.

## B. THERMODYNAMIC CONSIDERATIONS

The maximum work obtainable from an isothermal continuous process is the negative of the Gibbs free energy change between the product and reactant streams -ýg. When an oxidation reaction, e.g.,

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{H}_{2}+1 / 2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \\
\mathrm{CH}_{4}+2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
\end{gathered}
$$

is carried out in a fuel cell, the useful electrical work produced tends to the upper limit of -ýG when reversible operation is approached, i.e., when the operating cell voltage $E$ approaches the open-circuit reversible voltage $\mathrm{E}_{\text {rev }}$ :

$$
\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{rev}}=-\Delta \mathrm{G} / \mathrm{nF}
$$

where n is the number of electrons involved in the oxidation reaction, e.g., 2 for Reaction (13.6) and 4 for Reaction (13.7).

The fuel cell efficiency $\varepsilon$ is defined by:

$$
\varepsilon=\frac{W}{-\Delta H^{0}}=\frac{E}{E_{T H}}=\frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H^{0}} \cdot \frac{E}{E_{r e v}}
$$

where $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{TH}}$ is the "thermoneutral" voltage defined as $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{TH}}=\left(-\mathrm{y} \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\right) / \mathrm{nF}$. According to Equation (13.9), $\varepsilon$ can exceed unity for reactions with a positive entropy change ý $S^{\circ}$ such as the ammonia oxidation to NO. ${ }^{10}$ Under such operating conditions ( $\varepsilon>1$ ) a fuel cell absorbs heat from the environment instead of producing heat.

When the external resistive load of the fuel cell is finite and electrical power is produced, then in general the operating voltage E drops below the reversible voltage $\mathrm{E}_{\text {rev }}$ and the difference $\eta$ :

$$
\eta=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{rev}}-\mathrm{E}
$$

is called the cell overpotential (Figure 13.2).
One of the key problems in electrochemical power-producing devices, which hampered commercialization for years, is the thorough understanding and minimization of cell overpotential or "polarization."

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-445.jpg?height=876&width=1277&top_left_y=92&top_left_x=141)
FIGURE 13.2. Typical current-voltage curve of a fuel cell obtained by varying the external load. (Reprinted with permission from Debenedetti, P.G. and Vayenas, C.E., Chem. Eng. Sci., 1983, 38, 1817. Copyright 1983 Elsevier Science Ltd.)

## C. TYPES OF OVERPOTENTIAL

The cell overpotential $\eta$ can be considered as the sum of three major components termed ohmic overpotential $\eta_{\text {ohm }}$, concentration overpotential $\eta_{\text {conc }}$, and activation overpotential $\eta_{\text {act }}$ :

$$
\eta=\eta_{\text {ohm }}+\eta_{\text {conc }}+\eta_{\text {act }}
$$

The ohmic overpotential can be measured via ac impedance spectroscopy or via the current interruption technique in conjunction with a recording oscilloscope and is proportional to the cell current I. It is due to the ohmic resistance $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{el}}$ of the electrodes, of the solid electrolyte $R_{i}$, and the electrode-electrolyte contact resistance $R_{c}$ :

$$
\eta_{\text {ohm }}=\mathrm{I}\left(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{el}}+\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{i}}+\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{c}}\right)
$$

By introducing the current density i ( $\mathrm{A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}$ ) and the area-specific resistances, $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e} \mathrm{l}}^{\prime}, \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}$, and $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{c}}^{\prime}\left(\Omega \cdot \mathrm{cm}^{2}\right)$, one can rewrite Equation (13.12) as:

$$
\eta_{\mathrm{ohm}}=\mathrm{i}\left(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{el}}^{\prime}+\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{i}}^{\prime}+\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{c}}^{\prime}\right)
$$

To minimize the ohmic overpotential, one has to use thin, highly conductive solid electrolytes and highly conductive electrodes with a good adherence to the solid electrolyte to minimize the contact resistance.

The cell concentration overpotential $\eta_{\text {conc }}$ is the sum of the concentration overpotentials at the anode and at the cathode and is caused by slow mass transfer between the gas phase and the tpb. For an SOFC operating on $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ fuel it can be expressed as:

$$
\eta_{\text {conc }}=\frac{-\mathrm{RT}}{4 \mathrm{~F}}\left[\ln \left(1-\frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{c}}}\right)+2 \ln \left(1-\frac{\mathrm{i}}{\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{a}}}\right)\right]
$$

where $i_{\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{a}}$ and $i_{\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{c}}$ are the anodic and cathodic limiting current densities. When mass transfer in the gas phase is rate limiting, then $\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{a}}$ and $\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{c}}$ can be computed for many geometries of the anodic and cathodic compartments via standard analytical or empirical mass transfer correlations. ${ }^{30,31}$ When surface diffusion on the electrode surface to the tpb is rate limiting then $\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{a}}$ and $\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{c}}$ must be measured experimentally. The concentration overpotential can be minimized by appropriate design of the anodic and cathodic compartments and by the use of porous electrodes. In this way, limiting current density values $\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{a}}$ and $\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{c}}$ of at least 2 to $3 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}$ can be achieved so that in view of Equation (13.13), $\eta_{\text {conc }}$ can be rather small $(<100 \mathrm{mV})$ even for current densities up to $0.7 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}$, which is close to the maximum current densities obtained with state-of-the-art SOFC units. ${ }^{32}$ In general, due to the high values of gaseous and surface diffusivities at the operating temperature of SOFCs relative to liquidphase diffusivities, the concentration overpotential is a less serious problem in SOFCs than in low-temperature aqueous electrolyte fuel cells.

The cell activation overpotential $\eta_{\text {act }}$ is the sum of the activation overpotentials at the anode and cathode and is caused by slow electrocatalytic (charge transfer) reactions at both electrodes. The anodic and cathodic activation overpotentials, $\eta_{\text {act }, \mathrm{a}}$ and $\eta_{\text {act }, \mathrm{c}}$, respectively, can be related to the current density i via the Butler-Volmer equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{i}=\mathrm{i}_{0, \mathrm{a}}\left[\exp \left(\frac{\alpha_{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{a}} \mathrm{~F} \eta_{\mathrm{act}, \mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{RT}}\right)-\exp \left(\frac{-\alpha_{\mathrm{c}, \mathrm{a}} \mathrm{~F} \eta_{\mathrm{act}, \mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{RT}}\right)\right] \\
& \mathrm{i}=\mathrm{i}_{0, \mathrm{c}}\left[\exp \left(\frac{\alpha_{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{c}} \mathrm{~F} \eta_{\mathrm{act}, \mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{RT}}\right)-\exp \left(\frac{-\alpha_{\mathrm{c}, \mathrm{c}} \mathrm{~F} \eta_{\mathrm{act}, \mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{RT}}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where $i_{0, \mathrm{a}}, i_{0, \mathrm{c}}$ are the exchange current densities of the anode and cathode, respectively, $\alpha_{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{a}}$ and $\alpha_{\mathrm{c}, \mathrm{a}}$ are the anodic ( $\mathrm{i}>0$ ) and cathodic ( $\mathrm{i}<0$ ) charge transfer coefficients of the anode, and $\alpha_{\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{c}}$ and $\alpha_{\mathrm{c}, \mathrm{c}}$ are the anodic and cathodic charge transfer coefficients of the cathode. The anodic and cathodic charge transfer coefficients $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{c}}$ depend on the electrocatalytic reaction mechanism and typically take values between zero and two. ${ }^{33,34}$ A zero value (a rather rare case) implies that the rate-limiting step is catalytic or in general involves no net charge transfer, in which case the term chemical overpotential can also be used.

The exchange current densities $\mathrm{i}_{0, \mathrm{a}}$ and $\mathrm{i}_{0, \mathrm{c}}$ of the anode and cathode, respectively, are of crucial importance as they determine the magnitude of $\eta_{\text {act, a }}$ and $\eta_{\text {act, }}$ via Equation (13.14). A good electrocatalyst is characterized by a high value of the exchange current density $\mathrm{i}_{0}$.

Two points need to be emphasized about the well-known Butler-Volmer equation:

$$
\mathrm{i}=\mathrm{i}_{0}\left[\exp \left(\frac{\alpha_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{~F} \eta}{\mathrm{RT}}\right)-\exp \left(\frac{-\alpha_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{~F} \eta}{\mathrm{RT}}\right)\right]
$$

and for its limiting high-field or Tafel approximation ${ }^{30}$ to which it reduces for ${ }^{*} \eta^{*}>200 \mathrm{mV}$ :

$$
\eta=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\alpha_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{~F}} \ln \left(\mathrm{i} / \mathrm{i}_{0}\right) \quad ; \quad \mathrm{i}>0
$$

$$
\eta=-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\alpha_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{~F}} \ln \left(-\mathrm{i} / \mathrm{i}_{0}\right) \quad ; \quad \mathrm{i}<0
$$

Firstly, is a kinetic expression, a rate law, such as, e.g., the Langmuir-Hinshelwood-Hougen-Watson rate expressions in heterogeneous catalysis, ${ }^{35}$ and as such has no universal applicability. It is derived on the basis of mass action kinetics ${ }^{30,34}$ and does reduce to the fundamental thermodynamic Nernst equation for $\mathrm{i}=0$, thus $\eta=0 .{ }^{30}$ Nevertheless, experimental deviations can be expected as with any other, even most successful, rate expression.

Secondly, the parameters $i_{0}, \alpha_{a}$, and $\alpha_{c}$, which are usually treated as constants, are in reality dependent on the coverage of species adsorbed at the tpb. Consequently if the coverage of these species changes significantly with $\eta$, then $i_{0}$, but also $\alpha_{a}$ and $\alpha_{c}$, can vary significantly.

Figure 13.2 shows a typical current-voltage curve of a fuel cell obtained by varying the external resistive load which shows that, as expected from Equations (13.12) to (13.14), the concentration overpotential becomes important at very high current densities, while the activation and ohmic overpotentials dominate at low and intermediate current densities where the cell power density $\mathrm{P}=\mathrm{Ei}$ is maximized. State-of-the-art SOFC units can produce power densities of the order of $0.3 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}$ and, to a good approximation, E decreases linearly with i with a slope, denoted by $\mathrm{R}_{\text {cell }}^{\prime}$, as low as $0.5 \Omega \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$. This area-specific resistance provides a good measure of the practical usefulness of a SOFC unit. Obtaining such small $\mathrm{R}_{\text {cell }}^{\prime}$ values ( $<0.5 \Omega \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ ) which are necessary for technological SOFC power-producing applications, depends crucially on minimizing the cell ohmic and anodic and cathodic activation overpotentials.

## D. EXCHANGE CURRENT DENSITY AND ELECTROCATALYTIC ACTIVITY

The magnitude of the activation overpotential for an electrocatalytic reaction occurring at the electrode/solid electrolyte tpb or interface depends crucially on the exchange current density $i_{0}$ (Equation 13.14). In order to measure the overpotential of a working electrode ( W ), anode, or cathode, and thus extract $i_{0}, \alpha_{a}$, and $\alpha_{c}$ from the Butler-Volmer equation, it is necessary to utilize a reference ( R ) electrode. ${ }^{13,30}$ When a current I flows between the working electrode and a counter electrode, then the potential of the working electrode $V_{\text {WR }}$ with respect to the reference electrode deviates from its open-circuit ( $I=0$ ) value $V_{\text {WR }}^{\mathrm{o}}$ and the working electrode overpotential $\eta_{\text {WR }}$ is defined from:

$$
\eta_{\mathrm{WR}}=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}^{\mathrm{o}}
$$

The overpotentials $\eta_{\mathrm{W}}$ and $\eta_{\mathrm{R}}$ of the working and reference electrodes (the latter vanishes for an ideal reference electrode) are defined as the deviations of the inner (or Galvani) potentials ${ }^{13,16}$ of these electrodes from their open-circuit values:

$$
\eta_{\mathrm{W}}=\varphi_{\mathrm{W}}-\varphi_{\mathrm{W}}^{\mathrm{o}} ; \eta_{\mathrm{R}}=\varphi_{\mathrm{R}}-\varphi_{\mathrm{R}}^{\mathrm{o}}
$$

One can write Equation (13.16) in the form:

$$
\eta_{\mathrm{WR}}=\eta_{\mathrm{W}}+\eta_{\mathrm{R}}+\eta_{\text {ohmic,WR }}
$$

Ideally, no current flows through the reference electrode; therefore in principle $\eta_{R}=0$ and $\eta_{\text {ohmic }, \text { WR }}=0$. In practice, the first assumption is usually adequate for reasonably nonpolarizable reference electrodes, since the parasitic (uncompensated) current flowing via the reference electrode is usually very small. The ohmic drop, however, between the working and reference electrodes, i.e., $\eta_{\text {ohmic,WR }}$, may in general not be negligible ${ }^{13,30}$ and must be

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-448.jpg?height=1013&width=1307&top_left_y=87&top_left_x=129)
FIGURE 13.3. Typical Tafel plots for Pt catalyst/YSZ (a) and Ag catalyst/YSZ (b) interfaces. (From Bebelis, S. and Vayenas, C.G., J. Catal., 1989, 118, 125; Vayenas, C.G. et al., Catal. Today, 1992, 11, 303. With permission.)

determined in each case using, e.g., the current interruption technique in conjunction with a recording oscilloscope. ${ }^{13,36}$ The ohmic component decays to zero within typically less than $1 \mu \mathrm{~s}$, and the remaining part of $\eta_{\mathrm{WR}}$ is $\eta_{\mathrm{W}}$. As in aqueous electrochemistry, the reference electrode must be placed as near to the catalyst as possible to minimize $\eta_{\text {ohm,WR }}$.

It is worth emphasizing that although overpotentials are usually associated in electrochemistry with electrode/electrolyte interfaces, in reality they refer to, and are measured as, deviations of the potentials of the electrodes only. Thus the concept of overpotential must be associated with an electrode and not with an electrode/electrolyte interface, although the nature of the interface will, in general, dictate the magnitude of the overpotential. This becomes an important point in Section III (catalysis).

The usual procedure for measuring the exchange current density $\mathrm{i}_{0}$ is then to measure $\eta$ as a function of I and to plot ln I vs. $\eta_{\mathrm{w}}$ (Tafel plot). Such plots are shown in Figure 13.3 for Pt and Ag electrodes deposited on YSZ. From the slopes of the linear part of these plots (* $\eta^{*}>200 \mathrm{mV}$, in which case Equation 13.15 are valid) one obtains the transfer coefficients $\alpha_{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\alpha_{\mathrm{c}}$. By extrapolating the linear part of the plot to $\eta=0$, one obtains $\mathrm{i}_{0}$. One can then plot i vs. $\eta$ and use the "low field" approximation of the Butler-Volmer equation which is valid for * $\eta$ * $<10 \mathrm{mV}$, i.e.,

$$
\mathrm{i} / \mathrm{i}_{0}=\left(\alpha_{\mathrm{a}}+\alpha_{\mathrm{c}}\right) \mathrm{F} \eta / \mathrm{RT}
$$

in order to check the accuracy of the extracted $i_{0}, \alpha_{a}$, and $\alpha_{c}$ values.
The exchange current density $\mathrm{i}_{0}$ is a measure of the electrocatalytic activity of the tpb or, more generally, the electrode/solid electrolyte interface for a given electrocatalytic reaction. It expresses the (equal and opposite) rates of the forward (anodic) and backward (cathodic)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-449.jpg?height=860&width=1090&top_left_y=97&top_left_x=233)
FIGURE 13.4. Effect of temperature and $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ on the $\mathrm{I}_{0}$ of a Pt catalyst film ( $\mathrm{A}=2 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ ) on YSZ. (From Manton, M., Ph.D. thesis, MIT, Cambridge, MA, 1984.)

electrocatalytic reaction rates when no net current crosses the electrode/solid electrolyte interface or, equivalently, the tpb. It has recently been shown that quite often, the exchange current $\mathrm{I}_{0}$ is proportional to the length 1 of the tpb ${ }^{37-39}$ as one would intuitively expect. The tpb length 1 can be estimated via cyclic voltammetry ${ }^{37}$ and electron microscopy. ${ }^{38}$ In principle, different electrocatalysts should be compared via the parameter $\mathrm{I}_{0} / \mathrm{l}$. In practice this is seldom done due to the difficulty in measuring 1 accurately and the comparison is usually made via the exchange current density $\mathrm{i}_{0}=\mathrm{I}_{0} / \mathrm{A}$, where A is the superficial surface area of the electrode/electrolyte interface.

The exchange current density $\mathrm{i}_{0}$ is usually strongly temperature dependent. It increases with temperature with an activation energy which is typically 140 to $180 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ for $\mathrm{Pt} / \mathrm{YSZ}$ and 80 to $100 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$ for $\mathrm{Ag} / \mathrm{YSZ} .{ }^{13,36}$ The $\mathrm{i}_{0}$ dependence on gaseous composition is usually complex. Figure 13.4 shows the measured $\mathrm{i}_{0}$ dependence on $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ and T for $\mathrm{Pt} / \mathrm{YSZ}$ films. ${ }^{40}$ These results can be described adequately on the basis of Langmuir-type dissociative oxygen chemisorption at the tpb, i.e:

$$
\theta_{\mathrm{O}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{1 / 2} /\left(1+\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{1 / 2}\right)
$$

where $\theta_{\mathrm{O}}$ is the coverage of atomic oxygen at the tpb and $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{O}}$ is the adsorption equilibrium constant of oxygen on Pt. It can be shown ${ }^{36,40}$ that:

$$
\mathrm{i}_{0}=\mathrm{c}\left[\theta_{\mathrm{O}}\left(1-\theta_{\mathrm{O}}\right)\right]^{1 / 2}
$$

where c is a constant, or, equivalently:

$$
\mathrm{i}_{0}=\mathrm{cK}_{\mathrm{O}}^{1 / 2} \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{1 / 4} /\left(1+\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{O}} \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{1 / 2}\right)
$$

which explains nicely the observed $\mathrm{i}_{0}$ maximum and the fact that $\mathrm{i}_{0}$ is proportional to $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{\mathrm{G}}$ for low $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ and $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{-\mathrm{G}}$ for high $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ (Figure 13.4). According to this successful model, the $\mathrm{i}_{0}$ maximum corresponds to $\theta_{\mathrm{O}}=1 / 2$.

Extraction of $\mathrm{I}_{0}$ and of the transfer coefficients $\alpha_{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\alpha_{\mathrm{c}}$ from steady-state currentoverpotential data is frequently complicated by significant changes in surface coverage of oxygen at the tpb or by surface oxide formation. ${ }^{40,41}$ It has been shown recently ${ }^{41}$ that several of these problems can be overcome by the use of cyclic voltammetry in conjunction with the equations:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\frac{\mathrm{dE}_{\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{~d} \ln v}=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\alpha_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{~F}} \\
\frac{\mathrm{dE}_{\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{~d} \ln v}=-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{\alpha_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{~F}}
\end{gathered}
$$

where $v$ is the sweep rate ( $\mathrm{V} / \mathrm{s}$ ) and $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{a}}, \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{p}, \mathrm{c}}$ are the potentials corresponding to the anodic and cathodic oxygen peaks, respectively. ${ }^{42}$ In this way it has been shown that for porous Pt/YSZ electrodes exposed to oxygen $\alpha_{\mathrm{a}}=\alpha_{\mathrm{c}}=1 .{ }^{41}$

The transfer coefficients $\alpha_{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\alpha_{\mathrm{c}}$ convey useful mechanistic information and can be used for mechanism discrimination. For a one-electron transfer process ( $\mathrm{n}=1$ ), e.g.,:

$$
\mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}(\mathrm{a})
$$

the Butler-Volmer equation is

$$
\mathrm{i} / \mathrm{i}_{0}=\exp (\beta F \eta / \mathrm{RT})-\exp [-(1-\beta) \mathrm{F} \eta / \mathrm{RT}]
$$

where $\beta$ is the symmetry factor ( $0<\beta<1$ ), which is frequently found to equal $0.5 .^{33,34}$
For multielectron transfer processes (n Š 2, e.g., oxygen reduction) the Butler-Volmer equation is

$$
\mathrm{i} / \mathrm{i}_{0}=\exp \left(\alpha_{\mathrm{a}} \mathrm{~F} \eta / \mathrm{RT}\right)-\exp \left[-\alpha_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{~F} \eta / \mathrm{RT}\right]
$$

It can be shown ${ }^{30,33}$ that $\alpha_{\mathrm{a}}$ and $\alpha_{\mathrm{c}}$ are related to the symmetry factor $\beta$ of the ratedetermining step (rds) via:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\alpha_{a}=\frac{n-\gamma}{v}-s \beta \\
\alpha_{c}=\frac{\gamma}{v}+s \beta
\end{gathered}
$$

where:
$\mathrm{n}=$ total number of electrons transferred in the overall electrocatalytic reaction
$v=$ stoichiometric number, i.e., number of times the rds occurs for one act of the overall reaction
$\gamma=$ number of electrons transferred before the rds in the forward reaction
$\mathrm{s}=$ number of electrons transferred in the rds ( 0 or 1)

$$
\alpha_{a}+\alpha_{c}=n / v
$$

The above equations can be quite useful for discriminating between various mechanisms for a given overall electrocatalytic reaction. They give rise to several well-known Tafel slopes, the most common of which ( $2 \mathrm{RT} / \mathrm{F}, \mathrm{RT} / \mathrm{F}, 2 \mathrm{RT} / 3 \mathrm{~F}, \mathrm{RT} / 2 \mathrm{~F}$, and RT/4F ) have been observed. ${ }^{33,34}$ However, the derivation of Equation (13.26) is based on three important assumptions:
I. A single clearly defined rds
II. Low coverages ( $\theta$ ! 1 ) which can be described by a linear isotherm
III. A single reaction pathway

## II. ELECTROCATALYTIC OPERATION OF SOLID ELECTROLYTE CELLS

## A. ELECTROCATALYSIS FOR THE PRODUCTION OF CHEMICALS

The preparation, structure, and performance of SOFC used for power generation is described in Chapter 12 of this handbook. Both the electrocatalysis and the catalysis of the $\mathrm{Ni} / \mathrm{YSZ}$ anode are reasonably well understood and the same applies for the electrocatalysis of the $\mathrm{La}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Sr}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{MnO}_{3-\delta}$ and $\mathrm{La}_{1-\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{Sr}_{\mathrm{x}} \mathrm{CoO}_{3-\delta}$ cathodes. State-of-the-art SOFC units with anodic and cathodic overpotentials of less than 150 mV each at current densities up to $0.5 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{cm}^{2}$ at $\mathrm{T}=950^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are currently available. ${ }^{6}$

In recent years it has been shown that solid electrolyte fuel cells with appropriate electrocatalytic anodes can be used not only for power generation via oxidation of $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{CH}_{4}$, but also for "chemical cogeneration", i.e., for the simultaneous production of power and useful chemicals. This mode of operation, first demonstrated for the case of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ oxidation to $\mathrm{NO},{ }^{8-10}$ combines the concepts of a fuel cell and of a chemical reactor (Figure 13.5). The economics of chemical cogeneration have been modeled and discussed recently. ${ }^{7}$ The economics appear promising for a few highly exothermic reactions which can be carried out at temperatures above $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, such as the oxidation of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ to sulfur and $\mathrm{SO}_{2}{ }^{11}$ and of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ to NO. ${ }^{8-10}$ It is likely that if SOFC units operating on $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ or natural gas become commercially available, then they could be used with appropriate anodic electrocatalysts by some chemical industries.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-451.jpg?height=594&width=874&top_left_y=1558&top_left_x=342)
FIGURE 13.5. Operating principle of a chemical cogenerator. (Reprinted with permission from Vayenas, C.G., Bebelis, S., and Kyriazis, C.C., CHEMTECH, 1991, 21, 422. Copyright 1991 The American Chemical Society.)

TABLE 13.1
Electrocatalytic Reactions Investigated in Doped $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$ Solid Electrolyte Fuel Cells for Chemical Cogeneration

$$
\begin{array}{lll} 
& \text { Electrocatalyst } & \text { Ref. } \\
2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+5 \mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+10 \mathrm{e}^{-} & \mathrm{Pt}, \mathrm{Pt}-\mathrm{Rh} & 8-10 \\
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} & \mathrm{Ag} & 12 \\
\mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{CH}_{3}+\mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{6} \mathrm{H}_{5}-\mathrm{CH}_{1} \mathrm{CH}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} & \mathrm{Pt}, \mathrm{Fe}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} & 43,44 \\
\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}+3 \mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} & \mathrm{Pt} & 11 \\
\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{6}+\mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{3} \text { dimers }+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} & \mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}-\mathrm{La}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} & 45 \\
\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{NH}_{3}+3 \mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \mathrm{HCN}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+6 \mathrm{e}^{-} & \mathrm{Pt}, \mathrm{Pt}-\mathrm{Rh} & 46 \\
2 \mathrm{CH}_{4}+2 \mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} & \mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{Ag}-\mathrm{Sm}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3} & 59
\end{array}
$$

Table 13.1 lists the anodic reactions which have been studied so far in small cogenerative SOFC units. One simple and interesting rule which has emerged from these studies is that the selection of the anodic electrocatalyst for a selective electrocatalytic oxidation can be based on the heterogeneous catalytic literature for the corresponding selective catalytic oxidation. Thus the selectivity of Pt and $\mathrm{Pt}-\mathrm{Rh}$ alloy electrocatalysts for the anodic $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ oxidation to NO :

$$
2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+5 \mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+10 \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$

turns out to be comparable (>95\%) with the selectivity of Pt and $\mathrm{Pt}-\mathrm{Rh}$ alloy catalysts for the corresponding commercial catalytic oxidation where oxygen is co-fed with $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ in the gas phase. ${ }^{8-10}$ The same applies for Ag which turns out to be equally selective as an electrocatalyst for the anodic partial oxidation of methanol to formaldehyde: ${ }^{12}$

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}+\mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}
$$

as it is a catalyst for the corresponding heterogeneous catalytic reaction:

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}+1 / 2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

Another reaction which is of considerable interest from the view point of chemical cogeneration is the oxidative coupling of methane (OCM) to $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ hydrocarbons ethane and ethylene: ${ }^{47}$

$$
2 \mathrm{CH}_{4} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{O}^{2-}} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{O}^{2-}} \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{O}^{2-}} 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}
$$

There have been several OCM studies utilizing SOFC reactors ${ }^{48-58}$ and a recent review. ${ }^{48}$ In many of these studies electrical power was supplied to the cell to increase cell current. This, however, tends in general to decrease the selectivity and yield of $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ hydrocarbons. Very recently it was found that it is possible to obtain $\mathrm{C}_{2}$ yields up to $88 \%$ by means of a novel gas-recycle SOFC reactor-separator. ${ }^{59}$ The $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ yield is up to $85 \%$. ${ }^{59}$ These systems are of considerable technological interest.

Aside from chemical cogeneration studies, where the electrocatalytic anodic and cathodic reactions are driven by the voltage spontaneously generated by the solid electrolyte cell, several other electrocatalytic reactions, including the OCM reaction, have been investigated in solid electrolyte cells ${ }^{52-58,60-67}$ and are listed in Table 13.2. Earlier studies by Schouler and co-workers focused mainly on the investigation of electrocatalysts for $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ electrolysis. ${ }^{61}$ Gür and Huggins ${ }^{64-66}$ and Mason and co-workers ${ }^{63}$ were first to show that other electrocatalytic reactions, such as NO decomposition and CO hydrogenation can be carried out in zirconia

TABLE 13.2
Electrocatalytic Reactions Investigated in Doped $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$ Solid Electrolyte Fuel Cells with External Potential Application
| Electrocatalyst |  | Ref. |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ | Ni | 60-62 |
| $2 \mathrm{NO}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2}+2 \mathrm{O}^{2-}$ | Pt, Au | 63,64 |
| $\mathrm{CO}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ | Pt, Ni | 65,66 |
| $\mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{6}+\mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{3} \mathrm{H}_{6} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$ | Au | 67 |
| $\mathrm{CH}_{4}+\mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{6}, \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}, \mathrm{CO}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}$ | $\mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{Ag}-\mathrm{MgO}, \mathrm{Ag}-\mathrm{Bi}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{Ag}-\mathrm{Sm}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ | 48-59 |


cells. More recently Otsuka et al., ${ }^{52,54,55}$ Eng and Stoukides, ${ }^{50}$ Seimanides and Stoukides, ${ }^{53}$ Belyaev et al., ${ }^{56}$ and Vayenas et al., ${ }^{59}$ have concentrated on the investigation of the OCM reaction using a variety of metal and metal oxide electrodes deposited on YSZ. The use of protonic conductors to enhance the OCM reaction has also been explored, ${ }^{68,69}$ which is in principle a very interesting idea.

## B. ELECTROCHEMICAL REACTOR ANALYSIS AND DESIGN

SOFCs can be viewed as a special type of a chemical reactor which generates electrical power in addition to heat. The first rigorous engineering modeling of SOFC units was carried out in 1983 by Debenedetti and Vayenas, ${ }^{29}$ who assumed well-mixed (CSTR-type) anodic and cathodic compartments, and isothermality within the SOFC structure for the overall reaction:

$$
\mathrm{A}+1 / 2 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{~B}
$$

e.g., $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ or CO oxidation, and showed that the following dimensionless mass, energy, and electron balances govern the SOFC behavior:

Fuel (A) and oxygen mass balances:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}=\mathrm{A}_{1} \cdot \xi \\
\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=\mathrm{A}_{1} \cdot \mathrm{~A}_{2} \cdot \xi
\end{gathered}
$$

Energy balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{1} A_{3}\left(1-A_{4} \xi\right) \xi= & \left(1+A_{6}-A_{1} A_{2} A_{7} \xi\right)(\Theta-1)- \\
& -A_{5}\left(\Theta_{1}^{o}-1\right)-A_{6}\left(\Theta_{2}^{o}-1\right)+A_{8}\left(\Theta-\Theta_{c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Electron balance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left\{A_{4}\left(1+A_{9}\right)+\exp \left[A_{12}\left(\frac{1}{\Theta}-1\right)\right]\right\} \cdot \xi=A_{10^{+}} \\
& \quad+A_{11} \Theta\left\{\ln \frac{P_{o} y_{O_{2}}^{o}\left(1-X_{O_{2}}\right)\left(1-X_{A}\right)^{2}}{\left(1-y_{O_{2}}^{o} X_{O_{2}}\right) X_{A}^{2}}+2 \ln \left(1-A_{13} \xi\right)+\right. \\
& \left.\quad+\ln \left(1-A_{14} \xi\right)-A_{15}\left[\ln \left(A_{16} \xi\right)+A_{17} \ln \left(A_{18} \xi\right)\right]\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

where the dimensionless current $\xi$ and temperature $\Theta$ are defined by:

$$
\begin{gathered}
\xi=\mathrm{I} \rho_{\mathrm{o}} \delta / \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{el}} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{TH}} \\
\Theta=\mathrm{T} / \mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{o}}
\end{gathered}
$$

All symbols appearing in Equations (13.33) and (13.34) are defined in Table 13.3. The dimensionless parameters $\mathrm{A}_{1}$ to $\mathrm{A}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{A}_{6}$ to $\mathrm{A}_{9}$ play a key role in SOFC performance. Their physical significance and importance for scale-up is discussed in Reference 29. The left- and right-hand sides of the dimensionless energy balance (13.33c) can be viewed as a heat generation and heat removal term, respectively. The dimensionless electron balance (13.33d) can be viewed as a dimensionless kinetic expression where the right-hand side represents the driving force for current flow, thus for reaction. Numerical solution of Equations (13.33) with realistic parameter values has shown that, similar to chemical reactors, SOFC units can exhibit steady-state multiplicity as well as ignition and extinction phenomena over a wide range of design and operating parameters. ${ }^{29}$

The above model is valid both for power-producing and chemical-producing SOFC units. It is a lumped-parameter CSTR-type model, i.e., it assumes uniform gas composition and temperature within the SOFC structure. When the first flat plate or cross-flow monolithic SOFC units were fabricated and tested, ${ }^{70}$ a new two-dimensional model was developed to account for the variation in gas-phase composition and temperature within the SOFC structure. ${ }^{71}$ The governing equations of this two-dimensional mixing cell model are not given here due to space limitations, but can be found in Reference ${ }^{71}$ as well as in more recent papers where in addition to cross-flow SOFC units, counter-flow, and concurrent-flow SOFC units are also modeled. ${ }^{72}$ Various improvements and modifications of the original model have been published recently, ${ }^{73,74}$ also accounting for SOFC operation with internal reforming. ${ }^{74}$

## III. CATALYSIS ON THE ELECTRODES OF SOLID ELECTROLYTE CELLS

## A. POTENTIOMETRIC INVESTIGATIONS

The interesting role which solid electrolytes can play in the study of heterogeneous catalysis was first recognized by Wagner, ${ }^{75}$ who proposed the use of solid electrolyte cells for the measurement of the activity of oxygen on metal and metal oxide catalysts. This technique, first used to study the mechanism of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ oxidation on noble metals, ${ }^{76}$ was subsequently called solid electrolyte potentiometry (SEP). ${ }^{77}$ It has been used in conjunction with kinetic measurements to study the mechanism of several catalytic reactions on metals ${ }^{77-91}$ and, more recently, metal oxides. ${ }^{92-96}$ It is particularly suitable for the study of oscillatory catalytic reactions. ${ }^{77,81,83,87-89}$ The SEP literature has recently been reviewed. ${ }^{92,97-99}$

Figure 13.6 shows a typical experimental arrangement for the use of SEP. The bottom porous metal electrode (e.g., Pt) of the oxygen ion-conducting solid electrolyte cell is exposed to ambient air and serves as a reference electrode (R). The top electrode, which we denote by W (for "working"), acts both as an electrode and as a catalyst for the catalytic reaction to be studied. The open-circuit potential $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}^{\mathrm{o}}$ of the catalyst electrode relative to the reference electrode can be written as:

$$
\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}^{\mathrm{o}}=(1 / 4 \mathrm{~F})\left[\mu_{\mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{~W}}-\mu_{\mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{R}}\right]
$$

where $\mu_{\mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{~W}}$ and $\mu_{\mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{R}}$ are the chemical potentials of oxygen at the catalyst and reference electrode surfaces, respectively. Equation (13.35) is derived on the following four assumptions:

TABLE 13.3
Dimensionless and Dimensional Parameters Appearing in the Governing Mass, Energy and Electron Balance Equations of Single-Cell SOFC Units (Equations 13.33 and 13.34)
| $\mathrm{A}_{1}$ | $\mathrm{E}_{\text {TH }} \cdot \mathrm{A}_{\text {el }} / 2 \mathrm{Fp}_{\mathrm{o}} \delta \mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\mathrm{o}}$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\mathrm{A}_{2}$ | $\mathrm{N}_{1} \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\mathrm{o}} / 2 \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{\mathrm{o}}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{3}$ | $\left(-\Delta \mathrm{H}^{0}\right) \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\mathrm{o}} / \overline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathrm{p} 1} \cdot \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{o}}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{4}$ | $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{ex}} \cdot \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{el}} / \rho_{\mathrm{o}} \delta$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{5}$ | $\overline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathrm{p}, 1}^{\mathrm{o}} / \overline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathrm{p}, 1}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{6}$ | $\mathrm{N}_{2} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathrm{p}, 2} / \mathrm{N}_{1} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathrm{p}, 1}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{7}$ | $\mathrm{N}_{2} \cdot \mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{\mathrm{o}} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathrm{p}, 2} / \mathrm{N}_{1} \overline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathrm{p}, 1}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{8}$ | $\mathrm{U} \cdot \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{ex}} / \mathrm{N}_{1} \cdot \overline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathrm{p}, 1} \cdot \mathrm{~T}_{\mathrm{o}}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{9}$ | $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{el}} / \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{ex}}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{10}$ | $\mathrm{E}^{\mathrm{o}} / \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{TH}}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{11}$ | $\mathrm{RT}_{\mathrm{o}} / 4 \mathrm{FE}_{\mathrm{TH}}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{12}$ | E*/RTo |
| $\mathrm{A}_{13}$ | $\mathrm{E}_{\text {TH }} \cdot \mathrm{A}_{\text {el }} / \rho_{\mathrm{o}} \cdot \delta \cdot \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{a}}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{14}$ | $\mathrm{E}_{\text {TH }} \cdot \mathrm{A}_{\text {el }} / \rho_{o} \cdot \delta \cdot \mathrm{I}_{L, c}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{15}$ | $4 / \alpha_{a}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{16}$ | $\mathrm{E}_{\text {TH }} \cdot \mathrm{A}_{\text {el }} / \rho_{o} \cdot \delta \cdot \mathrm{I}_{0, \text { a }}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{17}$ | $\alpha_{\mathrm{a}} / \alpha_{\mathrm{c}}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{18}$ | $\mathrm{E}_{\text {TH }} \cdot \mathrm{A}_{\text {el }} / \rho_{\mathrm{o}} \cdot \delta \cdot \mathrm{I}_{0, \mathrm{c}}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{\text {el }}$ | Superficial electrode surface area per unit cell, $\mathrm{m}^{2}$ |
| $\mathrm{A}_{\text {ex }}$ | Heat loss surface area per unit cell, $\mathrm{m}^{2}$ |
| $\overline{\mathrm{C}}_{\mathrm{p}}$ | Temperature and composition averaged molar specific heat, J/mol K |
| E | Operating cell voltage, V |
| Eo | Reversible cell voltage corresponding to unit activity of reactants and products, V |
| $\mathrm{E}_{\text {rev }}$ | Reversible cell voltage, V |
| $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{TH}}$ | Thermoneutral cell voltage ( $\left.=\left(-\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}\right) / \mathrm{nF}\right)$, V |
| E* | Activation energy for $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ conduction in solid electrolyte, J/mol |
| F | Faraday constant, $96,500 \mathrm{Cb} / \mathrm{g}$-equivalent |
| I | Current per unit cell, A |
| $\mathrm{I}_{0, \mathrm{a}}$ | Anodic exchange current, A |
| $\mathrm{I}_{0, \mathrm{c}}$ | Cathodic exchange current, A |
| $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{a}}$ | Limiting anodic current corresponding to completely mass transfer controlled anodic operation, A |
| $\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{L}, \mathrm{c}}$ | Limiting cathodic current, A |
| $\xi$ | Dimensionless current per unit cell ( $=\mathrm{I} \rho_{\mathrm{o}} \delta / \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{el}} \cdot \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{TH}}$ ) |
| n | Electrons transferred per molecule A converted |
| $\mathrm{N}_{1}$ | Fuel stream molar flow rate, mol/s |
| $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ | Oxygen stream feed molar flow rate, mol/s |


TABLE 13.3 (continued)
Dimensionless and Dimensional Parameters Appearing in the Governing Mass, Energy and Electron Balance Equations of Single-Cell SOFC Units (Equations 13.33 and 13.34)
| $\mathrm{P}_{\text {o }}$ | Operating cell pressure, bar |
| :--- | :--- |
| R | Gas constant, $8.314 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{K}$ |
| $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{e} 1}$ | Electrode resistance, $\Omega$ |
| $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{i}}$ | Electrolyte resistance, $\Omega$ |
| $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{ex}}$ | External load, $\Omega$ |
| T | Absolute temperature, K |
| U | Overall heat transfer coefficient, W/m²K |
| W | Work produced per mol of A converted, J |
| $\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}$ | Fuel conversion |
| $\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{O} 2}$ | Oxygen conversion |
| $\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{A}}^{\mathrm{o}}$ | Fuel mol fraction in feed |
| $\mathrm{y}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}^{\mathrm{o}}$ | Oxygen mol fraction in feed |
| Greek Symbols |  |
| $\alpha_{\mathrm{a}}, \alpha_{\mathrm{c}}$ | Transfer coefficients for anodic and cathodic overpotential, dimensionless |
| $\Delta \mathrm{G}$ | Gibbs free energy change for Reaction (13.32), J/mol A |
| $\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ | Standard enthalpy change for Reaction (13.32) J/mol A at $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{o}}$ |
| $\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}$ | Standard entropy change for Reaction (13.32), $\mathrm{J} / \mathrm{K} \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{A}$ at $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{o}}$ |
| $\delta$ | Electrolyte thickness, m |
| $\Theta$ | Dimensionless temperature |
| $\rho$ | Electrolyte resistivity, $\Omega$ |
| $\rho_{o}$ | Electrolyte resistivity at reference temperature $\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{o}}, \Omega$ |
| Subscripts |  |
| 1 | Fuel stream |
| 2 | Air stream |
| c | Ambient conditions |
| o | Reference conditions |
| Superscript |  |
| o | Feed conditions |


1. The solid electrolyte is a pure $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ conductor.
2. The catalyst and reference electrodes are made of the same bulk material.
3. The dominant electrocatalytic reaction taking place at the metal-solid electrolyte-gas three-phase-boundaries (tpb) is:

$$
\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{a})+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{O}^{2-}
$$

or

$$
\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{a})+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{O}^{2-}
$$

where $\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{a})$ and $\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{a})$ denote oxygen dissociatively or molecularly adsorbed at the tpb, respectively. It is also assumed that equilibrium is established for Reaction (13.36) or (13.37).
4. There are no concentration or chemical potential gradients within the porous catalyst film so that $\mu_{\mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{~W}}$ is the same at the tpb and over the entire catalyst surface. This implies that thin (e.g., 5 to $10 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ ) and sufficiently porous metal or metal oxide catalyst films must be used in order to ensure the absence of internal diffusional limitations.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-457.jpg?height=856&width=716&top_left_y=94&top_left_x=419)
FIGURE 13.6. Schematic of a solid electrolyte reactor used for simultaneous kinetic and SEP studies. (From Yentekakis, I.V., Neophytides, S., and Vayenas, C.G., J. Catal., 1988, 111, 152. With permission.)

Assumption 3 is certainly valid for the reference electrode, but may not always be valid for the catalyst electrode: if one of the reactants adsorbs strongly on the catalyst surface and has a high affinity for reaction with $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ (e.g., $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ or CO under fuel-rich conditions), then other electrocatalytic reactions, such as

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \\
& \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{O}^{2-} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$

may also take place at the tpb, leading to the establishment of mixed potentials. In this case $\mathrm{V}_{\text {WR }}^{\mathrm{o}}$ provides only a qualitative measure of surface activities. This point has been discussed in the past. ${ }^{84,85}$ In practice this means that one must be rather cautious when using Equation (13.35) to treat very negative $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}^{\mathrm{o}}$ values, e.g., less than -400 mV , unless one proves the validity of assumption 3 via electrokinetic measurements by utilizing a three-electrode system, which is not an easy experiment. ${ }^{84,85}$ When assumption 3 is also satisfied, in addition to 1,2 , and 4 , then the emf $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}^{\mathrm{o}}$ provides indeed an in situ quantitative measure of the chemical potential or activity of oxygen adsorbed on the catalyst under reaction conditions, and this information can indeed be quite useful. ${ }^{77,87-89}$

Before discussing how Equation (13.35) has been used in the past to analyze SEP data, it is important to discuss first some recent findings. It was recently found both theoretically ${ }^{13,16}$ and experimentally by means of a Kelvin probe ${ }^{17,100}$ that the emf $\mathrm{V}_{\text {WR }}^{\mathrm{o}}$ of solid electrolyte cells provides a direct measure of the difference in the work function e $\Phi$ of the gas-exposed surfaces of the working and reference electrodes:

$$
e V_{W R}^{o}=e \Phi_{W}-e \Phi_{R}
$$

Equation (13.40) is always valid provided the catalyst and reference electrodes are made of the same material. ${ }^{17,100}$ It is also valid when other types of solid electrolytes are used, e.g., $\beta^{\prime \prime}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$, a $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$conductor. ${ }^{17,100}$ Equation (13.40) is much more general and fundamental than Equation (13.35), as it does not require the establishment of any specific electrochemical equilibrium. It shows that solid electrolyte cells are work function probes for their gas-exposed electrode surfaces. It also shows that SEP is essentially a work function measuring technique and that several aspects of the SEP literature reviewed in References 92,97-99 must be reexamined in the light of these findings. One can still use SEP to extract information about surface activities, provided the nature of the electrocatalytic reaction at the tpb is well known, but, even when this is not the case, the cell emf still provides a direct measure of the work function difference between the two gas-exposed electrode surfaces. Equation (13.40) also holds under closed-circuit conditions, as is further discussed in Section III.B.

Equation (13.35) has been used in the following way in previous SEP studies. ${ }^{78-82,87}$ First one notes that the chemical potential of oxygen adsorbed on the reference electrode is given by

$$
\mu_{\mathrm{O}_{2, \mathrm{R}}}=\mu_{\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}^{\mathrm{o}}+\mathrm{RT} \ln (0.21)
$$

where $\mu_{\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})}^{\circ}$ is the standard chemical potential of gaseous oxygen at the temperature T , and 0.21 (bar) corresponds to the oxygen activity in the reference gas, i.e., air. Defining the activity of atomic oxygen adsorbed on the catalyst $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{O}}$ from:

$$
\mu_{\mathrm{O}_{2}, \mathrm{~W}}=\mu_{\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}}^{\mathrm{o}}+\mathrm{RT} \ln \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{O}}^{2}
$$

one obtains

$$
\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}^{\mathrm{o}}=(\mathrm{RT} / 2 \mathrm{~F}) \ln \left[\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{o}} /(0.21)^{1 / 2}\right]
$$

Consequently, by measuring $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}^{\mathrm{o}}$ and T one computes $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{O}}$ which expresses the square root of the partial pressure of gaseous oxygen that would be in thermodynamic equilibrium with oxygen adsorbed on the catalyst surface, if such an equilibrium were established. By comparing the potentiometrically obtained surface oxygen activity with the independently measured gas-phase oxygen activity $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$, it is possible to extract useful information about the rds of the catalytic reaction. Thus if $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{O}}^{2}=\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$, then the oxygen adsorption step is in equilibrium and cannot be rate limiting. If, however $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{O}}^{2}<\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$, as is often found to be the case, then oxygen adsorption is rate limiting.

Despite its limitations, SEP is one of the very few techniques which can be used to extract in situ information about adsorbed species on catalyst surfaces without UHV requirements. It is particularly useful for the study of oscillatory reactions. Figure 13.7 shows typical rate and $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}^{\mathrm{o}}$, thus also $\mathrm{e} \Phi$ oscillations during CO oxidation on $\mathrm{Pt} .{ }^{89,97}$ Figure 13.8 shows the dependence of the frequency and amplitude of the rate and $\mathrm{V}_{\text {WR }}^{\mathrm{o}}$ oscillations on $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{O}}$. It can be seen that the high-frequency transition (bifurcation) between oscillatory and nonoscillatory states occurs near the decomposition pressure $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{O}}^{*}$ of surface $\mathrm{PtO}_{2}$ which is given by:

$$
\ln \left(\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{o}}^{*} / \mathrm{kPa}^{1 / 2}\right)=14.8-12000 / \mathrm{T}
$$

This dissociation pressure expression has been obtained from similar experiments during $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ oxidation on $\mathrm{Pt},{ }^{87,88,101}$ which is also an oscillatory reaction. This expression is in good agreement with independent high-precision resistance measurements ${ }^{102}$ and with XPS data,

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-459.jpg?height=1210&width=617&top_left_y=99&top_left_x=472)
FIGURE 13.7. Effect of inlet CO partial pressure on reaction rate and on $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}^{\mathrm{o}}$ and $\mathrm{e} \Phi$ oscillations during CO oxidation on $\mathrm{Pt} ; \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=5.4 \mathrm{kPa}, \mathrm{T}=337^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, total molar flow rate $2.7 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{s}$. (From Yentekakis, I.V., Neophytides, S., and Vayenas, C.G., J. Catal., 1988, 111, 152. With permission.)

which have positively confirmed the existence of surface $\mathrm{PtO}_{2} .{ }^{103-105}$ The information derived by SEP, i.e., that the transition between oscillatory and nonoscillatory states takes place very near the dissociation pressure of $\mathrm{PtO}_{2}$, provided the first evidence that the rate, $\mathrm{V}_{\text {WR }}^{\mathrm{p}}$, and $\mathrm{e} \Phi$ oscillations during $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ and CO oxidation on Pt under atmospheric pressure conditions are due to the formation and decomposition of surface $\mathrm{PtO}_{2} .{ }^{88,101}$ This has led to mathematical models which describe the oscillatory phenomena in a semiquantitative manner. ${ }^{87,106}$

More recently, SEP measurements have been extended to metal oxide catalyst systems, $90,91,93-96$ such as Cu oxide used for the partial oxidation of propene to acrolein (Figure 13.9).

## B. ELECTROCHEMICAL ACTIVATION OF CATALYZED REACTIONS

During the last few years a new application of solid electrolytes has emerged. It was found that the catalytic activity and selectivity of the gas-exposed electrode surface of metal electrodes in solid electrolyte cells is altered dramatically and reversibly upon polarizing the metal/solid electrolyte interface. The induced steady-state change in catalytic rate can be up to $9000 \%$ higher than the normal (open-circuit) catalytic rate and up to $3 \times 10^{5}$ higher than the steady-state rate of ion supply. ${ }^{14,107}$ This new effect of non-faradaic electrochemical modification of catalytic activity (NEMCA) has been already demonstrated for more than

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-460.jpg?height=835&width=1249&top_left_y=87&top_left_x=152)
FIGURE 13.8. Effect of oxygen activity $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{O}}$ on the frequency and amplitude of $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{O}}$ oscillations during CO oxidation on Pt. Open and filled symbols correspond to oscillations obtained on the preoxidized and prereduced Pt catalyst film; T = 610 K. (From Yentekakis, I.V., Neophytides, S., and Vayenas, C.G., J. Catal., 1988, 111, 152. With permission.)

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-460.jpg?height=927&width=688&top_left_y=1162&top_left_x=435)
FIGURE 13.9. Effect of $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ on the rate of acrolein formation and on the oxygen activity on a Cu oxide catalyst during oxidation of propene, indicating rate and $\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{O}}$ hysteresis. The rate of by-product $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ formation increases monotonically with $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$. (From Vayenas, C.G., Bebelis, S., Yentekakis, I.V., and Lintz, H.-G., Catal. Today, 1992, 11, 303. With permission.)

40 catalytic reactions on $\mathrm{Pt}, \mathrm{Pd}, \mathrm{Rh}, \mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{Au}$, and Ni surfaces by using $\mathrm{O}^{2-}, \mathrm{F}^{-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}$, and $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ conducting solid electrolytes. There are also recent demonstrations for aqueous electrolyte systems. ${ }^{20,26}$ In this section the common features of NEMCA studies are summarized and the origin of the effect is discussed in the light of recent in situ work function and XPS measurements which have shown that:
I. Solid electrolyte cells with metal electrodes are work function probes and work function controllers, via potential application, for their gas-exposed electrode surfaces.
II. NEMCA is due to an electrochemically driven and controlled back-spillover of ions from the solid electrolyte onto the gas-exposed electrode surface. These back-spillover ions establish an effective electrochemical double layer and act as promoters for catalytic reactions. This interfacing of electrochemistry and catalysis offers several exciting theoretical and technological possibilities. We note that in the catalytic literature, the term spillover usually denotes migration of a species from a metal to a support, while the term back-spillover denotes migration in the opposite direction and is thus more appropriate here.

## 1. Introduction

During the last few years it has become apparent that the "active" use of solid electrolyte cells offers some very interesting possibilities in heterogeneous catalysis: it was found that solid electrolyte cells can be used not only to study, but also to influence catalytic phenomena on metal surfaces in a very pronounced and reversible manner. Work in this area prior to 1988 had been reviewed. ${ }^{97}$ Then in 1988 the first reports on NEMCA appeared in the literature. ${ }^{14,15}$ Since then the NEMCA effect has been described for more than 40 catalytic reactions, ${ }^{13-25}$ and work prior to 1992 has been reviewed in a monograph. ${ }^{13}$ In addition to the group which first reported this novel effect, ${ }^{13-20}$ the groups of Sobyanin, ${ }^{21,22}$ Lambert et al., ${ }^{23}$ Stoukides et al., ${ }^{24}$ and Haller et al. ${ }^{25}$ have also contributed recently to the NEMCA literature. Very recently, the NEMCA effect was also demonstrated in an aqueous electrolyte system by Anastasijevic et al. ${ }^{26}$ and by Neophytides et al. ${ }^{20}$ The term "Electrochemical Promotion in Catalysis" has also been proposed by Pritchard ${ }^{27}$ to describe the NEMCA effect.

It has been found that the catalytic activity and selectivity of porous metal catalyst films deposited on solid electrolytes can be altered in a dramatic, reversible, and, to some extent, predictable ${ }^{13}$ manner by carrying out the catalytic reaction in solid electrolyte cells of the type:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \text { gaseous reactants, metal catalyst } * \text { solid electrolyte } * \text { metal, } \mathrm{O}_{2} \\
& \left(\text { e.g., } \mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}+\mathrm{O}_{2}\right)(\text { e.g., } \mathrm{Pt})(\text { e.g., } \mathrm{YSZ})(\text { e.g., } \mathrm{Ag})
\end{aligned}
$$

where the metal catalyst also serves as an electrode and by applying currents or potentials to the cell with a concomitant supply or removal of ions, e.g., $\mathrm{O}^{2-}, \mathrm{F}^{-}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{H}^{+}$, to or from the catalyst surface.

The NEMCA effect was first demonstrated on Pt and Ag electrodes using $8 \mathrm{~mol} \% \mathrm{Y}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ stabilized $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$ (YSZ), an $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ conductor, as the solid electrolyte. ${ }^{14-16}$ Electrochemical $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ pumping to the catalyst electrode was found to cause an up to 60 -fold ( $6000 \%$ ) steady-state reversible enhancement in the rate of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ oxidation. ${ }^{16}$ Furthermore, this steady-state rate increase was found to be up to $3 \times 10^{5}$ times higher than the steady-state rate of supply of $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ to the catalyst, i.e., the enhancement factor $\Lambda^{13,16}$ or apparent faradaic efficiency for the process is $3 \times 10^{5} .^{16}$ More recently, the rate of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ oxidation on Rh electrodes supported on YSZ was found to reversibly increase by a factor of 90 , again with faradaic efficiency values of the order of $10^{5} .{ }^{107,108}$

The effect of NEMCA or electrochemical promotion ${ }^{27}$ does not appear to be limited to any particular metal, solid electrolyte, or group of catalytic reactions. Thus, in addition to

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-462.jpg?height=1135&width=718&top_left_y=83&top_left_x=419)
FIGURE 13.10. Schematic of the experimental setup for NEMCA studies (a), and for using XPS (b) G-P.: galvanostat-potentiostat. (From Vayenas, C.G., Ladas, S., Bebelis, S., Yentekakis, I.V., Neophytides, S., Jiang, Y., Karavasilis, Ch., and Pliangos, C., Electrochim. Acta, 1994, 39, 1849. With permission.)

$\mathrm{O}^{2-}$-conducting solid electrolytes, ${ }^{13-16,18,19}$ the NEMCA effect has also been demonstrated using $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$-conducting solid electrolytes such as $\beta^{\prime \prime}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}{ }^{17,23}, \mathrm{H}^{+}$-conducting solid electrolytes such as $\mathrm{CsHSO}_{4},{ }^{21}$ and, very recently, $\mathrm{F}^{-}$-conducting solid electrolytes, such as $\mathrm{CaF}_{2} .{ }^{109}$ In addition to complete ${ }^{15,16}$ and partial oxidation reactions ${ }^{13}$ the NEMCA effect has been demonstrated for dehydrogenation, hydrogenation, and decomposition reactions. ${ }^{13,21}$

## 2. Experimental Setup

The basic experimental setup is shown schematically in Figure 13.10a. The metal working catalyst electrode, usually in the form of a porous metal film 3 to $20 \mu \mathrm{~m}$ in thickness, is deposited on the surface of a ceramic solid electrolyte (e.g., YSZ, an $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ conductor, or $\beta^{\prime \prime}$ $\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}, \mathrm{a} \mathrm{Na}^{+}$conductor). Catalyst, counter, and reference electrode preparation and characterization details have been presented in detail elsewhere, ${ }^{13}$ together with the analytical system for on-line monitoring of the rates of catalytic reactions by means of gas chromatography, mass spectrometry and IR spectroscopy.

The superficial surface area of the metal working catalyst electrode is typically $2 \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ and its true gas-exposed surface area is typically 5 to $10^{3} \mathrm{~cm}^{2}$ as measured via surface titration of oxygen with CO or $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4} \cdot{ }^{13-19}$ The catalyst electrode is exposed to the reactive gas mixture (e.g., $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$ ) in a continuous-flow, gradientless reactor (CSTR). Under open-circuit conditions ( $\mathrm{I}=0$ ), it acts as a regular catalyst for the catalytic reaction under study, e.g., $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ oxidation. The counter and reference electrodes are usually exposed to ambient air.

A galvanostat or potentiostat is used to apply constant currents between the catalyst and the counter electrode or constant potentials between the catalyst and reference electrodes, respectively. In this way, ions ( $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ in the case of $\mathrm{YSZ}, \mathrm{Na}^{+}$in the case of $\beta^{\prime \prime}-\mathrm{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}$ ) are supplied from (or to) the solid electrolyte to (or from) the catalyst electrode surface. The current is defined positive when anions are supplied to or cations removed from the catalyst electrode. There is convincing evidence that these ions (together with their compensating [screening] charge in the metal, thus forming surface dipoles) migrate (back-spillover) onto the gas-exposed catalyst electrode surface. ${ }^{13,17,110}$ Thus the solid electrolyte acts as an active catalyst support and establishes an effective electrochemical double layer on the gas-exposed, i.e., catalytically active, electrode surface.

The (average) work function of the gas-exposed catalyst electrode surface can be measured in situ, i.e., during reaction at atmospheric pressure and temperatures up to $300^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, by means of a Kelvin probe (vibrating condenser method) using, e.g., a Besocke Delta-Phi Kelvin probe with a Au vibrating disc as described in detail elsewhere. ${ }^{17,100}$

XPS is a useful tool for investigating metal electrode surfaces under conditions of electrochemical $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ pumping. The experimental setup is shown schematically in Figure 13.10b. A 2 -mm-thick YSZ slab ( $10 \times 13 \mathrm{~mm}$ ) with a Pt catalyst electrode film, a Pt reference electrode, and a Ag counter electrode were mounted on a resistively heated Mo holder in an UHV chamber (base pressure $\left.5 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{Torr}\right)$ and the catalyst-electrode film ( $9 \times 9 \mathrm{~mm}$ ) was examined at temperatures 25 to $525^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ by XPS using a Leybold HS-12 analyzer operated at constant ýE mode with $100-\mathrm{eV}$ pass energy and a sampling area of $5 \times 3 \mathrm{~mm}$. Electron binding energies were referenced to the metallic $\mathrm{Pt} 4 \mathrm{f}_{7 / 2}$ peak of the grounded catalyst electrode at 71.1 eV , which always remained unchanged with no trace of an oxidic component. Further experimental details are given elsewhere. ${ }^{110}$

## 3. Catalytic Rate Modification

Figure 13.11 shows a typical NEMCA experiment carried out in the setup depicted in Figure 13.10a. The catalytic reaction under study is the complete oxidation of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ on Pt : ${ }^{16}$

$$
\mathrm{CH}_{2}=\mathrm{CH}_{2}+3 \mathrm{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}
$$

The figure shows a typical galvanostatic transient, i.e., it depicts the transient effect of a constant applied current on the rate of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ oxidation (expressed in g-atom $\mathrm{O} / \mathrm{s}$ ).

The Pt catalyst film with a surface area corresponding to N=4.2•10-9 g-atom Pt, as measured by surface titration techniques, ${ }^{13}$ is deposited on YSZ and is exposed to $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}= 4.6 \mathrm{kPa}, \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}}=0.36 \mathrm{kPa}$ in the CSTR-type flow reactor depicted schematically in Figure 13.10a. Initially ( $\mathrm{t}<0$ ), the circuit is open ( $\mathrm{I}=0$ ) and the open-circuit catalytic rate $\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{o}}$ is $1.5 \cdot 10^{-8} \mathrm{~g}$-atom $\mathrm{O} / \mathrm{s}$. The corresponding turnover frequency (TOF), i.e., the number of oxygen atoms reacting per site per second, is $3.57 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$.

Then at $\mathrm{t}=0$ a galvanostat is used to apply a constant current of $+1 \mu \mathrm{~A}$ between the catalyst and the counter electrode (Figure 13.10a). Now oxygen ions $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ are supplied to the catalyst/gas/solid electrolyte tpb at a rate $\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{O}}=\mathrm{I} / 2 \mathrm{~F}=5.2 \cdot 10^{-12} \mathrm{~g}$-atom $\mathrm{O} / \mathrm{s}$. The catalytic rate starts increasing (Figure 13.11) and within 25 min gradually reaches a value $\mathrm{r}= 40 \cdot 10^{-8} \mathrm{~g}$-atom $\mathrm{O} / \mathrm{s}$, which is 26 times larger than $\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{o}}$. The new TOF is $95.2 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The increase in catalytic rate ýr $=\mathrm{r}-\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{o}}=38.5 \cdot 10^{-8} \mathrm{~g}$-atom $\mathrm{O} / \mathrm{s}$ is 74,000 times larger than $\mathrm{I} / 2 \mathrm{~F}$. This means that each $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ supplied to the Pt catalyst causes at steady-state 74,000 additional chemisorbed oxygen atoms to react with $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ to form $\mathrm{CO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$. This is why this novel effect has been termed the non-faradaic Electrochemical Modification of Catalytic Activity (NEMCA).

There is an important observation to be made regarding the time required for the rate to approach its steady-state value. Since catalytic rate transients obtained during galvanostatic

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-464.jpg?height=865&width=1277&top_left_y=94&top_left_x=141)
FIGURE 13.11. Rate and catalyst potential response to step changes in applied current during $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ oxidation on $\mathrm{Pt} ; \mathrm{T}=370^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}=4.6 \mathrm{kPa}, \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}}=0.36 \mathrm{kPa}$. The steady- state rate increase ýr is 74,000 times higher than the steady-state rate of supply of $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ to the catalyst $(\Lambda=74,000)$. (From Bebelis, S. and Vayenas, C.G., J. Catal., 1989, 118, 125. With permission.)

(i.e., constant current) operation are found in NEMCA studies to be usually, but not always, ${ }^{13}$ of the type:

$$
\Delta \mathrm{r}=\Delta \mathrm{r}_{\max }[1-\exp (-\mathrm{t} / \tau)]
$$

i.e., similar to the response of a first-order system with a characteristic time constant $\tau$, one can define the NEMCA time constant $\tau$ as the time required for ýr to reach $63 \%$ of its maximum, i.e., steady-state value. As shown in Figure 13.11, $\tau$ is of the order of $2 \mathrm{FN} / \mathrm{I}$, and this turns out to be a general observation in NEMCA studies utilizing doped $\mathrm{ZrO}_{2}$, i.e.:

$$
\tau \approx 2 \mathrm{FN} / \mathrm{I}
$$

This observation shows that NEMCA is a catalytic effect, i.e., it takes place over the entire gas-exposed catalyst surface, and is not an electrocatalytic effect localized at the tpb metal/solid electrolyte/gas. This is because $2 \mathrm{FN} / \mathrm{I}$ is the time required to form a monolayer of an oxygen species on a surface with N sites when it is supplied at a rate $\mathrm{I} / 2 \mathrm{~F}$. The fact that $\tau$ is found to be smaller than $2 \mathrm{FN} / \mathrm{I}$, but of the same order of magnitude, shows that only a fraction of the surface is occupied by oxygen back-spillover species, as discussed in detail elsewhere. ${ }^{13}$ It is worth noting that if NEMCA were restricted to the tpb, i.e., if the observed rate increase were due to an electrocatalytic reaction, then $\tau$ would be practically zero during galvanostatic transients.

As shown in Figure 13.11, NEMCA is reversible, i.e., upon current interruption the catalytic rate returns to its initial value within roughly 100 min . The rate relaxation curve upon current interruption conveys valuable information about the kinetics of reaction and

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-465.jpg?height=935&width=728&top_left_y=94&top_left_x=414)
FIGURE 13.12. Effect of gaseous composition on the regular (open-circuit) steady-state rate of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ oxidation on Pt and on NEMCA-induced catalytic rate when the catalyst film is maintained at $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}=1 \mathrm{~V}$. (From Bebelis, S . and Vayenas, C.G., J. Catal., 1989, 118, 125. With permission.)

desorption of the promoting oxygen species as discussed in detail elsewhere. ${ }^{108,109}$ Negative current application has practically no effect on the rate of this particular reaction.

## 4. Effect of Gaseous Composition on Regular (Open-Circuit) and NEMCA-Induced Reaction Rate

Figure 13.12 shows the effect of $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ to $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ ratio on the regular (open-circuit) steadystate rate of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ oxidation on Pt and on the NEMCA-induced rate when the same catalyst film is maintained at a potential of $+1 \mathrm{~V}\left(\mathrm{~V}_{\mathrm{WR}}=+1 \mathrm{~V}\right)$ with respect to the reference $\mathrm{Pt} /$ air electrode (Figure 13.10a). It can be seen that the effect is much more pronounced at high $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}} / \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}}$ ratios, i.e., for high oxygen coverages, where the NEMCA-induced reaction rate or TOF values are a factor of 55 higher than the corresponding open-circuit rate. A quantitative description and explanation of the NEMCA behavior of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ oxidation on Pt can be found elsewhere. ${ }^{13,16}$

Figure 13.13 shows the effect of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ partial pressure at constant $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{O}_{2}}$ on the rate of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ oxidation on Rh at various imposed values of $\mathrm{V}_{\text {WR }}$. Increasing $\mathrm{V}_{\text {WR }}$ causes up to 90 -fold ( $9000 \%$ ) rate enhancement relative to the open-circuit rate value. The dramatic rate enhancement with increasing $\mathrm{V}_{\text {WR }}$ depicted in Figures 13.12 and 13.13 is due to the weakening of the metal-covalently chemisorbed oxygen chemisorptive bond, cleavage of which is rate limiting, as discussed in detail elsewhere. ${ }^{13,16,108}$

## 5. Definitions and the Role of the Exchange Current $\mathbf{I}_{\mathbf{0}}$

Table 13.4 provides a list of the reactions which have been studied already and shown to exhibit NEMCA. In order to compare different catalytic reactions, it is useful to define two dimensionless parameters, i.e., the enhancement factor or faradaic efficiency $\Lambda$ and the rate enhancement ratio $\rho .^{13}$ The former is defined as:

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/2025_10_16_72ceac0a8fd0baa16477g-466.jpg?height=777&width=844&top_left_y=83&top_left_x=356)
FIGURE 13.13. Effect of gaseous composition and catalyst potential $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}$ on the steady-state rate of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ oxidation on Rh. (From Vayenas, C.G., Ladas, S., Bebelis, S., Yentekakis, I.V., Neophytides, S., Jiang, Y., Karavasilis, Ch., and Pliangos, C., Electrochim. Acta, 1994, 39, 1849. With permission.)

$$
\Lambda=\Delta \mathrm{r} /(\mathrm{I} / 2 \mathrm{~F})
$$

where the change in catalytic rate ýr is expressed in terms of g-atom of O . More generally, $\Lambda$ is computed by expressing ýr in g-equivalent and dividing by $\mathrm{I} / \mathrm{F}$.

A catalytic reaction is said to exhibit NEMCA when $* \Lambda^{*}>1$. When $\Lambda>1$ as, e.g., in the case of $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ oxidation on Pt , the reaction is said to exhibit positive or electrophobic NEMCA behavior. When $\Lambda<-1$, then the reaction is said to exhibit electrophilic behavior.

The rate enhancement ratio $\rho$ is defined from:

$$
\rho=r / r_{o}
$$

In the $\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{4}$ oxidation example presented in Figure 13.11 and discussed above the $\Lambda$ and $\rho$ values at steady state are $\Lambda=74,000$ and $\rho=26$.

As it turns out experimentally (Figure 13.14) and can be explained theoretically, ${ }^{13}$ one can estimate or predict the order of magnitude of the absolute value of the enhancement factor $\Lambda$ for any given reaction, catalyst, and catalyst/solid electrolyte interface from:

$$
|\Lambda| \approx 2 \mathrm{Fr}_{\mathrm{o}} / \mathrm{I}_{0}
$$

where $\mathrm{I}_{0}$ is the exchange current of the metal/solid electrolyte interface.
As noted in Section I.D, the parameter $\mathrm{I}_{0}$ can be easily determined from standard $\ln \mathrm{I}$ vs. $\eta$ (Tafel) plots. ${ }^{13,16}$ The overpotential $\eta$ of the catalyst electrode is defined, according to Section I.D, from:

$$
\eta=\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{WR}}^{\mathrm{o}}
$$

I. Positive (electrophobic) NEMCA effect (ýr $>0$ with $\mathrm{I}>0^{*}, \Lambda>0$, ýe $\Phi>0$ )
